description:
Dungeon Twister: Traps, the first expansion for Dungeon Twister / Dungeon Twister: Prison, comes packaged in four blister packs with storage space for the components available in the DT: Prison box. The blister packs will be released roughly once a quarter.

Each blister pack of Dungeon Twister: Traps includes a pair of new rooms, four new figures (two per side) of two new characters, new items, and assorted markers for the characters and rooms.

This game pits players against each other in a country music star setting. The objective of the game is to make it all around the board and arrive in Music City with the most money. Players roll the dice to get to destinations or complete objectives like getting a hit record where each player will either be rewarded or have to pay. "Stars on tour"

boardgame: DIG: the Card Game – Black Diamond Promo Card
description:
Black Diamond is a promo card for DIG: the Card Game that can be added to the deck for more game play variety.  Simply shuffle the card into the starting deck and play.  The pertinent rules are included on the card itself.

Card Text: 
Always sells for 6 Gold Coins; do not shuffle back into the Mine Deck.

Note:  The Black Diamond is a once-per-game find, adding additional excitement and fun every time your Dwarves go digging.

boardgame: DIG: the Card Game – Dwarf Thief Promo Card
description:
Dwarf Thief is a promo card for DIG: the Card Game that can be added to the deck for more game play variety.  Simply shuffle the card into the starting deck and play.  The pertinent rules are included on the card itself.

Card Text: 
Always costs 6 Gold Coins. Play in your Play Area. Steal 2 Gold Coins from any 1 Player during each Dig Phase.

Note:  The Dwarf Thief does not dig or mine; instead, simply play him face-up in your play area as soon as you hire him. From then on, during each Dig Phase, you may steal 2 Gold Coins from any 1 player. So, for example, if you hire the Dwarf Thief on Day 2, you can feasibly net 10 Gold Coins (2 Gold Coins per day on Days 2-6).

boardgame: DIG: the Card Game – Luck Mitigation Die
description:
The Luck Mitigation Die is meant to add varying levels of fun and chance to DIG: the Card Game.  It has three blank faces and three faces with icons that correspond to three types of cards in the game: Tunnel, Cave-In, and Gemstone.

Simple and Fun Rules

     Each time you draw a Cave-In Card, roll the Luck Mitigation Die:



         If you roll a blank, nothing happens. Continue drawing, if appropriate.
         If you roll the Cave-In Icon, activate the Cave-In Card. Stop drawing cards for that Tunnel.
         If you roll the Tunnel Icon, play a free, next-level Tunnel. Continue drawing cards, if appropriate.
         If you roll the Gemstone Icon, draw one extra card.
    


If you encounter another Cave-In during any successive card draws, follow the steps above again.

Advanced Strategic Rules


     Before you draw a card during the Mine Phase, you may choose to pay for use of the Luck Mitigation Die as follows: Apprentice Dwarves: 1 Gold, Journeymen Dwarves: 2 Gold, and Master Dwarves: 3 Gold. You may make this payment (to the bank) with only one dwarf per Day.
     Draw cards from the Mine deck, as appropriate, one card at a time. If you draw a Cave-In Card, roll the Luck Mitigation Die.



         If you roll a blank, nothing happens. Continue drawing, if appropriate.
         If you roll the Cave-In Icon, activate the Cave-In Card. Stop drawing cards for this Tunnel.
         If you roll the Tunnel Icon, play a free, next-level Tunnel. Continue drawing cards, if appropriate.
         If you roll the Gemstone Icon, draw one extra card.
    


If you encounter another Cave-In during any successive card draws, activate the Cave-In as normal--do not roll the die again.

boardgame: DIG: the Card Game – The Flood
description:
The Flood for DIG: the Card Game is a 10-card mini-expansion that includes:

     Flood Cards
     Pump Cards
     Raft Cards


Shuffle the Flood Cards into the Mine deck.
Shuttle the Pump  and Raft into the Tools deck.

If a Flood card is drawn when mining, that Tunnel is now flooded.  A Pump card is needed to clear it.

Leap Frog! is a game for 2-4 players. In Leap Frog! each player takes control of a frog in a pond filled with lily pads of varying heights. Players then hop their frogs between lily pads in an attempt to eat a variety of different flies. At the end of the game, each player counts the point value of the flies his or her frog has eaten. The frog that has had the tastiest meal (scored the most points) is the winner.

The movement/hopping mechanic in Leap Frog! uses a small deck of cards, containing distance values between 1 and 4. In order to hop to a lily pad, a player must play a card from his hand equal to the distance of the jump. Leap Frog uses a simple, yet novel, system to determine jumping distance, which takes into account both the number of spaces and the height a frog must jump.

Each player rolls the dice and moves on the course to finish in the home of an animal or the house of all animals.

From the house of all animals, the player chooses the food for the animal of their choice.

When a player arrives at the home of an opponent's animal which has all types of food collected, he can steal chosen food..

Whoever has the most food at the end wins the game.

boardgame: Call of Cthulhu: The Card Game – Ebla Restored Asylum Pack
description:
Game description from the publisher:

Knowledge stolen from the ruins of an ancient city now steers the fate of the world.

Ebla Restored, the third Asylum Pack from the upcoming "Revelations" cycle for Call of Cthulhu: The Card Game, adds to the cycle's focus on the power and dangers of eldritch knowledge. Its new cards promote exciting new synergies between dark Tomes and the daring Relic Hunters who seek them. Meanwhile, players gain unprecedented control over their decks and discard piles, finding new ways to work with lost knowledge.

Will the forgotten secrets of ancient Ebla provide you the answers you need to stymie your foes? Will humanity tremble before the terrifying might of the Ancient Ones restored to their former glory, or will heroic investigators and scientists thwart them and their zealous cultists to maintain sanity, at least for another day?

Ebla Restored introduces sixty new cards (three copies each of twenty cards) that provide players with new incentives to hunt down ancient Tomes, visit remote Libraries, and prevent rival Relic Hunters from fetching powerful artifacts for their respective factions.
family: Collectible: Living Card Game (Fantasy Flight Games)
Game: Call of Cthulhu – The Card Game (Fantasy Flight Games)

description:
Game description from the publisher:

Sparks are gonna fly in this lightning-fast card game! In Zip Zap, you want to quickly rid your hand of cards by playing numbers in order. Lay down a run and consider yourself in the zone, but don't get caught ZZZ-ing or else – Zap! – another player will steal the lead! Be the first one out of cards and zero in on the win!

In Mäuse verstecken ("Hiding Mice"), the players take turns hiding two (foam) mice somewhere on their body: in their fist, their knee, elbow, armpit or between their head and shoulder.

The other players have to guess where the mice are hidden. Each player may make two guesses. After all guesses have been made, the active player reveals where the mice are located, and points are scored. A player earns one point for each correct guess. If the location of a mouse has not been guessed by anyone, the player who hid the mouse scores one point. These points are recorded on the scoring track.

After each player has taken a turn hiding the mice, the player who is furthest ahead on the scoring track wins.

In Streck dich, Mieze!, the players play cats who are fishing in the river.

At the start of the game, the river is created by marking the riverbanks with two cords. Cards depicting fish worth 1-3 points are scattered in this river.

Now the players take turn fishing. At the start of a player's turn, a cat card is drawn, which shows the position the player must take at the riverbank, and what body part(s) he may use to get a fish (left hand, right hand, left foot, right foot, nose).

When he has taken up position, the player tries to move one of the fish cards to the riverbank. If he succeeds in doing so, he gets to keep the card. If he falls over or uses the wrong body part for fishing, his turn ends without gaining a fish.

After each player has made five fishing attempts, the game ends, and the player with the highest total value of fishes caught wins the game.

Paper-and-pencil game where from 2 to 4 players must score the most possible points by filling spaces of an 8x8 board; whole rows and columns add extra points.

Each player throws the die in their turn and ticks with their own symbol (dot, circle, square or cross) as many consecutive spaces on one row or column of the board as pips they've thrown; any free space on the whole board can be chosen to start this segment, but no space on the selected segment can be already marked by the player (different symbols can coincide on the same square, though). Dice throws that can't be used are forfeited.

When every space on the board has at least one symbol the game ends; every symbol scores 1 point and every whole row or column scores extra 5 points.

boardgame: Ascension: Storm of Souls – Askara of Fortune Promo
description:
Askara of Fortune is a promo card given to those that preorder the new official deck box and sleeves with Storm of Souls artwork.

From the Card:

Askara of Fortune
Cost: 3 Runes to aquire
Enlightened Hero
Each player reveals the top card of their deck.  Choose a Hero revealed this way and copy it's effect.

In Rigamaroles, players compete over a series of rounds to be the last two players standing. In each round, role chits will be placed in the center of the room. When given the "GO" by the moderator, players race, and grab one chit. That chit determines the role that player will act out. There will always be one other player in the group who is acting out the same role. Each player must find his/her match quickly, link arms, and sit down on the outside of the room to be safe for the next round. In each round, the last two players standing are ELIMINATED! Play continues as such until there are only two players left and those two players are crowned the Rolemasters for that game! Play several games for a full night of laughs!

The game plays quickly. Averaging about one minute per number of players! You can play it with as many players as you wish. In fact, the more the merrier! Unlike other player-elimination games, Rigmaroles is such a blast to watch, and the rounds go so quickly, that nobody minds being eliminated.

Although players must know how to read to play, there is a variant that allows even non-reading children to join in on the fun. So players of all ages will enjoy Rigamaroles!

Game description from the publisher:

Grisbi is a very dynamic and entertaining game about encounters between cops and robbers. Each player's goal is to act quickly and – by matching the right pictures – collect all the location cards. Grisbi is not a turn-based game, so being swift is your best weapon. Try not to make any mistakes while matching pictures!

The Go Hunt card game is packed with tons of fun and laughs and is perfect for most any party.  It will be hard to keep your mind out of the gutter while playing or asking for any one of the 16 different hilarious images on the Go Hunt cards.

Goal: To collect the most matching sets, either sets of 2 or 4. You decide on game play.  Matching sets 2 will make for a quicker game, sets of  4 will extend game play.

There are 16 differant card images to play and match: Bald Beaver, Big Cock, Big Honkers, Cuddly Puss, Cute Ass, Dry Puss, Excited Monkey, Fat Beaver, Little Hooters, Fat Weiner, Little Monkey, Nice Rack, Red Pecker, Stretched Puss, Small Cock, Wet Puss.  The images are all innocent pictures of animals, check them out in our picture gallery! No animals were hurt in the making of the Go Hunt card game.

Game play: With four or less players; seven cards are dealt to each player.  Five cards can be dealt to each player in larger groups.  The remaining cards are placed face down in a draw pile.  The player left of the dealer goes first.  On your turn, ask any player for a specific card.  For example: "Michele do you have little hooters?"  You must already hold one of the requested cards.  If Michele has any little hooter cards she must give them all to you.  If the person you ask does not have the card you requested, they would say "Go Hunt!"  You would then draw a card from the draw pile.  When the draw pile runs out, keep playing until all the cards have been made into matching sets.  When you collect a set of matching cards, immediately show the other players and place the cards face down in front of you.

Is is fair to lump all pair matching games together? Yes it has the same type of play as go fish, but the card images really make the game stand out. Playing basketball, and playing a game of HORSE is differant, for example in the game Go Hunt there is a card with an image of a fat beaver eating an apple, the card is called "FAT BEAVER", now imagine this card and 15 others in play, would you ask your grandma if she had a "FAT BEAVER", "WET PUSS" or even a "LARGE COCK".

In conclusion if you have an inoccent soul and a naughty mind you"ll have a blast playing the Go Hunt card game with your friends!

There are also variants of the Go Hunt game that are drinking games, so if your over 21 give them a try!

Chivalry is the forerunner of Camelot.  Under development by George S. Parker in 1882, Chivalry was released by Geo. S. Parker & Co. in 1887, and by Parker Brothers in 1888.  Parker called Chivalry "the best game in 2,000 years," and looked upon it as his personal favorite.  Chivalry is played on a larger board with more pieces than Camelot.  Chivalry, like Camelot, is tactically complex, yet easily learned and quickly played.  It is a clever mixture of American Checkers (British Draughts) and Halma (Chinese Checkers).

The goals of the game is to move two pieces into the two goal spaces on the opposite side of the board. Of course, one has to get past the opponent's piece to do so. Movement of pieces in many ways resembles Checkers except that jumping and moving is omnidirectional.

This game tends to be dominated by key sacrifices and charges for the goal. Since a player has to get two pieces into the goal, the dynamic shifts from just trying to get a single piece into a goal. In other words, players won't make one sudden leap to victory; at least a 1-2 punch is required.

For those familiar with Camelot:

Chivalry is different than Camelot in the following ways:

     The Chivalry board has 176 squares.  The Camelot board has 160 squares.  
     Each side in Chivalry has eight Knights and twelve Men.  Each side in Camelot has four Knights and ten Men.

This four-handed pure abstract has both partnership and individual versions. The object is to capture all opponents' pieces.

Pieces jump like in checkers but in all directions and can jump (without capturing) friendly pieces before capturing any opponents' pieces. So the jumps can become very complex.

Uusually a clear vitor will emerge before the final pieces are atually taken-- but not always.

For those familiar with Chivalry (1888) or Camelot (1930):

Four-Handed Chivalry uses the standard Chivalry board, but is very different in several ways from the standard Chivalry game, as well as from Camelot or any Camelot variant:

1. Unlike all other Camelot variants, only Knights are used.

2. Unlike all other Camelot variants, the solitary object of the game is to capture all opposing Knights; moving Knights into a Castle has no significance.

3. Unlike Grand Camelot, in the partnership version of Four-Handed Chivalry, partners sit opposite each other, and move rotation passes clockwise around the table.

This game is to Camelot what 4-player chess is to chess. Of course, the set-up, board and gameplay are markedly different than Camelot.

The game is played in partnerships with partners sitting next to each other. The goal is for one set of partners to get any two pieces to te opponents' goal spaces-- either each partner getting one piece into one of the two the goal spaces opposite him or one partner getting two pieces into those spaces.

Each player has two types of pieces which mostly move similarly. Draughts-like jumps occur in all directions, or one can jump one's own pieces without capturing. Each player focuses on trying to get his pieces past the player opposite him BUT defense also has to protect the opponent from pieces moving laterally.

Note that Grand Camelot is distinctly different from the other 4-player games in the Series: Camelot (Abstract games) family, Four-handed Chivalry and Grand Cam.

Cam is a small but not tiny game of sudden death. This little abstract ends as soon as one player gets any one piece to the single goal space at the opposite end of the board. To do so, one has to get past the opponent's pieces.

Each player has two types of pieces which mostly move similarly. One can jump one's own pieces or jump to capture an opponent's. Multiple jumps in any direction are allowed. So, sudden reversals and leaps to victory are not uncommon. Since only one piece in the goal is needed to win, this game can be unforgiving. Still it's quick and many games can be played in one sitting.

Cam, released by Parker Brothers in 1949, is a down-sized version of Camelot.

Cam is different than Camelot in the following ways:

    The Cam board has 67 squares.  The Camelot board has 160 squares.
    Each side in Cam has two Knights and five Men.  Each side in Camelot has four Knights and ten Men.  
    The object of Cam is to get one piece into the opponent's Castle.  The object of Camelot is to get two pieces into the opponent's Castle.  
    Victory in Cam also occurs if one side, even with only one remaining piece, captures all of the opposing pieces.  In Camelot, if the side capturing all of the opposing pieces has only one remaining piece, the game is a draw.  Victory in Camelot by capture of all of the opposing pieces occurs only if the victorious side has two or more remaining pieces.    
    Stalemate in Cam is impossible.  Stalemate in Camelot is possible, and is a victory for the stalemating side if it has two or more pieces.  
    In Cam, if both sides have only one remaining piece, the game continues until one piece captures the other, or one piece enters the opposing Castle.  In Camelot, if both sides have only one remaining piece, the game is a draw.


Re-implements:

    Chivalry
    Camelot

Camette is a pure two-player abstract played on a very small board. Hence it plays very fast, especially since the object is to get any one piece onto an opponent's goal space-- which  that player canot occupy himself. As in all games of this family, there are two different types of pieces which mostly move similarly. Pieces can jump over friendly pieces and jump over enemy pieces to capture them. With a board this small, sudden leaps to victory are common.

For those familiar with Camelot (1888):

Camette is a down-sized version of Camelot, even smaller than Cam, with a further reduced board size and number of pieces.  Camette was invented by Michael Wortley Nolan in 2002.

Camette is different than Camelot in the following ways:

1. The Camette board has 23 squares.  The Camelot board has 160 squares.

2. Each side in Camette has one Knight and three Men.  Each side in Camelot has four Knights and ten Men.

3. The object of Camette is to get one piece into the opponent's Castle.  The object of Camelot is to get two pieces into the opponent's Castle.

4. Victory in Camette also occurs if one side, even with only one remaining piece, captures all of the opposing pieces.  In Camelot, if the side capturing all of the opposing pieces has only one remaining piece, the game is a draw.  Victory in Camelot by capture of all of the opposing pieces occurs only if the victorious side has two or more remaining pieces.

5. Stalemate in Camette is impossible.  Stalemate in Camelot is possible, and is a victory for the stalemating side if it has two or more pieces.

6. In Camette, if both sides have only one remaining piece, the game continues until one piece captures the other, or one piece enters the opposing Castle.  In Camelot, if both sides have only one remaining piece, the game is a draw.

Grand Cam is a four-player analog of the game Cam. This is a partnership pure abstract game. Partners sit next to each other. To win, one player has to move a piece into the goal opposite his pieces' starting position or all the losing partnership's pieces must be captured.

This has the marked differences in play between Camelot and Cam combined with two pairs of immediate opponents whose pieces generally move in directions orthogonal to each other.

The design of the Grand Cam piece setup and the design and construction of the first Grand Cam game board in 2005 owe themselves to WCF member Paul Bramwell Smith, who is deservedly acknowledged as the game's inventor.

Grand Cam is different than Grand Camelot in the following ways:

1. The Grand Cam board has 93 squares.  The Grand Camelot board has 180 squares.

2. Each side in Grand Cam has two Knights and five Men.  Each side in Grand Camelot has four Knights and eight Men.

Ostesibly a 3-handed analog of Camelot, this game bares some resemblance to Cam in that it has a single goal each player must protect. Additionally the board is markedly different than any game in the Camelot family and uses hexagons.

The game is a three-handed pure abstract. Each player protects a single goal space from both opponents. In spite of the name, this game is a three-handed version of Cam, not Camelot in that the first first to enter a goal wins and so ends the game. Often this means that a player can block one but not both opponents. Hence these games can end suddenly and kingmaking can be involved.

Rats, bats, snakes!  While exploring the Catacomb the heroes encounter hordes of them crawling and flying everywhere!  Horde of Vermin is a new expansion for the award winning dexterity game Catacombs.  In the base game, the heroes valiantly fight monsters from the dungeon, mythological, infernal and undead family.  This expansion introduces the vermin family:  seven new monsters from hordes of Rats to the horrendous Remorhaz who crawl from the sewers to challenge the heroes.  Do not be deceived by the diminutive size of these monsters, with their ability to instill fear and inflict poison attacks on even the most battle hardened warriors.  Left untreated, poisoned heroes can meet a swift end.  Fortunately, the heroes can retaliate with the lethal Vermin Blade.  Furthermore, the Wizard gets new spells to cure poison and the ability to cast the powerful Giant Fireball.

Also included are new rules and enhanced cards for owners of the Cavern of Soloth expansion.

Released March, 2012
A 2-player abstract game created for the Shibumi Challenge. Players attempt to connect opposite sides of the board with their color, but doing so clears all of your marbles out of the spillway!

The Lovers is the first expansion set for the Arcana of Love card game. It provides additional "spicy" cards for the erotic game for couples.

Components:

     24 cards
         1 ARCANA card
         4 ASSET cards
         3 DARE cards
         3 SPECIAL ACTION cards
         13 ACTION card
    


There was one version in a bigger box with additional accessories:

     The Arcana of Love Cuffs
     The Arcana of Love Scarf
     Vibrator "Lady Finger"
     2 Erotic Perfume Candles
     1 Red Feather

Attractive trivia game, centred around the players' knowledge of animals within the regions of France. The six areas deemed to have these animals resident in them, are Vanoise, Corse, Pyrénées, Camargue, Armorique and Lorraine.

Each player receives animal tiles which act like jigsaw puzzle pieces i.e. they fit onto the board thus filling the map-board of France. To place a piece you must answer a question correctly. The questions, of course, are related to the subject of the game. First to place all their pieces, wins.

Educational game about identifying road signs and observing road safety.

The track is ingeniously designed, going over and under itself at key points. Movement is by dice. At certain points on the track you must answer a question. The answer is hidden from you but if you place a transparent device over it, it becomes visible. You must get a question right to proceed. The first player to reach the end of the track, wins

Educational game about the geography of France. It has a board showing all the separate regions of the country, but it is really a card game,

Three 'Contracts' are revealed. The contract might say "Regions that begin with the same letter", that sort of thing. Each player has 10 cards and can claim a contract if you reveal the cards asked for. Each contract is worth points - easy to do might be 3 points, more difficult 6 points, very difficult 10 points. When a contract is taken another one replaces it.

The first player to obtain 10 contracts scores the points on each contract, plus two points for each contract that another player has less then ten, plus 20 points for going out first. The player with the highest score, wins.

A party game where teams take turns trying to guess nouns or phrases being described in increasingly limited fashion by a teammate.

At the beginning of the game each player writes five nouns or phrases on a slip of paper, which are all then shuffled into a bag.  Players are divided into two teams, and one team is chosen to start.  It is recommended the starting team alternate between rounds to reduce the starting advantage.

The first round is timed for 1 minute.  The active team chooses someone to draw nouns from the bag, while the rest of the team tries to guess the noun.  During this round the active player may say anything they like, as long as they do not say the words on the paper.  Each correct guess is worth a point.  Then, with all the drawn papers left out of the bag, the second team does the same.

The second round is timed for 2 minutes, and is played as the first round, except instead of speaking the active player may only perform charades.

The third round is timed for 1 minute, and is identical to the previous two except now the active player may only say one word.

Objective: find as many of wanted nations/capitals as possible

- acting by turn/simple 'draw & place' geographic game -

Flip tiles upside down
Draw a tile (flag/capital) and find the corresponding place on the map(s)
6 different maps Africa, Asia, Australia and Oceania, Europe, North America & Central America, South America.
Remarks: 
Version held by National Library of Australia dated 1890s. 
The individual maps were subsequently updated, another version having maps showing borders as follows: South America ~ 1905, Africa ~ 1885, Europe ~ 1920.

Earth - Present Day
The day started off so well for the humans, but then the sky darkened as a higher intelligence showed its face. These aliens are here to have a little fun, it's a race-to abduct the most humans. Can the humans strike back, or will the aliens have their way?

The Human Race is a competitive alien abduction game, a cooperative military defensive game, or both!

Alien Abduction
You and your alien opponents must move your spaceship pawns around the hex-filled game board to abduct the specific human target as indicated by the deck of target cards. While a player is abducting a human, opponents may move on to the next target or try to steal the human abductee from the alien abductor by attacking them. But look out! The humans aren't going to take this sitting down, the military is after you too. There are also bonuses to help you along on your journey. The alien with the most points when all the humans have been abducted wins!

Military Defensive
You will control the Earth denizens in a last-ditch effort to keep your peeps safe! Blast the heck out of the alien menace and end their sick game once and for all. Move jets and tanks around the board in order to quell the situation while moving human targets to safety. Rob the aliens of their prizes or their spaceships and save the day!

Objective: be the first reaching target space
 - moving by turn - 
simple 'Roll & Move' game
roll the die - ending on a marked space draw a card and follow instructions
remarks
huge board: 49 x 71.5 cm (19.3" x 28.1")
cover shows 3 well known mountains: Eiger, Mönch, Jungfrau.

The Hunt: Carpathia, 1897 is a period specific tabletop adventure game, set in the dangerous monster-ridden land of Carpathia.

Players of The Hunt will take turns moving around the game board, fighting monsters, avoiding hazards, collecting bounties, and trying to kill one another - all while trying to find better weapons and gear.  Ultimately they want to take down one of the unique Prey monsters, each of whom grants a unique ability to their vanquisher.

Weapons, items, monsters and hazards are all represented on detailed cards with unique art relevant (we hope) to the time period.  Players roll dice (d8) to move and attack, using their own special abilities and items to their best advantage.  Each player is one of 6 unique characters, each with different strengths.

Death in The Hunt is by no means final - when a player loses all vitality points (players start with and have a max of 3) he/she is turned into a "cursed" player, remains on the board with new abilities, and is now turned against the remaining live players.  Nobody is ever "out of the hunt."

Players battle monsters and each other earning trophy points for each monster vanquished.  A player must collect a certain number of trophy points and make it back to the ship for the win!  If all players become cursed, the cursed player with the most trophy points wins.

boardgame: The Lord of the Rings: The Card Game – The Long Dark
description:
Game description from the publisher:

After a brutal conflict outside the gates of Moria, several of Middle-earth's heroes press forward, into the dark, Dwarven mines to scout the ancient tunnels for the source of the increased Orc activity in the Misty Mountains. The way is dark and choked with foes and hazards.

The Long Dark is the fourth Adventure Pack in the "Dwarrowdelf" cycle for The Lord of the Rings: The Card Game and adds 60 new cards including a new hero, three copies of each player card, and an all-new scenario that pits your heroes against bands of Goblins, the hazards of traveling in the dark, and the risk of getting lost. As your heroes travel through the Dark, Underground realm of Moria, they must navigate carefully or suffer the consequences of losing their way. Any wrong turn may result in disaster.

Note: A copy of The Lord of the Rings: The Card Game Core Set and the Khazad-dûm Expansion are required to play the scenario included in this Adventure Pack.

description:
This is the sequal to The Sopranos Trivia Game.

How well do you know Tony and the family? Yo, you betta know deez guys good if youz are gonna play. Now you and your friends can test your knowledge of the number one show on cable with The Sopranos Trivia Game. There are one thousand trivia questions that will challenge you on your knowledge of the program and "Mob Speak". Place bets on which questions you can answer correctly. Five levels of difficulty make it easy for even occasional viewers to play against dedicated fans of The Sopranos.

Game Includes: Vinyl Playing Surface 200 Trivia Cards Betting ChipsFor 2 or more adult players ages 18 and up.

The first dexterity game using the Shibumi set!

Players roll their marbles onto the board (using paper as a ramp) to score points and multipliers.

Who can Zing the pyramid?

(Note, it is recommended to play this only with the Ninja set. Game may damage marbles from the Samurai set.)
Sound/picture matching game for 1 or 2 players. Players pick picture tiles and must deduce the first letter of the matching word.  Supplied with non-competitive rules.

A monopoly (from Greek monos μόνος (alone or single) + polein πωλεῖν (to sell)) exists when a specific person or enterprise is the only supplier of a particular commodity.

Monopoly is a roll-and-move game where players move around the game board buying or trading properties, developing their properties with houses and hotels. When opponents land on players owned property, the owning player collects rent from the landing player. Owning sets of properties increases the rent paid to the player who owns them, by the player who lands on the square. The ultimate goal is to have the most money and drive opponents into bankruptcy.

Another in the very long line of MONOPOLY Games.
family: Versions & Editions: Versions with IP after the original release

description:
A Naval Game, British made with Prov Pat No 2649. "River Plate". produced by Victory Games during World War II. Features a large scaled down model of a ship along with some miniature ships in different colors used by the players as player pieces. Also is sort of a search board numbered from 1-100 where different spaces might produce attacks by the enemy.

From Ebayer daviddunne who I have contacted for more info about the game

description: By Tudor. Game features vibrating base turned on by electricity. Line the horses up and watch them race around the track to see who crosses the finish line first. Bet on who wins the race.
By GABRIEL from the 1950's. A game featuring spinners and 6 plastic men. Spin the spinner to see what happens in the game

Paper-and-pencil game where from 2 to 4 players must score the most possible points by filling the 16 central spaces of an 8x8 board.

Each player starts from a corner of the board and marks with their own symbol (dot, circle, square or cross) a square two or three spaces away in a straight vertical or horizontal line from the square previously marked; a square can be marked by all players. The distance from one marked square to the next has got to be two squares on one turn, three in the next one, then two squares again, then three and so on (the player can choose which value goes first). A player can't land on a square already marked with their symbol.

The game ends when the 16 central squares of the board have been ticked. Players score for the central squares they have ticked: each square brings as many points as different symbols have been marked on the square.

Munch is a game about giant space monsters breaking into our dimension to eat worlds. Monsters also munch each other when they meet while floating through space.

Five Rainbow stashes of Looney Pyramids are required for two players to play. An additional Xeno stash allows up to four players. The game is played on a 6x6 board.

Players take turns placing worlds on the board. At any point players can unleash one of their monsters from their army to the board. Monsters continue to move on the board until they run out of worlds to munch or until they get munched themselves.

The goal of the game is to collect the most worlds, with single-colored trios scoring highest. The player with the highest score from trios and single worlds at the end of the game wins.

Spack is a two-player game for the Shibumi set.

The board is initially empty. Each player ownes 24 balls (8 white, 8 black, 8 red)

Players take turns adding one of their balls. 
Under certain conditions, they can return balls to their supply too.

The game ends, as soon as a player can´t make a legal move.

The winner is determined by the number of balls, the players have left.

Spack is an entry in the Shibumi Challenge.

Old cardgame published in Sweden probably in the 40s or 50s.

You try to complete pair of two cards by taking one card from your neighbour. If you get a pair you put down the cards infront of you. The first one who get rids of all the cards win.

The gift machine has broken down and has to be repaired! This is done by connecting the machine parts with a line of dominoes (standing up, so that they can be toppled, not lying down).

At the start of Tims Tüftel-Team, several cardboard tokens representing the machine parts are scattered along the table. The game is played in rounds; in each round, two tokens have to be connected. At the start of the first round, one token is selected as the starting token, and this also dictates which token will be the destination token. In later rounds, the destination token from the previous round becomes the starting token.

Now, the players take turns placing dominoes in the line from the start token to the destination token, building past any other tokens. Each turn, a player rolls a die, which allows him to add zero to three dominoes to the line. When the destination token is reached, the round ends. The player whose turn it is gets to topple the dominoes by pushing the first domino in the line. First, though, the other players have to predict whether or not the entire line of dominoes will fall.

After predictions have been made, the first domino is toppled. If the entire line falls, then the toppling player receives a gift token, as well as any player who made the right prediction; if some dominoes remain standing, the players who predicted that receive a gift token.

After six rounds, the game ends, and the player who has gained the most gift tokens wins.

In the expert version of the game, various contraptions for connecting two lines of dominoes are included, and in each round, one of these is included in the line of dominoes connecting the start and destination.

A trivia game about Finnish rock music from the 70's to 00's. The game includes a four-piece game board, 1000 questions, 30 pictures and a cd. The cd contains music samples. The publisher is a Finnish record company.

Players answer a question from their space and can advance if they answer correctly. A maximum of 2 advancements in one turn.

boardgame: The NetHack Board Game: Gnomish Mines Expansion
description:
The Gnomish Mines expansion for the NetHack Board Game adds a new area, new encounters, and new play strategies to the base game.

Descend into the Gnomish Mines and return with gemstones that can be used throughout the Dungeons for an extra measure of luck, to bribe monsters, or to bid for items found by your opponent - unless they turn out to be worthless pieces of glass!

Note that this is an expansion, and requires the NetHack Board Game (v2.0) in order to play.

Paper-and-pencil game where from 2 to 4 players must move on an 8x8 board as Chess knights - used spaces will be unavailable for the rest of the game. The last player who's able to move wins. It's more or less an adaptation of Alex Randolph's Knight Chase for several players.

Each player has got an own symbol (dot, circle, square, cross) and starts from one corner on the board; before anyone moves each player will fill in a square on the board, which will be unavailable from then on (it's not allowed to block a player before their first move). Players then move in turn following the rules for a Chess knight, ticking the corresponding free space on the board with their symbol and filling in the square they departed from. If a player can't move the turn will be forfeited. The last player who can move wins the game.

At Neuve Chapelle provides a tactical re-play of the World War I battle. The British attempted to reduce a poorly-defended German salient centered on the town of Neuve Chapelle. Plans were to expand a breakthrough and with cavalry and infantry reserves take the Aubers ridge line. However, poor communication and coordination and the weather conspired to slow the initial British successes and allowed the German army to rush reinforcements into the area. Among other game features, designer Michael W. Kennedy uses offensive artillery and defensive artillery fire, British command checks, and a unique assault combat mechanic to simulate the bloody struggle. (Sourced from the White Dog Games website.)

The game is played over a series of 14 turns which represent the three days of The Battle of Neuve Chapelle. Each day is divided into four turns with a separate night turn in between. During the day turns, both sides will fire artillery (weather permitting), move, and attack. During the night turns, both sides will dig in new trenches on the new front lines.

The game simulates early trench warfare and makes prominent use of artillery and the defensive advantages of trenches. Weather conditions and British communications among the four divisions are determined randomly each turn. Each assault phase contains a defensive artillery and defensive combat phase, making defense a major component of the game.

There are several hexes that have victory point values. The British win by controlling as many of these hexes as possible. The Germans win by preventing the British from achieving this objectives.

The King is dead and three powerful Lords vie for the throne. They will use everything in their power to win (Military Power, Allies, Knights, Nobles, Spies, the church, mercenaries, whatever it takes). Although 3 contend for the crown, in the end there can only be ONE TRUE KING!

In the game, each player plays out 3 columns of cards consisting of Knights, Nobles, Allies, and Army units.  The total strengths of the columns are compared.  Then, Intrigue Cards are played to change the balance of power.  Intrigue Cards include assassinations, bribery, desertions, reserves, etc. The final column strengths are revealed and the player who wins 2 of the 3 columns is the victor.

Overkill: Last Man Standing is a board game about space ships, guns and blood! The players control a spaceship crew fighting against the invaders.

This game has a deck of cards, four dice, eight paper figures, a game board and the rulebook.

Each player controls four soldiers lost in a space station. Every turn, you move your soldiers in the game board, finding items (by buying cards) and fighting the other players in order to survive. 
There is three missions: Steal data (Capture the flag), Invasion ( Time race ) and Overkill (Last Man Standing). In each mission you need to remake your strategy to win.

Battlejack is a player versus player high-stakes mock gambling game. Players Draft their Fighters in secret. Actions are resolved using Blackjack but the players play versus each other instead of a dealer. The players are gamblers who create electronic avatars to fight in a virtual arena. Players may use the included Fighters, or create a custom Fighter using a combination of Abilities and Gear. Players also have a Cheat which breaks the rules and an Exploit which gives your Fighter a huge short term boost. Momemtum Tokens are earned for successfully attacking and defending and can be used to increase a Fighter's Abilities.

Game Flow:
Players may have their Fighter Run or Attack on their turn. If they Attack, the player may wager points  on the Attack, if the Attack hits, it deals additional damage based upon the size of the wager. If the Attack misses, the wager is lost. Attack damage is also modified by weapon type and a random damage element based upon margin of success.

When a Fighter runs out of points they die. A Buy In oppurtunity allows players to bring their Fighter back into the Arena. In the end the last Fighter standing in the Arena wins the match.

An additional copy of Battlejack may be used at the same to increase the number of players from 2-4 to 5-8.

Game description from the publisher:

Fragments of Power is the second Battle Pack in "The Bloodquest Cycle" for Warhammer: Invasion - The Card Game. As the armies of the Old World collide, quests become more varied and important than ever. While the battles claim their blood and death, mighty heroes undertake quests to recover powerful artifacts, infiltrate enemy forces, rally troops, and turn the tide of battle.

Fragments of Power introduces three flavorful new quests to bolster Greenskin and High Elf armies, as well as Skaven in all armies of Destruction. New units, attachments, and tactics reward the play of Heroes and legends, and a bloodthirsty new Dwarf legend arrives to inspire the Dwarfs and rekindle the fire that burns for their ancient grudges.

If you begin the quest for Fragments of Power, you had better be ready for ferocious battle!
family: Collectible: Living Card Game (Fantasy Flight Games)
Game: Warhammer Invasion – Living Card Game

description:
Combat in the Lists  is a small hand to hand combat game/simulator that incorporates card management and strategy.

During the medieval period skilled knights were famous throughout the land and the lords of the realm would hold grand tournaments to demonstrate the skills of each knights in his service.  These tournaments brought together knights from across the realm to fight for glory and honor.  “Combat in the lists” is a game of hand-to-hand combat that pits knight against knight in pitch battle.  These battles were never fought to the death and the weapons used were usually dull or blunted to render an opponent unconscious but sometimes deaths did occur.  It wasn’t uncommon for a battle in the list to last for hours and end only when the last knight was left standing.  It was this lone knight that was honored as the tournament champion.

The goal of the tournament was to be the last knight standing inside the list!

Some of the objectives and actions within Combat in the lists:

1.  7 possible movement obtions
2.  Multiple attack and defense possibilities
3.  Sneak Attacks
4.  Backside attacks
5.  Pushed from hex - As you take damage you can get knocked into another hex
6.  Knocked Down - Get hit hard and you could get knocked down
7.  Multiple weapons that are used in either the Right or Left hand during battle
8.  Your weapon could get knocked out of your hand
9.  Special type of Attacks and Defenses
10. A revolutionary Attack and Defense system with multiple defense stages

Now WEAPON SPECIFIC ATTACK RESULTS
1. BROKEN BONES
2. SPRAINED WRIST
3. PUSH from HEX and KNOCKED DOWN
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable

description:
Motim Pirata (Pirate Riot) is a fun dice game were the players are pirates in the same ship trying to take the ship from the captain.
In this dice game, every die represents a pirate that you need to turn on you side to make a riot and than take the ship. Rolling the dice you have, you choose one of them and use its spacial. There is three kinds of active pirates (dice): Thug (represented by the red dice; the one that can kill another pirate), Smart (represented by the blue dice; the one that can recruit one more pirate to you side) and Drunk (represented by the green dice; useless but you can use them insted of any other die when kill). There is the captain die (represented by a black die). When you have enough thugs, you can make the riot. You roll all the red dice and the sum must be equal or greater than the sum of the captain die plus his mates, you win. Otherwise you loose and your dice goes to the captain.

Objective: be the first entering space '100' (finish school)
- moving by turns -
simple 'Roll and Move' game 
Roll the die - ending on a marked space follow instructions glued on cover inside
remarks:
published ~ mid 1950's

Objective: 
A.) become owner of 'Die Grosse Walze' ='Big Barrel' catching more than 50 % of your opponents - or
B.) save most of your meeples (more than any other); but 'Big Barrel' must catch less than 50 % of all, only
 - moving by turn; sequence: green, yellow, red, blue -
1 roll the die inserting all of your meeples (8) first
2 try to become owner of 'Big Barrel' (roll '1' after you've entered space 12)
3a if successful: remove all your meeples and chase the rest (1 step forward per turn)
3b if not: optimize your number of saved meeples (back to starting point, allowed only with matching numbers) 
remarks:
coproduction with 'Elo Spiele'

boardgame: Omen: A Reign of War – Forgotten Oath
description:
The Omen: A Reign of War - Forgotten Oath expansion includes a new type of unit – Champions – and introduces rules for a 3-4 player Blood Feud variant.

Note:  This expansion was an exclusive item for those who backed the Omen: A Reign of War. Second Edition.  Kickstarter project.
family: Game: Omen A Reign of War

description:
Game description from the publisher:

Little green men are a product of the imagination? That's what these spacemen thought until they learned otherwise during a trip to the moon. Once they arrived there, the alien Ansgar had a lot of fun trying to scare the little space travelers.

In Kraterschreck im Mondversteck, you don't know exactly when Ansgar will spring forth from a crater once again – only that he'll definitely do so at some point! The players travel on the game board in a circle with their three spacemen, and depending on the roll of the dice, they move not only their own characters but also Ansgar, who moves invisbly amongst the craters. If Ansgar pops up in a crater close to any spacemen, they'll scatter, leaving the moon to others.

Whoever has the best memory, a little luck with the dice, and the longest lasting spacemen will win!
family: Components: Game Box Used In Play

description:
A card game 2 to 4 players.

Deal out all the cards and each player looks at their cards. Whoever has the green Blanche Neige (Snow White) card starts by laying down that card. The next player has three possibilities; he can lay down another Blanche Neige card or he can play the green 7 stars Nain (Dwarf) card next to the green Snow White card. Or he can pass if he cannot do any of these options. Note that you have to play the same card if going down the columns or the card with the next lowest number of stars if going across the rows. And so the game progresses, each player having more and more options to lay cards along the rows or below in the columns.

The first player to use all their cards scores a point and the first to rack up 10 points, wins the game.
family: Folk Tales & Fairy Tales: Snow White

description:
Incredible Courage at Austerlitz - Pratzen adds the fight for the Pratzen Heights to the southern part of the fighting at Telnitz, in the Austerlitz battle.  The game adds (16) 11 x 17 map sheets to the 5 for Telnitz and encompasses the area from Sokolnitz Castle up to the area where Napoleon and the Imperial Guard started the battle around The Zurlan Hill.  The fighting forces added include the remaining 2 divisions of the French IV Corps, Imperial Guard, Grenadier Division, and I Corps.  These forces easily managed to defeat the Russian 3rd, 4th, and 5th Columns, and the Russian Imperial Guard.  This is the game where Guard meets Guard.  The Russian Guard is surely larger than the French, play this game to see how the French managed to defeat the Russians and send them routing away in what would end in the Coalition force being destroyed.  Initial copies of Pratzen will contain replacement maps for Telnitz and a replacement scenario card at no extra cost.

The last, and next, game in the set of 3 is Incredible Courage at Austerlitz - Santon and is due to be released in the middle of 2012.  It will complete the set with the inclusion of General Bagration's command and the V French Corps and the Cavarly Reserve commanded by Marshall's Lannes and Murat.  There are (6) 11 x 17 map sections to complete the northern part of this immense battlefield.

This game will showcase the map feature data of Rick Barber and have a considerable amount of detail for a 100m per hex battlefield definition.

The game also has some nice game system features such as variable combat strengths and a fairly easy to learn game system set of rules.

This is the next game in the Death Ride Salerno series and builds on the first game, Death Ride Salerno - 16th Panzer.  The map is extended to the east and covers the area where the 36th US Infantry Division had to force its way into the hills surrounding the bridgehead.  The Germans are on the defensive and must hold on and wait for the bulk of the 29th Panzer Grenadier and 26th Panzer Divisions to arrive and allow for the strong counter-attack that almost drove the Allies into the sea.  To counter this threat was a large and active Naval Gunfire effort and the 82nd Airborne Division needed to drop in and help out.

There are 4 games in total for this series and will dwarf existing Salerno games.  Next in the series is the area od the British and the 46th and 56th Infantry Divisions.  The battle for Battipaglia is highlighted next and then the area of Salerno and the defense by German paratroops in the hills to the north of the city.

Each player manage a country in sustainable development theme trivia and expansion game.

Depending on where the die-determined player's pawn lands each turn, he might be able to buy some infrastructure (50% chance) (energy, socio-cultural or economic : they cost money, energy, social and environmental resources and might provide a regular income, energy, social and environment resources or suffer some random event, join the Alliance, have the opportunity for a windfall through a trivia question, etc.

The aim of the game is, more or less, to build the largest number of infrastructures while keeping a good stock of environmental and social resources.

The object of the game is to give Justin Bieber the most kisses.

Each player selects one of the four Justin Bieber game boards. After a player completes a task from a "Kiss & Dare" card selected randomly from the deck, the contestant might be able to place a vinyl "kiss" sticker on Justin's picture. Other cards require a player to switch boards, to give another contestant a free "kiss" sticker, or steal a kiss. The tasks printed on the cards include things like doing a chicken dance for 15 seconds.

The contestant with the most kisses when the entire deck has been played is declared the winner.

Designed for 2, 3, or 4 players age 8 and older.

Includes four game boards, 60 cards, 50 kiss stickers, a storage insert, and the rules.

boardgame: Jim Knopf und Lukas der Lokomotivführer
description:
Game description from the publisher:

In Jim Knopf und Lukas der Lokomotivfüher, players start with a three-dimensional train engine in the station of Lummerland, then travel to the sea, the top of the world, and the valley of dusk, among other locations. Who will be the first to make a complete round-trip with the locomotive and correctly guess all the destinations?

boardgame: Der kleine Drache Kokosnuss und das Geheimnis der Mumie
description:
Game description from the publisher:

In Der kleine Drache Kokosnuss und das Geheimnis der Mumie, players try to help the little dragon Kokosnuss and his friends save the golden mask of the pharaoh from greedy grave robbers. To do this, the players must collect as many rolls of toilet paper as possible as only then can everyone dress up as mummies and scare the grave robbers into taking flight from the pharaoh's tomb.

During the game, players try to uncover toilet paper cards and match them to game boards for Kokosnuss, Oscar the fire-breathing dragon or Matilda the porcupine. Once a board is filled, players cannot reverse their actions. Stock up enough toilet paper, and you'll be able to keep the golden mask safe, winning the game together as the grave robbers flee.

Flinker Willi is a quick-playing word game in which players first lay out a circle of 21 letter chips, each of which has one of nine categories hidden on the obverse side. The wooden Willi figure is stacked on 13 wood tiles and placed on one of the letter chips at random.

In a round, players start by revealing the category under the chip lying to the right of Willi. Then one player rolls a die and everyone must count left from Willi to find the chip lying this many spaces away, after which the players must race to yell out something starting with that letter that matches the revealed category. The first player to do so receives one of the wood tiles on Willi if her answer is correct; otherwise she must return one tile to Willi.

To continue play, move Willi to the chosen letter tile, then start a new round. The first player to collect four wood tiles wins!

A late Victorian game which looks a lot like an English game called Bikee from roughly the same vintage. In the Bikee game, the board is assembled by laying cards next to each other but here there is a more traditional board. The graphics are extremely similar.

Velocipede Wedrennen is a promotional game for Mado & Co Utrecht (still in business today but now Mado B.V. Utrecht). 1981 was the company's 50th anniversary so that would explain why they might like promotional game.

The game is a simple roll and move game. If you land on the illustrated spaces, you must follow the instructions (Miss a Go, Go Forward 3 spaces, that sort of thing).

Objective:
place Confetti The clown on the board, trying to match three colours before the time runs out & completing your puzzle
 - acting by turn -
roll the three coloured dice: 
try to match the 3 colours with Confetti before the hour glass runs out
successful: complete your puzzle step by step
remarks: 
game was republished by Merit J & L Randall 1969

An LPS bonus mini-game, Bloody Hunlikely! is a solitaire treatment of Attila the Hun's Western Campaigns of 452 and 453 AD.

The player attempts to subjugate and pillage the Frankish part of Europe. The player's objective is to accumulate as many Talents as possible before his armies run out of steam.

The board is a single sheet of paper and components includes 28 die-cut counters.

description: This expansion of twenty fraction cards can be added to the base Krypto deck. Aside from removing the prohibition against fractions from the basic game, play remains the same

The Four Horsemen... They’ve always been here. Whenever humanity has stepped too close to the abyss, they’ve come. Often taking the guise of whatever was causing the Apocalypse of that particular age. The Fall of Rome, the Black Plague, World War 2, they were here. Ready to help usher in the end of man. United in the goal, but never the means. Because of this, either by some sort of grand plan or merely by chance, a balance has formed, preserving the time of man again and again. The Horsemen’s individual desires to be "The One" to snuff the candle of mankind’s light actually thwarting the very event they hoped to cause. Then came the Zombie Apocalypse, and again they came. War, relishing the conflict and self destruction of humans- now even after death. Famine, bringing hunger and desperation to both the living and the undead. Pestilence, with pain and suffering that could last this time even after one’s last breath was drawn. And finally Death, savoring the slow rot of a person’s demise more than ever before. But this time they weren’t alone. A Fifth Horseman has also come. One that could never exist before now. A Horseman born of music, technology, and the anger of a species on the brink. Will this new Horseman break the ages old balance that has existed and finally bring about the end of us all? Or is something else at play?

The Fifth Horseman is a 2 Player game where the aim is to bring about the end of man by defeating your fellow Horsemen and laying sole claim to the final Apocalypse. You accomplish this by landing various Strikes that lower your opponent’s Life and Hand Size, as well as increasing your own. The Fifth Horseman uses 6 sided hex cards that allow each card the ability to use one of 6 different effects (Strikes). Victory is achieved when your opponent is depleted of Life points.

Ernie the Geek, mascot of BoardGameGeek, joins BattleCON as a promotional character.

Ernie begins the game by distributing his game counters across the board, then collects them back over the course of a duel to gradually increase his capabilities. Will you prioritize certain stats to take advantage of your enemy's weaknesses, or try to build up gradually for a solid, well-rounded fight? And how will your enemies respond to your attempts to collect more? With his signature attack "Tableflip" and his finisher "Raise the Stakes" (that forces you to play another game from your collection in order to resolve), Ernie is a force of geekdom to be reckoned with!

boardgame: The New Game Aphelion or The Race to the Sun
description:
A game where the players follow different orbis with the aim to reach the centre of the board.

Players all contribute a number of counters to the Pool before the game starts  Movement is by the roll of one die. When you land on certain spaces you will probably be told to add or take some counters from the Pool

The intention was that this game would educate people about the known Solar System of the time.

A dominoes game for 1 to 4 players, on the theme of road traffic, by the author of the 1000 bornes.

RULES OF THE GAME

The box is placed flat and serves as a model.

A "contiguous" fragment is a fragment extending properly, either in the longitudinal direction or in the width direction, the image portion represented by the fragments or neighbors. Two neighboring fragments only by the edges should not be considered contiguous.

A "bad" fragment is one that involves a road user committing a careless or a contravention of the rules of the road. These fragments are shaded and numbered on the image model.

Mix all the fragments of the game, then share them with partners.

The first player chooses a piece and places. If he has a fragment neighbor, he also raises, etc. Each fragment must be placed with the previous close.

The next player selected a fragment close to the last set by the previous player, and the pose. He can continue, if another fragment placed next to last, etc.

If there is in the game, a hole (empty space on all sides by fragments), or a slot (slot in the periphery of the image), it is possible to plug this hole or that niche instead of asking a neighbor of the last fragment. Every time a player mouth hole or a slot, he can play again and where he wants to continue to ask neighboring fragments.

A player can pass. If all players pass, the one whose turn to play may be a fragment anywhere near a fragment already made.

The winner is the first who got rid of all its fragments. The other players count how many they have left fragments. The "bad" fragments are worth 4 points unless the player may explain the mistake, In this case, it counts two points.

Game made after local travel book about traveling from one light tower to another.
In the game you can play as one of the travelers: Mom, dog Jossi, cat Juppi or camper.
Game is simple roll and move game, where the goal is to visit most light towers. Different squares on the board have different kind of abilities. Like red square will stop you for few turn.

Winner is the player with the biggest score.

boardgame: Die Fürsten von Catan: Sonderkarte 2011 – Carol, die Spieleerklärerin
description:
Carol, die Spieleerklärerin  is a special card for "The Rivals for Catan" (published 2011).

Ability of the card (unit hero):

Place carol in the beginning of the game in your princedom. One-time in the turn of your opponent: Answer a rule question correctly. Each player gets one resource next to Carol.

e.g. Italy (wanted is Colosseum)
2 left player asks for a letter the term probably contains

2b  if not next player may guess 
3   first player who makes a correct guess about the wanted term may move forward number of (still) missing letters (max. 5)

Licia Colò takes us on a fantastic voyage around the world, discovering famous places, that we are not always able to locate in the exact place on the map. An easy game, especially suitable for children, with also a high educational content. 
Each player has a "marker" and must be the first to arrive at the place written on a card "destination". 
Each time you reach a goal you add a stamp on your "suitcase travel" and the first that is able to have five wins the game.

1. reset the three big wheels (adjust them showing 'Ende')
2. roll the die & move forward - choosing 1 of the 3 large circles
3. meeting an 'obstacle' - rockets or sputnik - your roll must move your meeple excact into it (rear position)

Here's a chance to play a zombie game that works like it does in the movies. The system is fast and deadly. Comes with map as well as character cards. An innovative turn order keeps you guessing and zombies are likely to surround you at any time.

The goal is for all the characters to get out of the parking lot alive.

Ultimate Baseball The Game is a strategy baseball board game for two gamers (teams).  Each gamer drafts and manages his/her team, including managing lineups, roster decisions, and ongoing game play.  Play is simulated pitch-by-pitch using an order of play and often involving simultaneous decision-making (i.e., gamers secretly makes choices simultaneously which then are made "public" and enacted on the game board).  Each virtual player that comes in the game box (240 all-time stars) as well as those included in the Ultimate Baseball The Game Player Register (over 4,000 players) are based on top picks from across the annals of American major league baseball, and are rated and ranked based on lifetime composite career stats.  Each rating (pitching, hitting, running, fielding, etc.) is based on meticulous research and heavily influences game outcomes.  These ratings, along with simultaneous pitch-by-pitch simulation and highly detailed charts and tables, afford gamers a wide spectrum of strategic options at nearly every turn.

The Ultimate Baseball The Game game box includes a basic version for youngsters and beginners which takes around an hour.  Also included is the fully advanced and highly realistic game, which takes about as long as a real-life baseball game.

Ultimate Baseball The Game is for gamers age 11 and up.

A balance game featuring everyone's pandas in different poses.

Set up the wobbling tire on a table or floor and then take it in turns to place one of 12 Panda figures onto it using the chopsticks provided. Make sure you balance carefully and don't let the Pandas come tumbling off!

The game is aimed at children but is challenging enough for adults to play, especially in lighthearted settings.

It comes with numerous play options and mini games and puzzles.

Game Contents


     12 Panda figure (the second edition adds 2 much smaller baby Panda figures)
     1 Tire
     1 Base Stand
     Chopsticks

boardgame: Victory Decision: Advanced Wargaming Rules – World War II
description:
Victory Decision: World War II is an action based game that allows you to command a platoon sized force or more in this momentous conflict. It features a unique Leadership-modified alternate unit activation system. The result is a fun and fast paced war game that involves both players almost constantly. These rules cover virtually all aspects of World War II gaming. This rulebook not only provides Scenarios but also Point based Army Lists for late war British, German, Soviet and US forces. All necessary Game Markers are also included within this book.

This rule system is written with individually based 28 or 20mm miniatures in mind. However, scale is not important in "Victory Decision: World War II". As long as your minis are individually based you can use the rules straight away. In fact we found that the weapon ranges look more realistic when using individually based 15mm infantry miniatures. If you want to play with smaller tables, just use centimeters instead of inches and all will be fine.

Using individually based infantry miniatures is the recommended way of playing "Victory Decision: World War II".
family: Series: Victory Decision World War II (A.D.Publishing)

description: Old swedish game published by Aristogame. The goal of the game is to transport gargo from all over the world back home to Sweden. It is a simple roll-and-move game and each player has their own ship that they move around on the board to reach all continents. Each continent has some plastic chips representing goods that you brin back home.

Old swedish cardgame about camping.
Published by Charts before 1950.

The game consist of cards in for colors between 1-11. The first players starts with playing one card and the other player must play a card in hte same color. The highest card wins. The goal is to win as many and high card as possible but at the same ttime avoid all nines. In the end the one whit the least nines wins. If two players has the same amount of nines the one with highest cards win.

In Capua players try to make a name and fortune with gladiators. 
Buy gladiators and send them into the arena to win victories for the Lanista and fortune and honor to his Ludus.

The game takes place in four phases
- Auction of gladiators
- Fighting in arena
- Negotiation
- Maintenance of ludus

An auction game, where we must be careful what gladiators opponents are recruiting, and what they might plan to do with them.

A fighting game, where through the dices players give order to the gladiators.

A game of negotiation, where the alliances can cover some lanista with gold - but not for long, as a rich Ludus covet the treacherous eyes of others. And in the end only one can win.

An easy game to learn and play, with a dynamic auction, combat and negotiation that never becomes repetitive, and where alliances shift like the roll of a die.

Dare to be the best lanista of Capua! Conduct the Gladiators to Glory!

Moral Conflict 1940 is a strategic team game played by 2 to 6 players, which powerfully reconstructs the military, economic, diplomatic, technological and moral conflict as it enveloped the globe in course of the Second World War. It is possible to learn and practice not just teamwork, but also strategy, negotiation, planning and focus to achieve victory. This is a streamlined 12-sided dice version, ideal for play from 90 minutes by experienced gamers.

Victory points are collected in the beginners´game like many one dimensional military games, by capturing territories and defeating the other players. In the advanced game it is possible to play and win through the further four dimensions.

FIRST the military war of great land, sea and air battles.

SECOND the economic crisis, the struggle for economic growth and access to critical oil and iron ore resources. The price of military war is clearly seen and the player who amasses much wealth can win even without fighting.

THIRD the diplomatic pressure to coerce neutral nations into trade or conflict. The excitement of winning a major ally such as the Soviet Union and the pain if they change sides.

FOURTH the race to develop new war or economic technologies, such as jet aircraft, radar, rocketry or more efficient manufacturing through prefabrication and standardisation.

FIFTH the moral decisions which shaped each nation´s conduct. Seizing power through violence and treachery is contrasted with close cooperation, trust, sharing and self-sacrifice in search for peace.

The interplay of all five dimensions on each other is the unique Moral Conflict experience. Each game progresses differently with almost unlimited options.

An extra guide book, with historical background, teaches strategy in five dimensions and how to master each dimension to win the game and in real life.

Information on Moral Conflict 1941, a deluxe 20-sided dice version for sophisticated gamers, is also to be found on BoardGameGeek. Look out for information on BoardGameGeek for Moral Conflict 1939, an easy 6-sided dice version for play by most games enthusiasts.

Objective: build as many 'matching' animals with your puzzle pieces as possible
acting by turn
1 turn the puzzle pieces of 6 animals (total 24) upside down
2 roll the coloured die and pick a correspondent puzzle piece
3 try to form/build/complete an animal; only a completed one  will deliver winning points - fantasy or real - both are accepted

boardgame: Shufflin' Feet: Put Your Best Foot Forward
description: Shufflin’ Feet is the funny game that takes unexpected twists on every turn! The players compete to win chips by playing higher valued cards than their opponents. On every round players place one card down in front of them and pick a player to roll the cube. The cube has three number sides and three action sides. If a number is rolled, players bet that number of chips, flip the card in front of him/her and the highest valued card wins the chips. Actions consist of trade, slide, and chip. Each action moves cards around the table to an opponent. If an action is rolled, complete the action and pass the cube to the next player. Play stops when a number is rolled. Quick wits and a little luck can leave any player in position to win on any turn. It depends on which direction the feet shuffle!

Pocket Shock-It is a travel version of Shocking Roulette and Lightning Reaction.

All players touch electrically conductive pads that will shock one player, determined according to the game. In Shocking Roulette, the victim is random. In Lightning Reaction, the victim is the last to press their button after the music stops playing.
family: Versions & Editions: Travel Versions of Non-Travel Games

description:
Objective:
Find matching combinations of hidden cards placed on the board collecting winning points (3 or 4)
 - acting/moving by turn -
1  mix & place cards (pairs of buildings & sights/bears) upside down on free spaces of the circuit (board)
2 move your meeple (1 - 7 moving points)
3a ending on a card check it secretly and change places with another card
3b ending on a space with a bear move forward to next unoccupied bear space
4 at the end of your turn you may flip to adjacent cards:
5a if they show similar coloured markers (same building) you gain one winning point and the cards are removed
5b if there is a bear card next to your cards ==> gain an additional point
This version of Monopoly set in Santiago, Chile uses local streets and commercial districts.  It features attractive reproductions of bank notes from Banco de Chile.

Pirate Dice is an innovative, fast and funny little board game about pirate life. It is full of everyday events from usual pirate life: battles, horror gigantic krakens and old maps. In the game you choose the character to play. It's one of the famous pirate leaders. Each of them has his own ability to help you get rich quickly.

Turn begins with the player opens the top card in the event deck and everyone gets involved in a new adventure, which usually ends with a grandiose sea battles. For example, when the card Trade Fleet comes to play all players may send the quantity of their ships they want to rob that trade ship. In case the raid was successful all players divide the loot among themselves. When the card of Royal Fleet is opened all ships of all players have to take part in this battle. But they also have two alternatives: 1) they may try to escape: 2) or they may buy off those royal ships. There are also places where players may renew their ships. Floating Tavern is such card. The current player rolls a die and adds received number to the modification indicated on the card. This sum indicates the quantity of pirates in the floating tavern. These pirates are the cheapest in the game. They cost just one coin per pirate.  After this the players start hidden auction to determine who will be the first in buying the pirates. For the hidden auction they use coins, it means who may buy more rum to the pirates. Most likely, not all players will be able to buy new pirates. The player who collects more victory points  represented with gold and ships becomes the winner.

The first game in a serie of Swedish cities. 
"We Are Sailing" says that they with the game wanted to show the changes that the city of Malmö has gone through during the last 10-20 years.

The game is kind of monopoly with trivia questions about the city of Malmo.

This game draws its inspiration from and aims to re-create the large scale space battles of Star Wars and Babylon 5. The premise for SPACE FLEET ALPHA is simple, to be the last man standing in a great big battle royale between a number of opposing starship fleets.

At the start of the game each player elects to represent one of the six available factions, each of which has a unique technological advantage and disadvantage and then is dealt a number of cards face up. Players then take turns using their cards to attack and destroy their opponents' cards until all other players have been eliminated. Depending on players play styles, the gameplay can be brutally fast and efficient with fleets of ships disappearing in the blink of an eye or slow and tense as players nip at each other's heels.

Cards are roughly divided into ship cards, which are the most numerous and the main targets of your opponent's fleet, and bonus cards which perform a variety of special abilities ranging from defensive shields, technological bonuses, reinforcements, espionage cards and thermonuclear warheads. The first thing to realize when playing SPACE FLEET ALPHA is that the playing field is definitely not level and no two games will ever play out the same. The random nature of the deal ensures that one player will always start out on top however, fortunes can quickly change requiring players to adapt their strategy accordingly. In order to deal with this, the game actively encourages the weaker players to ally against stronger opponents as well as use any leverage they might have to blackmail other players into doing their bidding for them.

Of course these temporary situations only last for so long as there can be only one winner and its this aspect of the game that makes it so exciting to play. While SPACE FLEET ALPHA can be played with 2 players, the game really starts to shine with a group of 4 or more players. Independantly designed and published, SPACE FLEET ALPHA can be ordered direct from the website of the publisher The Game Crafter which prints, produces and ships the game on demand. Overall SPACE FLEET ALPHA is definitely worth a look for anyone into space battles and looking for something on the lighter side that can be played by anyone.

boardgame: A Game of Thrones: The Card Game – Valar Morghulis
description:
Game description from the publisher:

Valar Morghulis, the first Chapter Pack in the "Beyond the Narrow Sea" cycle for A Game of Thrones: The Card Game, transports players to the massive and diverse Eastern continent of Essos with characters, locations, and events drawn from George R.R. Martin's A Feast for Crows and A Dance with Dragons. Players gain fantastic options for exciting new strategies, including versatile decks that start without agendas.

New locations reduce the "out of House" gold penalties for characters, making thematic Trait-based decks more viable than ever, and new River plots build upon each other to create interesting and increasingly powerful strategic options. Beginning a cycle intended to welcome new players while providing veterans with exciting challenges, Valar Morghulis is an excellent second purchase for players looking to expand upon the Core Set!
