description:
Game description from the publisher:

The SSU Battle Squad brings tough new soldiers to your Dust Tactics army. Trained in the art of rapid assaults, the SSU Battle Squad frequently forms the front line of SSU armies. This squad is equipped with a pair of DPM machine guns that allow them to pummel the enemy from afar.

SSU soldiers have fought on every type of terrain from the American Everglades to the frozen Russian tundra. With so much experience battling on hostile terrain, the forces of the SSU are among the most reliable in the world.

boardgame: Dust Tactics: SSU Close Combat Squad – "Fakyeli"
description:
Game description from the publisher:

The SSU Close Combat Squad provides versatile new soldiers for your Dust Tactics army. After fighting in many intense close-quarter situations, the soldiers of the SSU have become experts at this type of combat.

Well-known for their ability to craft makeshift weapons like Molotov Cocktails, the SSU Close Combat Squads form a large part of the SSU forces. These grizzled squads carry Shotguns and a Flamethrower for maximum damage at close range, and to increase their effectiveness, they have learned how to scout out prime locations on the battlefield.

boardgame: Dust Tactics: SSU Airborne Transport – "MIL MI-45 / Chinese Volunteers"
description:
Game description from the publisher:

Swooping onto the battlefield to deliver reinforcements and perform quick attacks, the SSU Airborne Transport represents a large tactical advantage for the new SSU faction in Dust Tactics.

This is the first airborne unit in Dust Tactics, and it provides SSU commanders with a large amount of flexibility when coordinating their army. This expansion also includes an entire squad of Chinese Volunteers to back up the rest of your SSU forces. The SSU Airborne Transport is a must-have for any SSU player.

boardgame: Dust Tactics: SSU Command Squad – "The Medvedi"
description:
Game description from the publisher:

The SSU Command Squad is an expansion for Dust Tactics. With this squad, you'll gain a new level of control over your forces, as this unit is indispensable in overseeing their comrades.

Use their skills to reactivate units that you've already used, bring previously destroyed vehicles back to the battlefield, or bring in squads to reinforce your army. With their powerful leadership abilities, the SSU Command Squad is invaluable to any SSU commander.

boardgame: Dust Tactics: KV47 Walker – "Nadya / Natalya / Natasha"
description:
Game description from the publisher:

The KV47 Walker is a versatile new walker for Dust Tactics! While the SSU once lagged in walker technology, they have since developed the KV47, a walker platform capable of trading blows with any opposing armor. This impressive machine can be fielded in three different variants: the Nadya, Natasha, or Natalya.

Whether you want to field the Nadya with its Sulfur Throwers, the Natasha and its 45mm Howitzers, or the Natalya featuring twin Circular Saws, there is a KV47 for any situation. Take command of this devastating SSU walker!

Game description from the publisher:

Operation "Zverograd" is a Dust Tactics campaign expansion that delivers new scenarios, rules, and background for the Sino-Soviet Union. Inside you'll discover rules for airborne units, a type of unit unseen to this point.

Additionally, Operation "Zverograd" includes the first SSU hero, top-agent Koshka Rudinova and her walker "Grand'ma". This hero is also the first in Dust Tactics to pilot a walker on the battlefield. Prepare to defend the doomed city of Zverograd in this campaign expansion.

Published in S&T in from Volume 1, issue 3, through Volume 2, issue 1.  The game materials content was a bit limited; you were given a list of the unit counters and a photograph of the map, which was a giant Stalingrad board with a few extra terrain features, especially in Finland.  The limited assertions in 2.1 appear to imply that the game was played using the rules for Stalingrad, though references in Stalingrad III to Divisional Stalingrad imply that you could also use the Stalingrad III rules.  Nonetheless, it really is a game, a bit under-presented like many other early games, such as Operation Gigantis (which eventually became Confrontation).

The theme is the German invasion of Russia in World War 2. The goals and game play could be identical (except for the new map and new list of counters) with either AH Stalingrad or Poultron Press Stalingrad III, depending on the optional rules choice made by the players, because the optional rules choice was to use one rules book or the other rules book.

If the game was played using the Stalingrad rules, the core rules were:

Stalingrad uses a move-combat sequence, alternating between players.  There is limited advance  (one square) or retreat (two squares) after combat.  Units have one-square thick zones of control; movement directly from one square in the zone of control of a unit into an adjoining square of the zone of control of the same unit is not allowed.  Functional terrain includes swamp (one square per turn movement), mountains (one square per turn movement, defenders are doubled), major cities (defenders doubled), rivers (defenders behind rivers are doubled if attacked only across the river), railroads (ten square movement bonus, must be taken all at once), lakes (impassible).  Weather is good in summer, snow in winter, and governed by a table in the spring and fall.  Mud halves ground movement.  Snow halves ground and rail movement, and freezes (converts to clear terrain) some swamps, rivers, lakes, and oceans.  Combat is mandatory between units that are adjacent at the end of each turn, but the attacker chooses which units attack which other units, so long as all adjacent units are in some battle.  The classical Avalon Hill D6 table, with results such as D Elim, A Elim, etc is in use.  Germans have limited replacements; Russian replacement rates escalate as the game goes on.  The objective is to capture the opposition's key cities (for the Russians, Leningrad, Moscow, Stalingrad) or to destroy the other side's army.  The Russian (implicit in rules) can also win by taking Helsinki, Warsaw, and Bucharest, and holding them for two complete turns.

Stalingrad was the first board war game with the modern placement of rivers down the middle of hexagons, rather than the old-fashioned placement of rivers on hex edges, as also seen in Stlingrad II.

Game Title:  On belay – The Rock Climbing Game

Players: 1 or 2 players

Game Description:  A game where 1 to 2 players start on the bottom of a rock face and take turns trying to make their way up to the top of the summit without falling off.  Along the way your opponent and Mother Nature will try to sabotage your route and prevent you from making it to the top.  As you climb you will be confronted with diverse weather conditions, faulty equipment, hazardous conditions, physical endurance, and a host of other factors, which will work against you and try to prevent you from making it to the summit.

Winning Conditions:  The winner is the first player to make it to the summit.

The following are the [7] types of Weather Condition Cards and they’re affects:
 1.    Snowing – These conditions cause the climbers to become DISORIENTED due to falling snow.
2.    Raining – These conditions may cause the climbers to SLIP while climbing due to rainfall.
3.    Sunny & Clear Skies – These conditions have no negative affect on the climbers. 
4.    Night Climbing – These conditions cause the climbers to become DISORIENTED due to the darkness and will affect the SPEED of                 the climbers which will take twice as long.
5.    Icy – These conditions may cause the climbers to SLIP due to the ice covered rocks.
6.    Fog Cover – These conditions cause the climbers to become DISORIENTED due to low visibility from the fog. 
7.    Windy – These conditions cause the climbers to take twice as long and will affect the SPEED of the climbers due to the windy              conditions.

description:
Kaiju City is constantly plagued by giant rampaging monsters. For this reason, the city is always rebuilding itself in the hopes of fending off the latest giant monster attack. Will this be the end of Kaiju City or will the monsters be defeated? Pick a side and play against your friends to decide the fate of Kaiju City!

In Kaiju City 2-6 players divide into two teams. One team controls four titanic skyscraper-toppling monsters, the other directs the efforts of Kaiju City to defend itself and drive off its invaders.

The monster team stomps their way through the city destroying as much as they can as quickly as possible using special powers like poison breath and causing earthquakes. Destroy the city and the monsters win! This team provides a lighter gaming experience allowing monster players to focus on stomping their way to victory.

The City team draws and places tiles allowing them to manage resources and mobilize the military to engage and destroy the invaders. Kill four monsters and the City team wins! This team provides a more strategic gaming experience as the city players must choose their tile placement carefully as well as managing their resources to ensure that they have what they need to drive the monsters from their beloved Kaiju City.

Experience an asymmetrical game design and have several tons of fun crashing through buildings and zapping monsters with your lightning cannon!

The elderly wizard Zeljin zipped and zapped around the woods collecting ingredients for his dinner. Looking forward to his world-famous toadstool and cactus burgers, he returned at sunset to his treehouse hidden atop an ancient sturdysprout tree on the edge of Gnarlwood Forest. Removing his cloak, he discovered a hole in his bag of Magic Jelly Beans. Oh, no! they must be scattered all over the forest floor. Even worse, these were Moon Beans, which can only be seen by the light of the moon. Dangerous creatures come out at night in those woods and the old wizard can’t see in the dark. He needs you and your friends to find them and bring them back. He has promised a reward of fun treats and magic spells made with the mashed magic beans, but only if you can bring them back in the right combinations.

Players attempt to find matching pairs of jelly beans from a pool of face down tokens and collect amusement (victory) points.

When a player discovers unmatched pairs, he or she can collect recipe cards for amusement points instead. Players must also watch out for monsters hiding among the jelly bean tokens, waiting to steal their jellybeans.

This is a family fun game of matching and memory with a drop of strategy to help kids develop these lines of thinking and to keep the parents engaged as well.

In Lutso, the game is played on a wooden board with spaces for 36 marbles. Players receive wooden pieces in various shapes. On a turn, players place a marble in a random square. If a player places a marble that creates a pattern that matches one of their shaped pieces, they claim the pattern as their own by placing their wooden piece on the board. Whoever gets  the most wooden pieces on the board wins the game.

It is the age old question: what would you do if there was a zombie apocalypse?  How would you find food?  Or ammo?  Where would you hole-up to protect you and your loved ones from the hungry dead?  Could you survive with the people that you call your friends or would you be forced to leave them on the side of the road?

Welcome to Deadworld.

In Deadworld, you and your friends play yourselves by choosing the trait cards that best fit your true personas; this is done at the start of the game.  After everyone makes their "game-self", players take turns searching the ruins of civilization for supplies, fighting off the growing legions of undead, building defenses and improvements to their home base, and doing their best to live with those they considered their peers before the world fell into chaos.

Be careful: the allure of anarchy and unbridled wickedness is powerful in Deadworld.  You and your friends may choose to follow the path of good or you all may quickly descend along the road to evil.  It's your choice.  But that choice will affect how you interact with events and other survivors.

Ultimately, the goal of Deadworld is to survive.  If all players can make it to the end of the chosen scenario, then the player with the highest survival points is the winner.

Can you survive a Deadworld?

description:
Fingerlinge is a childrens game played with hair ties in different colors.

Firs, all players randomly draw 5 hair ties an put them over one of their hands (1 tie per finger).
Than 5 goal cards are revealed, showing a sequence of ties in different colors.

Players take turn by randomly drawing a tie and putting it over a finger.
As soon a p player has the sequece shown on a card on one of his fingers, he gets that card and a new on is revealed.

The first player to have 3 cards is the winner.

The other side is a queue management card game (Guillotine, Parade, etc.) where you try to get your chickens across the road in one piece! If you don't, they're roadkill. There are two decks of card, the action deck full of queue re-arrangers, Chicken killers, bonus points, and more. The chicken deck is full of chickens (imagine that!) Whoever has more of their own chickens safe at the end of the game, wins! The Other Side is full of humor and fun for most ages...

description:
The game represents the Three Kingdom Era in Korea, around the 7th Century AD.

The game consists of three phases:  power play and point calculation.  Each player places cards on a three by three grid, then uses the results to gain either general, emperor, or cultural pieces to play on the map.  Each of the key locations have a point value, and battles are fought if more than one player is on a location.

During the point calculation phase, the player uses the points from the map to increase general or culture points, determined by the type of piece that garnered those points.  At the end of the game, the winner is the one with the most general points, but ignoring culture will subtract points from the total score.

Who will be the first to create the Rattle-Hoofed Eelbatross, the Pacific Duckguana Snail, or the dreaded Chipmunktopus?

Mail Order Mutants is a set-collecting card game in which 2-4 players assume the roles of mad scientists seeking to genetically engineer bizarre creatures to sell to rich, anonymous businessmen.  The scientists scoop raw DNA out of a shared toxic sewer to create the mutants indicated on a series of order request cards, and then ship them to the businessmen's private menageries.

The scientist who is best able to scoop, splice, and slice the DNA in and around his gestation tanks to churn out the most valuable collection of mutants will win the game, and a retire a still-mad, but rich scientist.

Mail Order Mutants is a free print-and-play game, consisting of 103 cards.

Sportsgame, football (soccer)

Play a season in the Danish Superliga (known as Faxekondi Ligaen, when the game was released).

Goal of the game: To finish as #1 in the league.

Through comparing dice rolls with stats on the fielded football-players, the gameplayers move the players up and down the gameboard (football field) and hopefully score goals (just like a real football match).

In the off-season gameplayers can transfer their players and buy better players for their team.

"Makes Literacy a Game"
A word game designed to be played in English, French, Italian and Spanish concurrently. Players choose to play individual games in 1, 2, 3 or all 4 of the languages. There are 3 Play Variations: Word Play, Sentence Play, or Theme Play.
In Word Play: Players randomly select 8 letter tiles from the bag. A player spells a word using all/some of their tiles, and takes coloured tokens corresponding to their point score. Players can also Borrow words previously played to create another word by Adding, Subtracting, Replacing, or Rearranging the tiles and so earn more tokens.
The game ends when there are no more tiles in the bag and one of the players uses up all their tiles. The winner is the one with the highest value of tokens.
Sentence Play requires words played in a meaningful sentence.
Theme Play requires the words are used in a sentence in the context of a chosen theme (e.g., environment, fairy tales, countries, etc)
Can be played competitively or cooperatively.

"A Game of Quick Wit & Repartee"
There are 2 sections: Objectives and Performance.
The Objectives section is divided into 3 areas. The player must correctly answer a multiple-choice question from the area in which they land. They move forward one space for every correct answer. A player's turn ends when they answer incorrectly, or when they move out of the Objectives area.
The Performance section is divided into 3 areas. The player must perform one of the 3 area-actions: Connections, Puns, or Retorts.
Connections - player is given 5 topics in 15 second intervals and must speak for 15 seconds on each topic, connecting it to the one before.
Puns - player has 15 seconds to respond to a pun read out by another player.
Retorts - player must respond to an insult read out by another player.
In the Performance section the player's performance is voted on by the other players [1,2,3] and their marker moves forward on the board the average of the sum.
To win, the player arrives at the centre of the board, the 2nd place player picks an Objective card and reads out from one of the 3 multiple-choice questions. If the player answers correctly they win. If incorrect, they move back one square and the game continues. Note: if the player is making a second attempt at winning by answering the question, and fails, they move back 2 spaces, etc.

A Victorian card game, featuring 55 cards showing the contemporary locations in London. Many of these buildings are no longer around and, if you know London at all, you will notice the fact that still-existing buildings are not hemmed in by modern developments and office blocks as they are now.

The game is played with 64 cards - 55 picture cards, 8 District cards and 1 Key Card. The Key Card contains the numbers of picture cards in each district. It is placed face-up in the middle of the table. The remaining 63 cards are all shuffled and dealt to the players in rotation until the pack is exhausted.

The game is commenced by the player to the left of the dealer asking any other player for a card to complete a district of which he holds at least one card in his hand. If a player asked has such a card in his hand, he is bound to surrender it, and the first player then asks for another and so on until refused, when the right of asking is transferred to the player who refused him. The latter, in like manner, then asks for a card to complete a district in his hand, and so the game proceeds until all the districts are completed. Each district, when completed, is to be laid on the table (face downwards) by the player, and the game is won by that player who, at the conclusion, holds the most districts. A player may, at any time, refer to the Key Card in the centre of the table.

The game is undated, but one of the picture cards refers to "the late Sir Gilbert Scott", the most famous Victorian architect. He died in 1878 so the game was probably new around 1880.

Early 20th century card game published by a Missionary Society, with the intention of making people aware of their work in the sub-continent. The game is fundamentally about India although Ceylon (now Sri Lanka) is included.

Three games are mentioned in the rules; Jugger, Picture Lotto and Buss. The former is the main game, the Lotto game has nothing to do with the traditional Lotto game, and there are no rules (in my set) to the game of Buss..

Players ask each other for cards in a set they already have at least one card in that set in their hand. In Jugger, the object is to collect the most sets. In Lotto, the object is to claim the most cards by answering correctly when a question is read out.

General Notes from the rules;
Picture Cards
At the top of each picture card there is the name of a place where missionaries are working. In the game these places are called 'Juggers', the phonetic spelling of 'Jugah', a Hindustani word meaning 'place' or 'post'. Where a place is not well known and the pronunciation is difficult, phonetic spelling is given.

Map Cards
These are the Key Cards. Each map gives the names of the Juggers (marked by a heavy dot) in the set.

Question Cards
All the question cards are essential for Picture Lotto.

Wineology: The Extraordinary Wine Tasting Game" is a marketing/promotional board game released by E & J Gallo Winery, to promote their line of "Turning Leaf" wines. It is a wine-themed derivative of Trivial Pursuit and is similar to Trivial Pursuit in some respects.

The game involves tasting five varietals of "Turning Leaf" wines (Sauvignon Blanc, Chardonnay, Merlot, Zinfandel, and Cabernet Sauvignon) and answering specific questions about them. The players (or team of players) taste one wine at a time, in a specific order, and must correctly answer questions in four categories (Wine Senses, Food & Wine, Wine Making, & Wild Card) in order to move on to the next varietal.

The first player (or team of players) to answer all four questions correctly in each of the five varietals is declared the winner.

boardgame: Le Petit Poucet et la maison de l'ogre
description:
"Tom Thumb and the Ogre's house"
In this game of cooperation Tom Thumb and his brothers move around the game board and must find all the stones before nightfall. Some stones are trapped (wolves are hidden nearby) and require support from the players to break free. It is possible to make the game harder by increasing the number of wolves hidden.

The ogre fell asleep at the foot of a tree, Tom Thumb and his brothers will benefit for they rob him of all his gold?

In turn, the player must roll a die and advance the number of spaces indicated, and then act on the square where he stopped taking gold at the ogre, take his next turn etc.
But beware, fall on the bell box too often wakes the ogre!

Players win if they get back together all the pieces of the ogre before he wakes up.

"Eat the Soup" in English
Players move around the board and try to eat the entire bowl of soup! Otherwise the witch will eat the players! The game can be played both in cooperative or competitive mode.

The game is won if the players have finished their soup before the fourth child is stolen by the witch.

Players have six tiles in hand. They choose one to create a path that leads to the center, the blue flower.

There are many obstacles on the tiles. Sometimes a player is completely blocked, and has no other choice but to give up.

Player who reach first to the tile  with blue flower is the winner.

Players collect cards of the same color pairs when their sum is equal to 10, 20 or 30.
The goal is to get the most out of the red cards.
The part may take place in one or more rounds.
A round ends when players have more cards.

"Consisting of forty eight pictorial cards illustrative of the Adventures and Games of the Boys of Dr. Lion's Jungle School from Constance Stannard Chapman's Original Designs."

Each numbered cards of the sets are similar, for example number 2 of each set shows an animal with a musical instrument. There are 48 cards arranged in 3 sets of 16, each lettered A, B or C and numbered 1 to 16; all the illustrations are different and have an appropriate title.

A set number of cards are dealt, probably 5, to each player. The object of the game is to complete a set of three cards, one from each letter, for example a set would be cards A1, B1 and C1, not 1 to 16 of A. The player to the dealer's left begins the play by asking his next neighbor on his left side for A, B or C of any set to complete the three cards of the set. When a player has succeeded in completing a set, he must show the completed set, and may then draw a card from the pool but no card must be drawn from the pool except upon the completion of a set, and only one card drawn.

The winner of the game is the one who completes the most sets.

This game is copyright Thomas De La Rue on the box, but the rules say that H P Gibson are the copyright holders. As far as I know, there has never been a reissue of the game so I assume H P Gibson still hold the copyright. However, I am crediting them both with this game. Almost certainly, De La Rue printed it and H P Gibson distributed it.

boardgame: Battle Royale: The First Silesian War
description:
(From the game's Consimworld folder)

Battle Royale is the first in a series of games dealing with campaigns of famous 18th century military leaders (Frederick the Great and Marlborough among others).

Battle Royale is a simple wargame about the First Silesian War, 1741-42. The campaign marks the debut of Frederick the Great's military career, however inauspicious this was for him personally. The war began with Prussia taking advantage of a perceived weakness of the new and first Queen of Austria, Maria Theresa, when she assumed the throne. Bavaria, France, and Saxony contested Maria's claim to the Austrian crown, as well as having long disputes over their borders or policies of the Austrian empire. Prussia sought to claim the rich province of Silesia from Austria and in a whirlwind (for the time) campaign in 1740 seized virtually the entire province. Prussia's Allies took longer to mobilize their forces and work out the disputes they had with one another to form a coalition to defeat Austria and overthrow its new queen. A long era of war in central Europe was about to begin. Can you do as well, or better, than your historical counterparts?

The game uses the "chit-pull" system with a deck of cards similar to Paul Rohrbaugh's Pocket Battle Games series from Against the Odds.

description:
A religious take on the Bingo type of game. The game for the youngest in the family is straight Bingo, although adults should make sure the children understand the scripture references on their bingo card.

The game for older children requires them to name a verse or story from the bible relating to the colour they draw. If other players have that colour and can name another verse or story reference, they too can cover the square on their bingo card.

In all versions, the first to create a line of five covered squares, wins.

Original board game
The aim is to complete the Olympic Triangle Course by rounding all the bouys in the correct, predetermined sequence.

Surf Slalom
The aim is to be the first player to complete the course of 6 bouys. This must be done in the right way and in the right order and ending up back at the start.

Each player has a miniature sailboard and a points card. Dice are thrown to determine the boardspeed (1 or 2 boardspeed drops 1, 3 or 4 boardspeed remains the same, 5 or 6 boardspeed increases 1). Cards are used to help or hinder progress.

You must be the first to bring your four pawns of their starting line to their finish line: The only slight problem is that the finish line is located on the side of your opponent. So we have to use tricks to get there.
You have several techniques: one to protect your camp, another, called GROOVE to move your pieces strategically. But in both cases it will sacrifice one of your pieces!

A quiz game for two or more players.  Sliding cards have picture clues on one side and questions and answers on the reverse.

Choose a level, easy, medium or hard and push out a card to reveal the picture. Answer three related questions and score the points.

Play against one other person or in teams.

the object of the gmae is to earn money wagered at the parties To do this You must have a combination of higher than your opponent to win the this..

LAPIPOKER cards represent two cards at once which are situated in two lines: one red and one black. 
Cards are on the table next to each other respecting the colored lines and each player will build the best poker on its own line of color - it should be noted that the cards are of the game for two players simultaneously. So, each player makes his own game but also try to ruin the game for his opponent playing the card on the table.

The goal is to create a family of four cards of the same color clover. To do this, you can share hands, draw a cards, share your game with someone, complete your family with one or more jokers, bring down the joker of an opponent and especially announce "Stoick" when you made ​​your family.

The game of literary knowledge and skill

Object of the Game
To win the most cards.

Bibliophile Cards
Each card consists of the opening and closing lines of a well known piece of literature as well as its title, author and publication date.

To Win a Card
Players or teams of players must identify from the opening and closing lines, any one of the following: Title, Author or Decade of publication.

boardgame: The History Channel presents Time Troopers
description:
Answer history trivia questions correctly and advance along a board track. Answer wrong and go back. Fate cards may force you go back as well. Play cooperatively or competitively with up to 4 players.

Hosted by John Cleese as Agent Wormold, there are video clips that have to be identified as well as questions.

Octopath is a tile laying, road building and discovery game for two or more players. Players control crews of dwarves operating in an unexplored part of the country. The dwarves build roadways on the valley floors, and mine the mountains, following rumours of great wealth to be found: emeralds, sapphires, rubies and gold. But not all mountains have a source of wealth. Some have mines which initially look promising, but result in dead ends. Others are not suitable for mining at all, and the dwarves just build passes into the next valley.

You try to earn bags of gold by getting your crews to build looping roads that the farmers in the valleys use, and roads that run from one mine to another. But beware, other dwarven crews are also building roads and excavating in the mountains, and they can join their road up with one of your roads to capitalize on your finds, or make one of your roads join two dead ends, losing you money!

There are two types of tiles used in Octopath. Larger octagonal tiles are the valley floors, and are just used to construct roads. Each valley tile has four road sections each with entry/exit points on two of the 8 sides of the octagon. There are 72 tiles consisting of 4 copies of each of 18 different tiles. When placed edge to edge, the road segments join into meandering roads.

Octagonal tiles do not tessellate – when you place four octagonal tiles touching each other, there is a small square space between them. The square spaces represent the mountains, and when the mountains are explored, small mountain tiles are placed in the square spaces. Each mountain tile has four road entry/exit points, one on each side. These may join together to form passes through the mountains, or may lead to a terminus, which is either a mine of a specific type (red = ruby, green = emerald, blue = sapphire, yellow = gold), or a dead end.

Frog and Toad is a curious race game for 3-6 players. The players control froads (or is that togs?), creatures which are never quite sure whether they are hopping frogs or walking toads. The froads need to escape from their current environment which they do by racing down two spiraling roads  to the exits, collecting bonus tokens on the way.

In each round of the game players first select the actions their froads will take from a shopping list of available actions. Then they move their froads. Depending on the situation, players will find that some actions are better than others, but each action may only be selected once in a round, so players scramble to select what they think will be their best actions, whilst second guessing what the other players will do.

Some actions that players may select change the settings of the game – who selects first and moves first, and what the criterion is for earning bonus move tokens. Other actions move your froads with hops or walks, forwards or backwards (going backwards allows you to pick up bonus move tokens). There are even actions that cause both of your froads to move.

There are effectively two separate races, and the winner of the game is the first player to get both of his froads to the end of their roads.

Escriba is an adventure game based on Bible stories. The game is set in the Old Testament times. One player is the Master and will coordinate the adventure. The other players are pilgrims on the land, facing several adversities.

The Master will lead the pilgrims through one of 20 adventures contained in the Adventure Book. As the game goes on, the pilgrims will collect points from the results of the battles against the adversities and from a list of Bible questions they have to answer. The player with the most points in the end of the adventure wins the game.

In the simple but tricky set-collecting card game 9Tours, players plan a sightseeing trip through Thailand, but the trip conductor imposes certain restrictions on what players can do during the nine-day trip. In game terms, players all have a hand of differently colored cards numbered 1–4. Taking turns, they form lines of these cards, but they can neither place the same color twice in a row nor have the cards in a row sum to more than nine. Each time a player plays a card, he takes one of the face-up cards as replacement to his hand.

Making a row sum to nine gains you that row, and you skip your turn for the remainder of the round. Play continues until everyone has a row. The game lasts two rounds, then a final, secret round with the cards left in hand, after which players calculate their score; having the most of one color gains you a bonus, and some special cards factor into your score, too.

The game consists of 31 cards.  There are 15 "duets" of 2 cards each and a lucky puss in boots cards.  Each side of the duet has pictures and words that go with the other half, and each duet is numbered.

All cards are dealt out.  On your turn, you pull a card from the hand of the player on your left.  After this, if you have a completed duet of 2 cards, you can lay down one duet per turn.  Play continues counterclockwise.

The game ends when all duets are played and the winner is the person who holds the lucky puss in boots card.  The winner gathers the duets in numerical order, then reads the fairytale segments on the duets as they receive them.
family: Folk Tales & Fairy Tales: Puss in Boots

description:
The object is to block Your opponents so that they can't play a card.
To succeed, you need to use addition and subtraction to the total of the discard pile is as close (or equal) to 22 without exceeding this value.
Over the rounds the player who can not discard one of his cards  will be eliminated. The last player to ask a card is declared the winner.

The version for the younger audience of the game Verbillico.
The goal is to gain the maximum time cards and Challenge by combining the verbs correctly. The game ends when all the time and challenge boxes are occupied by the players.
Verbillico combines many qualities. During the course of the game to win, it is obviously necessary to combine correctly, but also be a strategist, negotiator and have a good luck, because the path is strewn with pitfalls.

In his turn player roll the dice and move. There are 3 types of boxes in this game:
1. Time boxes that indicate the time together;
2. Challenge boxes that are testing the players;
3. Trap boxes that reveal the chance of the moment.

If player answers correctly to aquestions and conjugates verbs he places he marks this box with his token.
family: Versions & Editions: Junior Versions of Grown-Up Games

description:

The goal is to gain the maximum time cards and Challenge by combining the verbs correctly. The game ends when all the time and challenge boxes are occupied by the players.
Verbillico combines many qualities. During the course of the game to win, it is obviously necessary to combine correctly, but also be a strategist, negotiator and have a good luck, because the path is strewn with pitfalls.

If player answers correctly to aquestions and conjugates verbs he places he marks this box with his token.

Verbillico combines many qualities. During the course of the game to win, it is obviously necessary to combine correctly, but also be a strategist, negotiator and have a good luck, because the path is strewn with pitfalls.

1. Time boxes that indicate the time together;
2. Challenge boxes that are testing the players;
3. Trap boxes that reveal the chance of the moment.

If player answers correctly to aquestions and conjugates verbs he places he marks this box with his token.

MathSumo mechanism is simple: the cards represents the results and sumos on the board indicate the numbers to multiply. The game is to get rid of his cards by moving a single Sumo at a time and play the card with the result of the 2 sumos.
Special cards allow players to participate in the course of the game.

Addi Cat's is a mathematical card game of memory, concentration and speed.
translated into several languages ​​(English, German, Spanish and Italian): the main rule, the challenges of the additions, the memory and the battle.
You can see the animation to quickly learn to play the main rule.

Players take turns turning their cards over and compare their results. If 2 players have the same result (for instance 7 + 5 and 12), they must hit the mouse card placed in the center of the table (purple card) as fast as possible. The quickest gives his cards to his opponent. SOme bonus cards also included.
The first player to get rid of all his cards wins.

Contents:

     100 Addition cards, 
     19 Sum cards, 
     19 Action cards
     19 "Correction" cards.

Multipli Cat's is a card game of calculation, memory, concentration and speed.

Players take turns turning their cards over and compare their results. If 2 players have the same result (for instance 4 x 9 and 36), they must hit the mouse card placed in the center of the table (green card) as fast as possible. The quickest gives his cards to his opponent.
There are six different action cards which give more punch to the game. They either require speed or quick counting.
The first player to get rid of all his cards wins.

The air to air combat game in World War 2 Pacific theater. This game incorporates technology of the Role Playing Games. Usually played with the game master.

Player(s) control a Japanese naval fighter pilot, and performs various missions. Allied aircraft are operated by the game master.
Each mission is played like a typical air combat game. In addition, players let their pilots could survive, let him gain experience, and aim to survive until the end of the war.
The campaign game will start from China in 1940, then transfer to Philippines and Rabaul, until the end of 1943.

Para - Leader introduces paratrooper and glider carried units to Panzer Leader and Panzer Leader 1940 in 10 new scenarios (situations 31 - 40).

The game adds rules conserning the drop of paratroopers and gliders while it introduces the concept of sticks (small units that comrpise, when matched together, a regular paratrooper unit).

Although the "Designer's Notes" in Panzer Leader state the difficulties regarding the tactical simulation of paratroop action (mainly because of the lack of proper intel) the designer of Para - Leader refers to this arguement as "not completely valid" due to the fact that all units on the board are open to both players thus, self negating this military inteligence chaos.

Components:
- 141 Allied, German and aircraft counters
- 10 scenarios
- Rules and designer notes

Published in The GENERAL Vol.20,No.2 (July/August 1983).
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
Each player has a stack of green cards face down in front of him. The 18 yellow cards with the numbers are placed in the center of the table.

In his turn player turns a card face-up and reads an enigma.
The other players must hit as fast as possible on the number they think is the right answer. The fastest receives the "enigma" card.

When all the "enigma" cards have been given, each player counts the number of cards he has won. The winner is the one with the most cards.

There are 340 simple mathematical problems, divided into 3 categories : "French language and numbers", "Mathematical language" and "Operations".

Numé Cat's for younger players

Each player has a stack of green cards face down in front of him. The 18 yellow cards with the numbers are placed in the center of the table.

In his turn player turns a card face-up and reads an enigma.
The other players must hit as fast as possible on the number they think is the right answer. The fastest receives the "enigma" card.

When all the "enigma" cards have been given, each player counts the number of cards he has won. The winner is the one with the most cards.

There are 180 simple mathematical problems, divided into 3 categories : "French language and numbers", "Mathematical language" and "Operations".
family: Versions & Editions: Junior Versions of Grown-Up Games

description:
In every turn players turn cards over and compare the number they get with the other players' numbers.

If 2 players have the same number (for instance six squares, "six" or "6"), they must hit as fast as possible on the "cheese" card placed in the center of the table.

The quickest player gives his cards to his opponent and plays again by turning a new card over.

The first player who gets rid of all his cards wins.

Each their turn, players turn a card over and compare the tense of their verb with the tenses of the other players. If 2 players have the same tense (for instance "présent" and "finissons"), they must hit the "cheese" card placed in the center of the table as fast as possible.
The fastest player gives his cards to his opponent. Six different special cards give more intrust to the game.

The first player to have no more cards left wins.

Each player has a stack of cards face down in front of him.
The 9 cards with the grammatical categories are placed in the center of the table.

The players take turns turning their cards over, read a sentence then one of the bold words. The other players must hit the right grammatical category as fast as possible. The quickest receives the "sentences" card.

When all the "sentences" cards have been given out, each player count the number of cards he has won.
The winner is the one with the most "sentences" cards.

The game covers 10 sounds and their writings: en/an/em/am, on/om, in/un/ain/un, o/au/eau, è/ê/ai/e/ei, j/g, s/z, f/ff/ph, s/c/ç/ss/t, c/k/qu.

Each player has a stack of cards face down in front of him.
The 18 ending cards are placed at the center of the table.

Each his turn, players turn a card over, give the sound of the ending and then read the sentence. The other players must hit on the right ending as fast as possible. The fastest player receives the "sentence" card. And you start over,the next player turns his card over...

When all the "sentence" cards have been given out, each player counts the number of cards he has won.
The winner is the one with the most cards.

The game is on 18 grammatical homophones :
ou/où, on/ont, a/à, est/et, ce/se, c'est/s'est/ses/ces, son/sont, leur/leurs.

Each player has a stack of cards face down in front of him.
The 18 ending cards are placed at the center of the table.

Each his turn, players turn a card over, give the sound of the ending and then read the sentence. The other players must hit on the right ending as fast as possible. The fastest player receives the "sentence" card. And you start over,the next player turns his card over...

When all the "sentence" cards have been given out, each player counts the number of cards he has won.
The winner is the one with the most cards.

Ortho Cat's is a fun way to considerably improve your level in french spelling concernant of words with endings in "i", "é" and "u" : past participles, conjugated verbs, adjectives.

Each player has a stack of cards face down in front of him.
The 18 ending cards are placed at the center of the table.

Each his turn, players turn a card over, give the sound of the ending and then read the sentence. The other players must hit on the right ending as fast as possible. The fastest player receives the "sentence" card. And you start over,the next player turns his card over...

When all the "sentence" cards have been given out, each player counts the number of cards he has won.
The winner is the one with the most cards.

The players take turns turning their cards over and compare their letter with the other players' letters. If two players have the same letter (capital or small), they must hit the "Cheese" card in the center of the table as fast as possible.
The fastest player gives his cards to his opponent. Six different Action cards give more punch to the game.

The first player to get rid of all his cards wins.

A 3D tic-tac-toe variant with a random element, where the randomizer is provided by an electronic LED doohickey in the base of the board (requires 9-volt battery).  The randomizer determines a random space (on the 3d tic-tac-toe board), and if it's occupied, its owner must remove the piece.

The set of 50 cards consists of combinations of 5 colors and 5 figures. The order of the numbers, everyone knows, but there is also an order of colors, shown in part on a special card, and recalled the other on the back of each card.

The goal is to get rid of his cards by asking a sequence dictated by a die type: numbers, or color, and 1 to 3 cards. The cards are face down except the last. We can blame the previous player for asking illegal cards, of course at his own risk.

The object of the game is to make words with the highest value.

Each player takes 12 letters at random. They try to form words with the anagrams. In the case where the player can call such a word, he can put it anywhere on the board in order to score maximum points, trying to put the letter of a given color on a square with the same color
The game ends when one player has made five anagrams. The winner is then the one who scored the most points.

It is the game of speed and consumer knowledge.

You must as soon as possible, collect the cards corresponding to a specific criterion, or a named brand. You get the cards using a special vacuum.

Examples of criteria: "Mark in four letters," "Cheese," "product that makes the bubbles." etc.

A good knowledge of the consumer society is essential.

In this game you must try to align three balls with different colors (blue, red and yellow) on their respective color (blue ball on blue square etc.) on the board horizontally or vertically.
This alignment can be achieved with one or more white balls (chameleons) who take the color of the square where they are.

In his turn, player pushes a ball from the lines outside of the board to the 3x3 grid board and sometimes this may push one ball out of the board to the outside lines.

Holding Pattern is an adult air travel game designed for 2-4 players.

Players move their tokens from the start position to the finish position. players must use the assigned course on the game board paying appropriate tariffs and fees. The player who reaches the finish position with the most money wins.

The Down In Flames-Wingmen Expansion includes Wingmen cards for all the fighters appearing in DiF: Aces High and DiF: Guns Blazing.

Wingmen aid your Leader in attacking, and help to protect him when under attack. The Wingmen rules are simple and easy to incorporate, fitting on to one sheet of paper. These include the rules for incorporating Wingmen into Campaign games.

Component List:
160 Aircraft Cards
1 Page of Rules

boardgame: Was ist Was: Junior – Pferde & Ponys
description:
What do horses like to eat? What things do you need for horse care? What can be discovered in the paddock?

In Was ist Was - Junior: Pferde & Ponys, forty cards show everything that girls want to know about horses in ten different topics. These forty cards are first revealed at the start of the game, then hidden. A player draws a topic card – say, horse care – which will show 3-5 items that are on individual cards. Players try to remember where these items are and reveal them; with each success, a player moves her horse pawn one space forward on the track. Whoever reaches the goal line first wins!

A cricket card game, probably from the fifties because Trever Bailey is pictured on the box. He was a England Test Match player in that era.

Take two of the four 'Out' cards out of the deck and shuffle the rest. The cards dealt into four piles, all face down. Take one of these piles out of play and use the other three shuffled together. To decide the outcome of each delivery, turn over the top card and follow its instructions. Most of the time it will be a number of runs scored but it could say 'Out' or other options. If four wickets have fallen, add another 'Out' card into the remaining cards and shuffle them again. If 6 wickets fall, add the other 'Out' card. These rules both indicate that later batsmen are easier to get out by the bowler.

Two innings are played and the one with the best score, wins.

Very rare game from around the turn of the 20th century.

The cards feature characters and scenes from famous fairy stories. When laid out in a set of four, the bottom of each card can be read, in squence, and providing a précis of the story.

Deal out a number of cards and stack the rest in a draw pile (called the Pool back then). On your turn you must add a card to one of the stories (and it must be the correct card in the sequence, not just a card related to that story). If you cannot go, you draw a card from the Pool. The first player to use their last card, wins.

If reversed, the cards can be used as a regular set of dominoes.

A rare promotional card game made by Golden Wonder, a manufacturer of crisps in Britain.

The game, which can be played by 2-6 players, consists of:

a) 12 assignment files, each file giving the names and visual characteristics of the four enemy Agents to be captured and

(b) pictures of all 48 enemy Agents which together with 4."Licence to Kill" cards make up the pack of 52 playing cards.

Objects of the Game: Each player is given an assignment file which outlines the 4 Agents he must capture (the details on this file are not divulged to the other players until the end of the game). Using the pack of 52 cards, the players try to capture each of the Agents listed in their assignment file.

Deal 4 cards to each player and place the remaining cards face downwards on the table. (We will call this Pack A). The player on the left of the dealer then picks up a card from Pack A. If this is a card he wants he discards one of the cards in his hand. If it is of no use, the card is thrown down. Unwanted cards are placed face upwards in Pack B. In turn, each player can take the top card from either Pack A or B. One card must always be put back on to Pack B as at no time must you have more than 4 cards in your hand.

In each game you will find 4 "Licence to Kill" cards. If you are lucky enough to obtain one of these cards, you may interrupt the game at any time and exchange it for a card thrown on to Pack B.

Once all the cards in Pack A have been used, the cards in Pack B are turned face down and the game continues as before.

The air to air combat game in World War 2 European theater. This game incorporates technology of the Role Playing Games. Usually played with the game master.
The basic system is the same as Samurai in the Sky.

Player(s) control a Luftwaffe (German Air force) fighter pilot, and performs various missions. Allied aircraft are operated by the game master. Each mission is played like a typical air combat game. In addition, players let their pilots could survive, let him gain experience, and aim to survive until the end of the war. The campaign game will start from Spain in 1936 (or can be select later time), then transfer to Poland, Western and Eastern front, until the end of war in 1945.

A Ludo style roll the dice and move game, with 3 different variations.

Take Off
Each player has four planes, 1 at the airfield and 3 in the terminal. Roll the dice and move your plane from the airfield or along the flight path, if you land on another plane they go back to the nearest empty space. Rolling a six allows you to bring a plane from your terminal to the airfield and gives you another go. When one of your planes reaches 32,000ft (this does not have to be an exact throw) you remove it to your '32' spaces next to your terminal. The first to get all four planes to 32,000ft is the winner.

Land
Play works as above, but you start at 32,000ft and work your way down to the airfield, landing has to be an exact throw or you overshoot the runway.

Take Off & Land
Choose which players are going to 'Take Off' and which players are going to 'Land' and place planes accordingly. Enter the flight path either way by the amount you throw with the dice. If you settle into airspace that is occupied by another player then your opponents plane must leave the flight path altogether and return to its starting position.
Roll a six to get another plane on the flight path, after rolling a six place the plane six airspaces in. You do not need to throw the exact number when finishing. Then put your plane either at 23,000ft or in your terminal and airfield depending on whether you were Taking Off or Landing. The first person to successfully complete their flight with all their aircraft is the winner.

The Blue & The Grey is a Civil War card game which pits two players against one another in a battle between the United States of America and the Confederate States of America.

Two players choose and play as one of four famous Generals who possess their own special bonuses.

Both sides have in their arsenal Conscripts, Career Infantrymen, Cavalry, Cannons, and Gatling Guns.  Additionally, each deck has Research & Development cards which allow for certain advantages over the other player. Some of these include: Balloon Reconnaissance, rifling, and the hiring of mercenary Native Americans!

Will your battle change history for the Confederacy or follow history and reunite the Union?

Special cake is a two players game playable with the Shibumi game system. It uses the 4×4 holes board and 30 balls of the set. The game consists of three variants, created together, at the same time: "mint and liquorice candies", "sugar candies" and "mint, liquorice and cherry candies (aka the poisoned candy)".

Mint and liquorice candies
In the “mint and liquorice candies” variant the balls are 15 white (mint candies) and 15 black (liquorice candies).
Rules
Set up: a player places a mint candy and the other a liquorice candy until the pyramid (the cake) is complete. The candies can be placed either on empty board holes or stacking on 2x2 platforms of existing candies.
Game: the game starts when the cake is complete. Players take turns eating (removing) a candy, either mint or liquorice, or a line of mint and liquorice candies. The candies forming the line can be removed, one-by-one, in any desired order. 
A line is a straight group (in 3D) of at least two tastes touching candies, either orthogonally if they are in the same level or one on top of the other if they are in different levels. The lines don't wrap around corners or edges (since in this case they are not really straight when you consider the 3D space).
The player who eats the last candy loses the game.

Sugar candies
The “sugar candies” variant of the game uses 30 balls, randomly selected among the whole Shibumi set of 16 white balls, 16 black balls and 16 red balls.
Rules
The game starts with all the 30 candies placed to form the complete square pyramid (the cake). Players take turns eating (removing) a candy or a line of touching candies. The candies forming the line can be removed, one-by-one, in any desired order.
A line is a straight group (in 3D) of at least two touching candies, either orthogonally if they are in the same level or one on top of the other if they are in different levels. The lines don't wrap around corners or edges (since in this case they are not really straight when you consider the 3D space).
The player who eats the last candy loses the game.

Mint, liquorice and cherry candies (aka the poisoned candy)
In the mint, liquorice and cherry candies variant of the game the balls are 15 white (mint candies), 14 black (liquorice candies) and one red ball (cherry candy).
Rules
Set up: a player places a mint candy and the other a liquorice candy till the 29th candy, then the cherry candy is placed on the top of the pyramid (the cake). The candies can be placed either on empty board holes or stacking on 2x2 platforms of existing candies.
Game: the game starts when the cake is complete. Players take turns eating (removing) a candy, either mint or liquorice, or a line of mint and liquorice candies. The candies forming the line can be removed, one-by-one, in any desired order. 
A line is a straight group (in 3D) of at least two tastes touching candies, either orthogonally if they are in the same level or one on top of the other if they are in different levels. The lines don't wrap around corners or edges (since in this case they are not really straight when you consider the 3D space).
The player who eats the cherry candy loses the game (and, if it is poisoned, the life, too!).

Notes
According to the Shibumi drop mechanism, when a ball is removed, then any others that it supports will drop. Any ball that directly supports two or more on the level above is pinned and cannot be removed. 
Due to the drop mechanism and to the possibility to remove a straight group of balls, one-by-one, a ball of the line that you want to remove, pinned at the beginning of the move, can become unpinned during the move after the removal of another ball of the group, thus allowing its removing. The players are not forced to remove full lines, i.e. the longest lines in that direction.

Online play

Special cake can be played online (real time) at igGameCenter - http://www.iggamecenter.com/info/en/sandboxshibumi.html

boardgame: We're gonna hang out the Washing on the Siegfried Line
description:
Patriotic game that drew its inspiration from the popular song during the Second World War.

Rules inside the lid as follows;

1.    The game may be played by any number.
2.    The object of the game is to get all the washing hung on the line (i.e., set out on the table in front of each player).
3.    Shuffle the small Washing Cards, and deal to each an equal number. Put any cards left over back in the box.
4.    Shuffle the large cards and place them face down in the centre of the table in one pile.
5.    Each player chooses any three Washing Cards from his own hand, and passes them on to the player on his left.
6.    Everyone takes it in turn to pick a card from the central pile, and places it face up alongside. He may hang an article on the line as soon as he picks a card with the correct washing cost. A player picking a hazard card takes back into his hand any of his washing on the table that is specified, or otherwise obeys the instructions on the card. All hazard cards count as a turn. Each time the central pack has been gone through, re-shuffle and carry on.
7.    When one player succeeds in hanging up all his washing, the other players add up the money-value of the washing not hung out, and this is counted against them. Then proceed with the next round.
    Any number of rounds may be played, the winner being with the least money-value at the end of play. Alternatively a price-limit of, say, five shillings or seven shillings and six pence may be fixed. Any player reaching this limit drops out, and play continues until only the winner is left.

A game you don’t put away…            
Game objective: Have most pieces at the top of the piles.  
A strategic game: choose the game piece and where and how to place it, is already a brain-teaser… but to make the good choice between playing or passing the lead, is a true strategic choice!

Presentation:

-A board game with 5 axes of different heights. 
-2 series of 12 pieces: 
    4 parallelepipeds pierced in the 3 axes of its space. 
    4 cylinders, pierced in the 2 axes of its space. 
    4 cones pierced in their height. 
    (One set of pieces of the same colour for each player.)

Aim of the game:

To have the most pieces piled on the top of the axes at the end of the play.

Rules:

Each player disposes of a set of 12 pieces of the same colour. 
Lots are drawn to choose the player who starts.
The first player puts one of his pieces on one of the axes. 
Then each player plays at his turn by putting one of his pieces on one of the axes. 
When a player puts one of his pieces on an opponent’s piece, he has to play again. 
The game is over when one of the players has no more pieces, or when it is not possible to place piece any more.
The player who has most pieces on the top of the axes (piles) is the winner.

boardgame: Great War at Sea: Dutch East Indies
description:
From the Avalanche Press website:

For hundreds of years, the Dutch maintained dominion over a vast empire of overseas possessions. By the early 1900’s, many of these territories had been lost to Great Britain, however one of significant import remained: the East Indies, which would later become modern day Indonesia. Little Holland controlled the resources of an island empire of some 40 million people, rich in the resources – oil, rubber, tin and other metals - that would ultimately drive the industrial economies of the 20th century.

Covetous eyes watched the development of this region with interest. Japan made numerous inroads with trade missions, while more radical voices called for outright conquest, Germany expressed interest in obtaining island bases there (if not overall acquisition), while Britain stood by, intent that no other nation which could pose a threat to her interests should gain control the islands. Fears of Japanese aggression led the Dutch to consider a vastly expanded Koninklijke Marine with modern dreadnoughts to defend their prize.

Dutch East Indies is a 64-page book supplement for the Great War at Sea series, focusing on battles which could have and in some cases did take place in and around these islands over the period 1914-1917. Conflicts between the navy the Dutch considered but never built and Japanese invaders (and others) are explored, along with historical battles and hypothetical situations arising as the principal maritime combatants of the Great War, England and Germany, struggled for control of the seas. There are 35 scenarios, or separate game situations, as well as background articles presenting the history of this Dutch territory, the politics of the Great Powers around it, and the ships that fought or might have fought there. In addition, a campaign game is provided, for those wishing to examine the challenges and surprises that would have faced Dutch and IJN planners in a 1916-1917 Japanese invasion of the East Indies.

This supplement contains no pieces or maps; ownership of Pacific Crossroads, Jutland, Cruiser Warfare and the operational maps from Strike South (from the Second World War at Sea series) is required to play the majority of the battle and operational scenarios, while Mediterranean and Dreadnoughts are needed to enjoy them all.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet
family: Series: Great War at Sea (Avalanche Press)

description:
A "persistent world" multiplayer boardgame designed to be run continuously for at least 48 hours, preferably more.

Marketeer is a card-based sci-fi exploration, trading and combat game in a persistent board game universe. It requires one game master and one book keeper on deck at all times. A minimum of three of each is recommended for anyone attempting to set up a session. I've listed 8 players as a minimum, but that would probably be pretty sparse. 30-50 is probably recommended. any more than that, and you will probably have people waiting in line to play for most of the peak hours.

Players set up corporations made up of up to three players. Each corporation collects and trades resources, makes bets on the stock market and can battle other players. The goal is to be the corporation with the highest score at the end of the game session. There are also individual goals (called Legend Trials) and many other accolades for achievements throughout the game, such as being the first to build a star base.

The game is divided into four phases with slightly different rules and prerequisites. Each card in the game has four different functions: as ship part, as cybernetic upgrade, natural resource and combat maneuver. How to collect and transform cards is one of the main mechanics of the game, the second is the card-vs-card combat system and the last the financial betting system.

This module is entirely composed of  X-ship material, and is (in effect) a prototype for Module X1. While it is incomplete and unfinished, it is the most complete set of X-rules ever published anywhere (although these rules and ships are still subject to change in Module X1, based on your comments).

The is the actual, complete, Master Ship Chart (plus a couple of important annexes) which is used by Amarillo Design Bureau and the Star Fleet Staff for internal checking and development.
