- Make one or more threats against the adversaries,
- Defense against the opponent's attack,

User summary
A Japanese home made card game. The players lead a team of nine house keepers through three houses, trying to clear room after room, competing with the other keepers, gaining points. Rooms are laid out in a 6x3 grid and the players attack it from the sides, advancing one room at a time. Each house keeper has a strength of 1–9 and all rooms have a similar difficulty. All keepers also have special powers that can be activated with different timing.
The game relies on planning and when to use the one-time special effects, creating a chaos with virtually no random elements.

Translated box back
The rules are extremely simple! You compare numbers 1 to 9 and the highest number wins! That's all! But in this game, you never seem to win as you think. The interaction between each of the 36 characters' special powers turn the tables in a eyeblink, causing the one who should win to lose and the one who should lose to win.
A simple game, allowing for many different tactics… not to mention the chaos emerging from having several players!

Play it like a poker, by building words from two to five letters.

There are following combinations in this game in ascending order:
1. five unpaired cards
2. a pair - a two-letter word
3. two pairs - two two-letter words
4. three of a kind - a three letter word
5. a full house - a three letter word and a two-letter word
6. a square - a four letter word
7. a straight - a five-letter word bicolor
8. a straight black - a black four-letter word
9. a straight red - a red five-letter word
10. the kinty - a red five-letter word, starting with a "Z".

Contents:

     The 104 cards consist of:


a) an alphabet of red color as a 52 cards.
b) an alphabet of black color as a 52 cards.
Red always wins black.

There are 26 boxes each marked with a letter, in order of
the alphabet on the game board. The players move a number of boxes the number of words written by them on a "subject" as determined by the chance. These words must start with the letter
marking the box where the players parked.
F.e. if player is in box "M" in must form only words beginning with M.

Player who is fully covered the alphapet and is the first who returns back to square "A" is the winner.

"The fast-paced aim, launch, and score game!"

This a dexterity game with a motorized revolving game board. Players use catapult-style flippers to launch balls into four different 5x5 grids on the board as it spins. Each grid target matches one of the four ball colors. The object of the game is to be the first player to get four balls of his or her color in a row.

There are three levels of play. For young players scoring is based on the total number of balls that land in his or her matching zone. For adavanced players only balls in the matching zone count when creating the four-in-a-row sequence.

Includes the motorized board with four attached catapults, 40 balls, 4 grids, and the rules.

Designed for 2, 3, or 4 players ages 4 and older.

The game consists of a number of wire supports for a wire 'basket' into which is placed coloured balls. Each player chooses a colour. Then someone spins the 'top' which will career around and hit some of the supports and the ball in those supports might be dislodged. After the top has stopped, the player with the most coloured balls still on their supports, wins.

This would be a ridiculously short game if a winner is decided after just one spin, so it would be sensible to award a point to that player. Then the first player to get, say, five points would be the winner.

This game was made by the Lord Roberts Memorial Workshops, set up to give work and support to invalid soldiers after World War 1. Lord Roberts was a distinguished officer who had won a Victoria Cross in 1857.

A monopoly (from Greek monos μόνος (alone or single) + polein πωλεῖν (to sell)) exists when a specific person or enterprise is the only supplier of a particular commodity.

Monopoly is a roll-and-move game where players move around the game board buying or trading properties, developing their properties with houses and hotels. When opponents land on players owned property, the owning player collects rent from the landing player. Owning sets of properties increases the rent paid to the player who owns them, by the player who lands on the square. The ultimate goal is to have the most money and drive opponents into bankruptcy.

The classic Monopoly with constructions and other elements from Ulm, in Germany.

description:
Objective: 
be the first making correct guesses, thus ending the race
1 active player draws a cards and tries to form the chosen (1 of 3) term with a chain
2 very first player giving the right answer (within fixed time) gets points, together with active player ==> move correspondent meeples forward

"Morph it. The Squishy Squashy Dough Modelling Game!"

From the back of the box:

"It's charades... but not as you know it!
Every mime must be acted out with the help of your trusty friends Morph and Chas - plus the aid of a lump of dough, of course!
It's a terrific team game, where everybody does their bit in a frantic race against the clock.
Do you go for the easy 'mime' that will ensure you move forward - or gamble it all on a difficult one that could all go horribly wrong?!
Racing around the board, it will be the first team that can catch and overhaul their opponents that'll be the winners... no time to waste, get moulding!"

Morph and Chas are stop-motion plasticine characters produced by Aardman Animations (who later went on to make Wallace and Gromit), who appeared with Tony Hart on childrens' TV in the UK in the 1970s and 80s, notably on the programmes Take Hart and Hartbeat.

The game is played by two teams. One team gets the Morph pieces and the other team the Chas pieces, and they start 6 spaces apart on the 12-space board track. Each card includes 6 words or phrases describing an action or pose (3 on each side, ranked 1/2/3 in order of difficulty).

The active player chooses one and in one minute has to get their team to guess the action or pose using their figure, the modelling dough, and Yes/No answers to any questions their team asks. Depending on success or failure, they then advance or retreat a number of spaces along the score track equal to the difficulty rank of the item they chose. There are also two "All play" spaces.

The winning team is the first to catch up with the other team on the track.

Saddle up with the heroines of the TV series, Steph, Lisa and Carole for a thrilling race!
In first part player must wins all four accessories to prepare your horse (saddle, bridle, brush and bucket), then place in training at the armory for master the five gaits of your horse, and finally the race on a cross country course happens with obstacles to win the rosette and be voted best rider!

This is essentially Tic Tac Toe with the element of Pitch Car and Sorry Sliders added.

You get a carry case that holds 10 disks that you flick along ramps to a 9x9 grid where you attempt to get 3 in a row.  The ramps when opened up give you about 30 inches of playing length.  There are two starting lines for beginners and advanced players and walls for back shots.  You can take control of a square by landing on the square after your opponent.

This is a roll and move game about visiting places in Richmond and collecting 8 Travel Cards and then return their residence.  The locations are popular places in Richmond from the 1980s or historic locations (like the Poe Museum).  It was a limited edition game when it came out and included $100 in coupons (yippee!).

By pressing the back of the electronic chameleon the sequence of four colors yellow, blue, green or red starts changing. Press it again to try to stop the color of your choice: it's blue? Bravo! You discard one of your blue chameleon cards (or joker) from Your hand. But beware! If you are not fast enough, it is the chameleon who himself chooses a color ... The first player who discards all of his cards wins the game.

From the back of the box:

"An 18-Hole Adventure Game

Fast, Action Golf that's easy to Play.  Beginners or Experienced players will feel the excitement of real golf action.

Play real 18-Hole, Par 72 Golf on this colorful Gameboard.  Roll the dice, avoid the hazards and land on the green to beat or match the par of the hole.  Lowest Score wins."

The game comes with a spiral bound book of the course, which is covered with dots.  On your turn, you choose how many dice to roll based on a chart (dependent on if you are on the Tee, Fairway, Rough, Trees, Sand or Green) and move the number of dots based on the outcome of the dice.  All the other rules for golf apply.  Lowest score at the end of the 18 holes wins.

A family game to find words and their definitions in the selected dictionary.

Choose a secret word, and be the first player to collect each of the letters that compose it. But to earn a letters, roll the dice (numbers 5-10), move aroung the board and use your knowledge to solve puzzles, form the words, exchange letters or bluff your opponents.

Buenos Aires Soy Gardel is a boardgame that involves competition, ramdom and knowledge of Buenos Aires City.

The game combines a playful look at the passion of Buenos Aires, and was conceived to surprise the participants with the thousands of stories that contain in every corner of the city in each of its interesting places, emblematic buildings, characters, historical events, stories and features.

The objetive in the game is to become Gardel, answering a series of questions about the city of Buenos Aires, to thereby conquer neighborhoods, which is indicated by placing obelisks on each of them. The first to win 3 neighborhoods of Buenos Aires is the winner of the game. Very simple and fun.
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable

description:
It's a game about jousting. The object is to remove both your opponents knights.

The game is played on board with two lines of holes which the knights fit in. Each player has two knights which are positioned facing the opposing player on one side of the board.

Player 1 moves one of his knights 1 space, the player 2 moves one of his knights 2 spaces, player 1 moves one of his knights 3 spaces. Each player continues to move a piece one hole further than his opponent's last move until one of the pieces reaches an end hole where it turns and complete its move in the opposite direction.

When an end has been reached or 'bounced off' the opponent's next move distance reverts to 1, each move again being one higher until an end is again reached by any piece.

A player must move his pieces forwards until one of them has reached the opponent's end of the board, thereafter both pieces may move in either direction.

When a pieces finishes its move alongside an opponent's piece, it scores a "hit" and the opponent's  piece is removed, when a player loses both of his pieces he has lost.

City Inc, is a city building card game for two to six players! With an easy to learn, hard to master style of play.  Each player is tasked to help build a great city, while trying to tailor it towards your Agenda and Occupation. Featuring a blocky 16-bit art style coming with over 100 cards and a quick to learn rule set making it easy for the whole family and friends to learn and play!


Excerpt from the rules:

Welcome, rich sucker wise investor! As a new majority shareholder in the company, this orientation tour will teach you the basics of running a multinational conglomerate successfully. By the time we’re done with you, you’ll have the ability to shape the company’s direction as we expand into the city of Metropolitia and absolutely will not be a figurehead whose pocket we’re picking. Definitely not. Perish the thought.

Moving right along, I suppose you’d like a tour of the place? Given that you control it, that seems pretty reasonable. Well, here we go then…
90 City Building Cards 
6 occupation cards 
6 agenda cards 
6 Headlines 
1 Rules Sheet 
1 Tuckbox

That’s a fair haul, huh? Just goes to show that a few billion dollars still does get you a lot in the modern world, I suppose. Anyway, on with the orientation. Has anyone mentioned to you that there are three other companies of roughly the same size and resources, all making a play for the same city at the same time? Unlikely, isn’t it? You’d think we’d plan these sorts of things a little better. Maybe that can be one of the ‘big changes’ you make around here once we’ve put you through orientation, huh?

--Goal of the game--

Our company can chase the others out of the city in one of two ways.

(1)    Control 20% of the city’s total income before anyone else

or
    (2)    Control the greatest percentage of the city’s income when the place gets as big as it can be (10x5 tiles)

So, I know we’re essentially at war with those other corporations and there probably shouldn’t be rules about this sort of thing, but there are. If you don’t follow them, the other corporations will all call us names and tell on us to judges. Seriously, boss, stick to the rules, it’s just easier on everyone.

Are you stumped by life’s deepest questions?
Would a Pirate beat a Ninja?
Could a Monkey dodge being eradicated by a Robot?
Can a Zombie avoid a Rampaging Viking?


Never fear, Purgatory Games is here to help you answer these pressing questions as we announce Cliché Conflict a fast paced game that brings the biggest stars of Geek Culture together for the craziest free-for-all battle.

Quick Overview:
Each player picks one of the icons and uses a deck of power and special cards to try to achieve one of 2 goals:

1) Exhaust all other players and have them leave the battle OR
2) Collect Special items that you are questing for!

The first player to achieve this wins the game. Simple!!!... or is it?!?

Comes in 3 sets:
- Pirate vs Ninja
- Monkey vs Robot
- Zombie vs Viking

Components (per set)
- 2 x 36 Card Faction Deck
     - 33 Power per deck
     - 3 Special per deck
- 1 Full Color Rule sheet
- 1 Tuck Box

The game simulates snooker through the playing of cards to represent shots.  Scoring is the same as in snooker, with cards representing the different coloured balls being "potted".  Breaks can be built, but the tactical use of safety shots, including snookers, will be needed to come out ahead over a series of frames.

Each player is dealt 5 cards, each of which represent a possible snooker shot: coloured balls score points, snooker and safety shots are defensive making it difficult for the opponent to "pot" a ball, whilst the fluke cards also score points acting as a "joker" for any ball.  Each player lays cards until they can no longer continue their scoring break, refreshing their hand after every card laid.  Their opponent then lays cards to score points in the same manner.  The game ends once all the red and coloured balls have been potted as in the real game of snooker.  The winner, as in snooker, is the player with the highest score.  More typically, one would agree to play a match over a number of games.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

In La Course à l'Élysée, players take the roles of a candidate in the presidential election and each player also is given a party. Your goal is to seduce the French. Under the spotlight, you multiply the televised debates. During these face-to-face with your opponents, you expose your political agenda, with ideas hastily prepared by your campaign team.

A pawn to your color evolves on a board representing the steps of the Elysee. Depending on the square where you are you should follow the instructions of a card or participate in a challenge.

You may use dirty tricks and ideas you have borrowed from others, you will choose an opponent for the debate which will be refereed by a "journalist", the player next to you whose role will be to control the time allowed to each.

Similar to Voyage en France by having educational point.

Each player receives some destination cards with places in France. The players have to move along the board by throwing a die. The first player who has visited all of his/her destination cards and arrives back in the starting place wins the game.

Additionally, in his destinations, player must answer questions: population size, name and department number etc. to score points.

Matakana is a game for 3 to 5 players of planting seeds, harvesting vegetables and selling them to customers at the bustling Matakana market.

Players will compete for sunshine, soil & gnomes (of course!) in a race to plant seeds and create the most bountiful garden.  Those vegetables are then harvested and taken to the local market where players sell to the waiting customers.

A game of Matakana takes place over rounds of two distinct phases: Gardening & Harvesting.

GARDENING PHASE

In the Gardening phase, players will compete with each other to take a row of cards that will allow them to cultivate their garden. The cards that players collect are:

1) Gnome cards - allows players to collect gnomes which act as a currency of sorts when planting seeds

2) Soil cards - allows players to take soil tiles and lay them in their garden (soil tiles come in various shapes and sizes and you will need them before you can plant any seeds)

3) Sunshine cards - dictates which columns in your garden you are able to plant seeds in this round.

Depending on the draw of the cards some rows will be more lucrative than others and it will be up to the players to decide when is time to compete for those cards. Once players have collected their cards they will lay soil and plant seeds according to those cards.

HARVESTING PHASE

The second phase of the round involves rolling 3 regular dice to determine which column of your garden will harvest vegetables.  The player rolling the dice choices a pair of dice from the roll and the sum of those dice is the column in the garden that all players can harvest.  Players then take vegetable cards that match the seeds they have planted into their hand.

Players will now have an opportunity to openly trade with each other for vegetables that they do not have.  Players will do this to sell a certain group of vegetables to waiting customers at the local market.  The market is a 9x9 grid made up of 9 customers of varying colours.  Each of these customers is wanting different combinations of vegetables and the players who can serve that customer with the vegetables that they are wanting will take that "Customer" and place it face up in front of them.

SCORING

Once the deck of Customers runs out the game will end and players will tally up their scores.  Points are given for:

1) Creating full columns of seeds in their garden
2) Having the majority of Customers of a certain colour (with points available for those in 2nd and 3rd)
3) Having the most money at the end of the game

To do well in Matakana requires a player to do well in all areas of the game: creating a bountiful garden that harvests often, trading wisely with other players to ensure you have the vegetables that you need and finally, keeping an eye on what customers other players are serving so that you can maximize your scoring opportunities.

A game of Matakana takes approximately 60-90 minutes and players 3-5 players.

Each player chooses an animal to build.
Twiga the giraffe and the monkey Chim are cut into 9 pieces. Each piece is available in 6 sizes.

In his turn player rolls the dice to determine the size of the next piece and place the piece with that size in front of him in correct order.
(If player roll 1, he gets the lowest piece, but if he rolls 6 he gets the highest piece of animal)

Player who has the highest animal is the winner

User Summary

This an unusual take on the usual Monopoly theme. In Greekopoly the object of the game is for the players to travel around the board collecting colonies (fraternities & sororities), build on these, and force other players to go broke. (Players have to pay dues when landing on other player's properties.) When only one solvent player is left, that player is the Winner.

There are several interesting wrinkles in this title. Unlike in Monopoly where the properties for a neighborhood are within a few spaces of each other, Greekopoly Triads (a combination of 2 fraternities & 1 sorority of the same color) are distributed on different sides of the board. In order to start building on these properties all the properties of a Triad must be owned by the same player. There are also Independent Colonies. Players may build on these without owning other properties, but only when they land on them. (This is in contrast to a Triad which may be built on at the rate of one building or upgrade per turn.) Other properties, simply referred to as Independents (like Greek Sportswear, Campus Pub, etc.) can be bought, but may not be built on.

The game's IFC cards and TGIF cards fulfill the roll that Community Chest & Chance have in classic Monopoly. They introduce the role of chance (with positive and negative outcomes) with the draw of a card. There are also some special spaces on the board, some positive, and some negative… like the Dean's Office, where a player may be stuck so long he has to forfeit one of his Colonies.

This title was clearly meant to capitalize on the enduring popularity of the 1978 film National Lampoon's Animal House" while skirting the need to pay licensing fees. Even the cover art, which also appears on the game board, strongly resembles the style and the content of the poster for Animal House's theatrical release.

Hamlet is a tile placing area-control game for two to four players that revolves around each player taking on a specific role for the turn in an attempt to build a medieval village. Each turn is divided into two phases. During the first phase, each player secretly selects a role from a limited set that will determine the play order for the round, as well as granting either a positive or negative benefit. During the second phase, player's place location tiles and play events while jockeying to place their tokens in such a way as to gain control of the largest point producing areas. Once each player has gone through all of their roles, the game ends and the player with the most victory points win.

From the box: the biggest TV success in a new sensational game for all family!

Show do Milhão 2 is game based on a brazilian Q&A  TV show. The player is presented with a question and four possible answers, but only one is correct. The player must choose the right answer or he loses the game. Addionally, the player may skip up to 2 questions, randomly excludes 0 to 3 wrong answer in 1 given question, or ask the other players about their opinion in 1 given question. As the player advances, questions begin to become more difficult until the final answer, where the player must answer without any help.

Inter Ação is a game in which players randomly draw cards and have to either drawn or mimic the object, action, people or animal in the card. If the player team correctly identifies the answer, they advance in the board. If they doesn't identifies or if the opposing team identifies first, the opposing team advances in the board.

From the box:

UFO? James Bond? Restaurant? What's your ID? The answer is "glued" to your face. It's more than just what you know. Be smart, do the right questions and guess your ID quickly. The cards, with very funny images, garantee laughs during all play. You will laugh and have fun trying to discover yourself!

Every player attaches a headband with a card, and the card may have a famous person, a object or even a place. Players alternate turns asking questions, and the other players must answer either "yes", "no" or "I don't know". When a player correctly guesses the content of his card, he advances one space in the board, while the others continue with the same cards on their heads. The first player to cross the board is the winner!

boardgame: Ore no Shikabane wo Koeteyuke -Jigoku Meguri-
description:
It's the expansion of Dungeon Star Dungeon Star

It's changed to the corporate rule to be arranged from the original version by Kenichi Tanabe.

The system is based on the original rule.
The components are only 20 cards.

The players make the party for the dungeon of the hell imaged in Japan.

Two players play as the Red or White Queens of Wonderland.

Through playing cards as either events or as influence, players can spread their ifluence throughout a modular map of Wonderland in order to gain control of the magical world.

Players also can try to control the mysterious Alice and help woo her to their cause.

The Queen who can influence Wonderland the greatest wins.

A children's game for 2-4 players.  Each player chooses 1 of the 4 recipe cards that (s)he want to bake.  Then it's a race to see who can "bake" their cupcakes the fastest! In order to bake their goodies, players must first collect all the ingredients on their recipe card.  To get your ingredients, you must flick the spatula spinner and hope it lands where you want (and not the the rotten egg - lose a turn space!)

The game is designed to be a fun way to help kids learn about following multi-step instructions, patience, taking turns, and matching & categorization, as well learning about baking in general!

Plus, the game is made from 90% recycled materials and is printed with soy-based inks!

Experience the struggle between good and evil represented in John Bunyan’s classic Christian story “The Pilgrim’s Progress”.

"The Pilgrim's Progress" book by John Bunyan is a metaphor for the Christian's journey to heaven and his battle against the enemies of his salvation. He is also weighed down by a great burden, so unbearable that Christian must seek deliverance. Narrated in the form of an allegory it records his dangerous journey and safe arrival at his desired country. In this game, the player(s) represent the pilgrims as they travel through the perilous land on their way to Celestial City.

OBJECT OF THE GAME
The player(s) must leave Destruction and make it safely to the Celestial City. In their way, they must overcome many obstacles that test their faith in a territory full of enemies and danger.

COMPONENTS
  - Player board
  - 20 location tiles
  - 25 location tokens
  - 10mm cubes markers
  Cards:
  - 51 Companion 
  - 10 Valley of the Shadow of Death 
  - 1 Divine Intervention

Push is played on a 15X15 square board with each player having 10 stones. Pieces move one as a chess king, and a player has three moves on a turn.

There must always be a one place buffer between opposing pieces. When a piece is moved next to an opponent piece it is pushed away one space to create the buffer. If a piece is pushed into another piece, or the side, it is captured. A player loses when they are down to one piece.

Push was an entry in the THE THOUSAND-YEAR GAME DESIGN CHALLENGE

A VERY High-Voltage Card Game for 2 to 6 players.

Through clever play of your cards, you place bids, hoping to win tricks which will bring you more powerful cards to use in future hands. So it's a blind-bidding, trick-taking, deck-building hybrid.

The 144 cards in the deck depict events in the amazing life of the great electrical genius, Nikola Tesla, one of the inventors of our modern age. Following the successes and pitfalls of his life story, each player in this game attempts to accumulate the greatest amount of Voltage (victory points) before the deck runs too low.

In keeping with the theme, many aspects of the game are in threes, and victory points are measure in Millions of Volts (A.C., of course).

Each player begins with an "Input Deck" of nine cards. From this they deal themselves a hand of six to play the round. Each round then consists of three tricks. The first trick consists of (A) drawing three "prize cards" from the main deck that the players will try to win and (B) each player chooses three cards from their hand to bid, trying to win those cards. (Sometimes, if the "prize" is undesirable, a player may play low to intentionally lose the trick.) The winner gets the prize cards, and all players keep the cards they played, which go into their "Output Deck" (discard) for future use.

The second trick in the round uses TWO prize cards and each player plays TWO cards from their hand to the trick. Finally the last trick is a One-Card trick. And again, prize cards go to the player's own Output Deck, and all other cards are recycled by each player.

In succeeding rounds, when a player's Input Deck is depleted, their Output Deck is shuffled and placed face-down to become the new supply for their hand. This way, the prize cards come into play as the game progresses. Some cards are "Power Cards" which cause extra draws, discards, and actions to be taken which add extra value to one's hand, and can hinder an opponent's efforts.

The winning is determined after all cards have been won. Some cards are "Positive" and some "Negative" scoring cards, and several have special point values. The suited cards do not have a scoring value UNLESS the player has acquired a "Value for your Voltage" card, which gives those cards points at the end. Therefore, gaining those during the game (even though they have no value until the end) can make the difference in a winning or losing score.

Now available at Amazon.com and RedHenToys.com !

description:
Antipode is played on a 5-hexagon, with elements of Hex, Othello, Yinsh

On a turn a player places a token of his color, flipping any adjacent tokens (ala Othello).

And the second step of your turn is to move your pawn. You may move your pawn any number of spaces in a straight line, making no turns in the process.

Your pawn must come to rest on a token of your own color. You may not land on an empty space, or one of your opponent’s tokens. While you are allowed to pass over as many tokens and empty spaces as you wish, you may not, under any circumstances, pass over your opponent’s pawn. Passing over tokens causes them to be flip.

The game ends when one player successfully makes a connection from any one corner to that corner’s exact opposite (Hex-like). The connection will cover a minimum of nine spaces, but the connection does not need to form a straight line.

boardgame: Modern Naval Conflicts: GIUK 1980s Expansion / Falklands Conflict
description:
This Expansion adds 200 new and updated unit data sheets (on CD-ROM) for NWS "Modern Naval Conflicts: 1970s" game to cover the USSR, UK, and USA for the 1980s period, as well as adding unit data for Argentina and UK for the 1982 Falklands Conflict. The Expansion also includes two new thick paper gloss map panels (each 11" x 17") that cover the Falkland Islands and Argentine coastal areas so that you can create and/or play scenarios that can range over the entire Falklands theater.
 The Expansion includes a 20 page Rules Manual that has two sections: the First section adds new, expanded and advanced rules for MNC that add new depths of realism to the game, as well as accounts for advances in technology that occurred during the 1980s time frame. The Second section of the manual covers the 1982 Falklands Conflict, with geographical and related information (as well as rules specific to the Falklands Conflict) needed to plan, create, and play scenarios for the Conflict. This section also contains information about different types of missions that each side performed during the Conflict to help players create their own scenarios. Each mission type also includes a historic 'starter scenario' that demonstrates a typical version of that mission.

The Expansion also includes a new sheet of 280 die-cut counters, with new counters for missiles for red and blue sides, and adds sufficient counters to add a third side (green) to play, which can be used for the Argentine side in Falkland scenarios, or perhaps as a minor navy in a GIUK battle.

Website: http://www.navalwarfare.net/mnc.html

Rush Run Riot is played in an 8X8 board.

Each player has a king (unmoveable) set in opposite corners.

Players also have 4 pawns which can move as rook up to 3 spaces. Players move two pawns per time.

A pawn may jump a pawn of its own color.

There are also four neutral 'bouncers' which are shared. They move as a chess king (and are moved in lieu of a pawn move.

Players must move their pawns across the board and surround the opponent's king.

It was an entry in the Thousand-Year Game Design Challenge.

Dollar Bill Poker - An inset on the box lid shows Tony Randall and Jack Klugman endorsing the game.

Dollar Bill Poker (a game of bluff and challenge) is similar to "Liar's Dice" using a special deck of numbered cards instead of dice.  There are six cards of each number 1 through 6 and six "Wild Cards".   Everyone (from two to six players) starts with a handful of dime-sized cardboard discs, each of their own color.  A special green playing mat is placed in the center of the playing area.  It contains places for cards and a grid for the betting discs.  The dealer deals out a predetermined number of cards depending on the number of players, and each player lays three cards in the "Down" space in front of him on the game mat.

The first player will add a card to their "Down" stack and place a bid of at least two of any number one through six by putting a disc on the grid  in the proper row and column to indicate their bid.  In turn around the board, each player must make a higher bid or challenge the bid just made.  The bids reflect the combined total number of cards in everyone's "Down"  stack having the same number on the card as the bid  (including "Wild Cards").   For example: a bid of 4 fives, means that a player thinks there are at least 4 cards containing the number five (or "wild") in the collective total of everyone's "Down" stack.  Players may bluff in their bidding in an effort to outwit the other players.

When a challenge occurs, the other players (in turn) get to place a higher bid or allow the challenging player go through with the challenge.  If no higher bids are placed, the cards from everyone's "Down" stack are revealed to see if the bid was successful or not.  Standard poker chips are used to keep score.  The winner is the player with the most chips after the predetermined number of rounds have been played or some other end-game condition occurs.  (The rules are inconclusive on how to end the game, but a predetermined number of rounds seems to be the direction intended by the game designers.)

Game contains 4 pagodas and 16 puzzle pieces, along with a spinner.  To start, all puzzle pieces are placed face down in the center and each player takes one pagoda.

On each turn, the player spins the spinner.  If it lands on black, you turn over a black piece from the pile.  If it lands on white, you turn over a white piece from the pile.  If the upturned piece matches the player's pagoda color, they keep it.  If not, it is shown to all players and returned to the center, white or black side up.  If the spinner lands on Mushu, the player must trade their pagoda with another player.  First player to complete their pagoda with all 4 pieces wins.

Translated from the French rulebook:
You don't know? It seems that our dear Paladin has received a fantastic proposal. He is on tour with a series of conferences entitled "My Struggles Against Evil". It is so famous, he was offered a caravan of luxury and in an interview with the magazine "Adventures, Adventurers", he confessed he did not intend to resume his original post.
Good news for you, right?
Well actually ... not really. It's complicated getting rid of the righteous. As soon as the tent of the former paladin was deserted, not one, but two of his colleagues took up residence: a slender elf and a robust dwarf (sigh). Beware this rather incongruous duet ... their mind is as twisted as that of their predecessor and they seem equally determined to fight Evil (sigh ...).

This mini extension allows you to replace the two original paladins with two pairs of new paladins. Leave the original Paladin in the box and place the elf and dwarf pair on the tent at the start of each year.  The first person to cross the paladin line on the evilometer will get the elf.  If another player crosses the line, the less evil of the two will get the elf, and the other will get the more difficult dwarf.

The elf is only worth 4 points at the end of the game while the dwarf is worth 6.

boardgame: Space Hulk: Death Angel – The Card Game: Deathwing Space Marine Pack
description:
Game description from the publisher:

The Deathwing Space Marine Pack, a Print on Demand expansion for Space Hulk: Death Angel - The Card Game, includes an alternate set of Space Marines to replace the ones from the Death Angel base game.

By simply switching out a number of your base game combat teams, you can bring new strategic options to your fight against the vile Genestealers. In addition to bringing new abilities such as Hellfire and Trail of Destruction, the Deathwing Space Marines offer an expanded use for the support tokens found in the base game. From an Apothecary that can use his Support Tokens to assist his wounded brothers, to the relentless Interrogator-Chaplain who is able to give a fellow Space Marine any Support Token he spends, the Deathwings make optimal use of the Support action.

description:
Truo or False? 2000 unusual and surprising assertions await you in four areas and interesting news: science and technology, environment, health and curiosity. 
Players must reach the boxes "Quiz" to be able to answer questions and win the corresponding rings. The first to win all 4 different "Quiz"-categories is the winner.

Party game for children. 12 cards with different photographs are placed where every participant can see them. They get a sheet to record their answers on. Each picture will have in it at least 4 items beginning with a letter of the alphabet. Picture 1 might be letter D, Picture 2 might be letter A, and so on.

The child with the most correct answers, wins.

It was selected as a favourite game by the BBC's Children's Hour programme.

A set of travel and hunting dogs and cats must walk around the board (in the opposite direction, to join their boxes with their pictures. Tokens are moved by rolling the dice. A little dog chasing a cat (or vice versa) arrived exactly on his square. The first player to put all his chips into the opponent's side triggers scoring.
10 points is earned for a chip on his arrival box, for a token in the opponent's camp, -5 is earned for a token that has not moved.

Contents:

     A plateau with 148 boxes
     24 dogs and 24 cats chips
     48 action cards representing tokens
     An explanatory booklet on the 48 dogs and cats.

Party game. Each guest is given a card with questions on it. The twist is that each answer will be a word related to the game of cricket. For example, 'Imposed by a judge' would be 'bail'. There are 12 answers in all. The most correct answers, wins

Party game. There are card slips (like tickets in size). One slip will have a word on it. Its matching slip will have another word but related to the first slip word. For example, a first-half slip might have the word 'Buckingham' and its other half would be the word 'Palace'.

The second half slips are placed around the house. Each player is given a first half slip with the word that will match one of the slips around the house. At the word "Go", the players set off to look for their match. If a player thinks he has found it, he takes both slips back to the host and, if correct, earns one point. Then he is given another slip to solve. After the host calls time, the player with the most points, wins.

The game “Underwood” reminds you of a simple card game based on its game mechanic but the actual point of it is to form words using letters, sort of like “Scrabble”. “Underwood” has a much more “intellectual-educational tint” compared to normal card games and a much more dynamic gameplay than “Scrabble”: words are easier to form, there are more unexpected events in the game and a lot less stress.

At the beginning of the game each player receives 7 cards. The deck is placed on the table, one card is drawn and placed next to it, this will be the beginning of the “tape”. Players then take turns.

A players turn consist of:
• Draw three cards from the deck,
• If possible form a word using the cards from your hand and placing it before you,
• Place one of his remaining cards to the “tape”.

Each player during his turn adds one card to the “tape”, this way the “tape” becomes longer. If a player needs a certain letter to form a word but doesn’t have them he can take them from the “tape”, he also takes the remaining letters following the one he took.

The “Underwood” card — a free card. Can replace any letter in played word. You can take the “Underwood” card used by another player by replacing it with the letter it was used as. “Underwood” cannot be placed on the “tape”.

Prize cards can be played together with a word if the word fits the cards criteria (for example, is a piece of clothing, none of the letters repeat). The prize card increases the points for that word and can give a player some extra possibilities.

When a player “leaves” (has no cards left to play at the end of his turn or only has prize cards), the round ends and points are counted. Points for a players word are calculated based on the letter cost of that word (each letter is worth a certain amount of points) and prize card used. The cost of letters that remain on the players hands is deducted from his point total. It is possible to get a negative result due to this.

Points earned during a round sum up with points gained on previous rounds. The game is played until one player earns the necessary amount of points to win (the amount of points should be decided before starting the game). The first one to earn them is the winner.

This game contains: 
• 102 letter-cards; 
• 14 prize cards;
• 4 “Underwood” cards; 
• Game rules.

More about cards
A letter card, apart from the letter also has its cost shown (how points a player gains from using it in his word) and the number of cards in the deck with this letter.

A prize card has: an icon, conditions to play this card and the prize that it gives. The icon shows what the card requirements are. There is also a more detailed explanation on what needs to be done.

From publisher's website

A vocabulary-learning game featuring the colorful illustrations of Swiss artist Karen Ichters. This game has 50 flashcards, 10 playing boards and 125 tokens to let you learn 50 words in 5 languages (the 4 languages of Switzerland plus English). Practice your language skills by trying to associate images with words. Young children can play an easier version using boards that already pair images with words. Older children and adults can play a more challenging guessing game using image-only boards. The first and lucky one to guess five successive images on the board shouts "Lingua" and wins!

Party game. 12 cards are placed around the house, each card having a cryptic clue to a subject sketched on it.

Example: a phrase;
An M in the top left corner
A T in the top right corner
A rabbit's head in the middle
An E in the bottom left corner
A D in the bottom right corner
Answer = Empty Headed (MT Head ED)

The player who gets the most right, wins.

The city of Whitewater has suffered a great tragedy; for reasons unknown the dead have risen up from their graves and seek to feed upon the living.  The city became overrun in a single night and the few remaining survivors now scavenge to survive during the day and barricade themselves safely away at night.

Players take control of a small group of three survivors who have managed to last the first few days of infection and even barricade a small building to hunker down for the night in.  During the day you will send out two teams to scavenge for weapons, materials to build defenses with, supplies and even other survivors in order to prepare for the night; when 'they' come.

However, things would be easier if other groups of scavengers were not also competing for the same resources as you!

This Mega Bloks Dominoes Build game challenges players to match cards with colors and animals to win block pieces. The first player to collect all four animal block pieces and build their zoo friend wins.

Oversized blocks and cards are perfect for little hands.
Details:
        Includes: 30 cards & 16 Mega Bloks
        Ages 3 years & up
        For 2 to 4 players

(website)

The object is to become the richest player or own a majority of 15 shares in at least three of the 10 newspapers in the game.

In his turn player choose one of a following action:

     Buys or sells shares to other players
     Buys shares in the bank to their value, that each log is represented by 15 shares increasing in value 10 000 (pr 1) 150 000 (for 15 °).
     Take tokens '+' & '-' depending on the number of shares it has. These chips will raise or lower profits of newspapers
     Place each token on a security that one has


and additionally player simultainously reveal the chips and adjusted earnings per share. Then each gets its profits or pays its losses.

It is repeated until one player reaches the goal.

boardgame: Dominant Species: The Card Game – Promo Card Set
description:
This is a six card promo set. Each card is an event that is thematically tied to one of the six animal classes represented in the base game. These cards can simply be shuffled in with the those of the base game at the start of play to add a bit of variety to the play experience.

The Promo Card Set includes 6 playable cards:
 • Apex Predator
• Intelligence
• Migration
• Poisonous
• Semi-Aquatic
• Speciation

(The promo set also includes a seventh "card" which is not intended for play, only for reference: it contains card clarifications and directions for use.)

description:
A target shooting game with some humour. Just look at the outlaw target, shooting his own foot.

Here are the complete rules:

WILD WEST SHOOTING GALLERY
Here we have four lawless characters from way out in the Wild West, at present in a gay mood. Little do they know that the Sheriff’s posse is hot on their trail.
Why not join the Sheriff and his men and try your skill at shooting these rascals down?
HOW TO PLAY
Stand the target in the slotted wooden blocks at the sides of the box, then place it at a distance of four to five feet from the players.
Hold gun in your right hand and insert the metal end of the dart into the muzzle, resting rubber head of dart against palm of left hand, press home dart until spring “clicks" into loaded position.
Play can be for a pre-arranged total score, the first player reaching such score being the winner, or for a certain number of rounds, the highest scorer being the winner, 2nd and 3rd highest runners-up.

By placing their tiles and moving tiles opposing players together form a square of 5x5 (2 or 3 players) or 7x7 (4 players). Within this square that moved over the part at the option of poses and movements of tiles, each aims to create rows of his color (an alignment with at least three tiles) and antagonize those of its opponents. But the player who occupies one of the tiles with the center of the square at the end of the game double its alignment points (2 players, it double its strongest alignment).

Ποιός Πού Πότε Γιατί? (Who Where When Why?) contains 3.600 questions with their answers, concerning history, geography, arts, animals/plants, sports and science.
The players roll the dice and move on the board, answer the questions and continue to move as long as they are correct. First to make it to the "exit" wins.


Περιέχει 3.600 ερωτήσεις μαζί με τις απαντήσεις τους γύρω από ιστορία, γεωγραφία, γράμματα και τέχνες, ζώα και φυτά, αθλητισμό, επιστήμες. Οι παίκτες παίζουν πάνω στο ταμπλό, απαντούν σε ερωτήσεις και τσεκάρουν τις απαντήσεις με σκοπό να φτάσουν πρώτοι στην έξοδο.

Party game.

Each guest receives a card with 20 lines from a saying I would think. They have to fill in the second line which must rhyme with the first line. The bizarre example given is;

"You may find, once you've got her for better or worse"
"That  ---------------"

Did you get it? Me neither. The actual winning line is;
"That the Grey Mare's the better horse"

Come on Delgado - Worse and Horse do not rhyme!

The guest getting the most right answers, wins.

In Mindstein, each player takes a turn as the reader, reading a card with five multiple choice questions. Where Mindstein differs from a run-of-the-mill trivia game is that each multiple choice question has three answers corresponding to three different letters; the correct answers for each question spell a five letter word that players compete to be the first to yell out.

If you get the five letter word first, you get to draw a letter from the draw pile; first to spell "S-M-A-R-T" wins!

From the publishers description: Who wouldnt own a really nice farm. With cow and horse, tractor, house and barn etc. Everyone can win these in this game. Guessing and luck with the dice will lead to victory. Who first gets a farm by dice rolls wins.

It is a roll and move game. Twelve cards for each player are shuffled and put on the board. Clockwise the players roll and move their pawns according the die roll. Sometimes they get to take a card, sometimes they must get rid of a card, and sometimes they challenge the other player. Challenging means putting the die in one hand and then the player guesses which hand. Correct guesses give the player a card, false guesses make him lose a card. The first player to have 7 (out of 12 per player) cards has a full farm and wins.

Board Game based on Athletic Disciplines

You will enjoy a real board game where athletic events are simulated on a track and field. Such as 100/200/400 metres, 110 metres hurdles, 4x100 metres, long, high and triple jump, and throwing events including: javelin, hammer discus and shot put.
The goal is to achieve the highest score in the different athletic disciplines that are considered before the event.

The board represents the field and running track. On the track disciplines are developed throws and jumps, and on the track racing. The players will use 2/5 dice of different colors, depending on how many of the same discipline to develop. Difficulties in the disciplines are taken into account with one of the dice to use. In each discipline each player (athlete) will move its piece on the board in the room or location that corresponds to the number thrown with the dice and thereby indicate the regulations in each discipline.

The player who collected the most points at the end of the disciplines developed will be the winner of the competition.

Thunderstone Avatars is a 25-card expansion – five copies of five different cards – that adds variable player powers to Thunderstone. The cards in detail are:


    Cleric


Village: Draw 1 card for each disease present.
Spoils: Discard 1 XP to destroy 1 card.


    Fighter


Weapons weigh 1 point less.
Dungeon: One weapon is considered equipped, even if you do not have a hero to equip it. You may use the abilities and traits of that weapon.


    Ranger


Dungeon: Switch the positions of 2 adjacent monsters in the hall.
Dungeon: Reveal the top card of the dungeon deck. You may attack this as if it were 1 rank deeper than the monster in the last rank.


    Thief


You can buy items and weapons for 1 fewer gold.
Dungeon: Discard 2 XP to have each other player discard a card.


    Wizard


Village: If you purchase a spell, draw an additional card when you draw a new hand.
Dungeon: If you have used a spell's ability this turn, discard 2 XP to gain Magic Attack +3.

description:
Face-Off Hockey is an abstract sports game that uses unique dice and a morale point system to simulate all the exciting action of hockey.

Easy to learn and teach, even if you've never watched or played the sport.

Face-Off Hockey uses 16 unique dice. Each player will select a set of 7 that include, 4 Forwards, 2 Defencemen, and a Goalie. The dice use a straight forward color scheme making them easy to identify, with glyphs that are universally recognisable.

Fight for puck possession and attempt to generate offence in order score goals and win the game.

The morale system simulates the highs and lows that your team will encounter throughout the course of a game. Use your morale boost to perform special actions which could lead to a turning point in the game!

Track scores, turns, period and any penalties with the cleverly designed play mat.

Face-Offs, Checking, turnovers, breakaways, odd man rushes, penalties, short handed goals, overtime, shootouts and more!

Rahan is a French comics series about an intelligent prehistoric man, that appeared first as part of Pif gadget, then published in albums of 2 to 4 complete stories. It was initially written by Roger Lecureux, and after his death in 1999, by his son, Jean-François Lecureux. Most of the artwork is drawn by Andre Cheret, as well as some other artists (Enrique Romero, Zam, DeHuescar)..
In 1986, Rahan was adapted into an animated series.

The Game :
Each player have a Prehistoric cave man wich moves with movement cards.
They must reach "Fire" or "Water" area and bring them back to the cave.
They shoot at wild animals with the "silex".
They try to meet Rahan to recover an invention from him.

boardgame: Wings of Glory: WW1 Airplane Packs
description:
Wings of Glory: WW1 Airplane Packs are single-plane blister packs with one 1/144 model plane, assembled and painted, and its deck of maneuver cards.

The first series includes Fokker E.III, Halberstadt D.III, Morane-Saulnier and Airco D.H.2, each one in three different color schemes.

New planes are available! The four famous fighters of the types first released as Wings of War miniatures have recently reached the stores. Now you can put the SPAD 13 and Albatros D-V through their paces, or restage the most famous competitions of the war with the Fokker Triplane versus the Sopwith Camel. In his turn, players must correctly answer questions to win the cards and to collect one of each family.

Exchange your gun for the Shotgun.

boardgame: Austria Stands Alone: The 1809 Campaign – Volley and Bayonet Scenario Book

Volley & Bayonet is the most practical way to fight the great campaigns of 1809. Find out if Napoleon will suffer his first defeat or even his last great victory!

description:
Description from the publisher:

"Global warming threatens Vanuatu. Many islands are in danger of disappearing under the sea due to rising waters. Players will therefore have to unite to protect the islands by building dikes. However, the interests of one are not necessarily the interests of others."

Vanuatu: The Rising Waters, the first extension for Vanuatu, adds an option (as part of the Build action) for players to build dikes to protect islands from rising waters. Water can reduce a player's points from houses, submerge an island completely, and even end the game early with no winner.

Similar to Voyage en France.

Instead of receiving all destination cards in start of the game player draws a next card if he reaches one of destinations.

The players have to move along the board by throwing a die. 
First player who has visited 5 destination cities and arrives back in the Paris wins the game.

Kubbuteo is an indoor version of popular garden game, Kubb. Players take turns flicking 6 disks the length of a 4’ by 2’ board, trying to knock down their opponent’s Kubbs [upright disks]. The opponent flicks back the downed Kubbs and then have their turn at flicking the 6 disks at the Kubbs. Once a player has knocked down all Kubbs in the opponent’s half, they may attempt to knock down the centrally located King and thus win the game – but doing this beforehand will result in losing the game, so care must be taken.
 Kubbuteo can be played by people of all ages and appeals to players of Crokinole and Carrom, requiring the same dexterity skills. Tactically it is identical to parent game Kubb, deciding where to return downed Kubbs to.

Object of the game is to obtain five budgets, four departments and one agency speaker's chair.

Players roll 2 dice moving on the game board in the "the budget route."
When you have your five budgets, You go to the circular route to win the "departments within your agency."
When you have four departments, simply drop the box "Armchair President" to win the game. Whether it budgets or departments, it is necessary to earn them by answering a questions.

User summary

A Japanese home-published tile/card game. The game starts with a setup of face down square tiles that have power lines on their face up side. Players choose one of the edge positions as their "exit" to empower, and take turns either turning up, rotating or echanging one of the earth tiles, with the goal of getting as many connected tiles as possible to their "exit". Once the exit has a set number of connected earth tiles, it's Sealed, and the first player to Seal four exits wins.

Players can also play cards from their hand for further effects.

description:
The game includes six dice whose faces are one of the letters of the name of the game in different colors.

The goal is to achieve more winning combinations after 1 or more shots.

There are 10 possible combinations ranging from 50-5000 points.

The first run must include the 6 dice you put aside the combinations you want to keep and raise the other dice. If no any combinations appear, You lose everything!

Each new launch may double the sum if it is succesful.

The winner is the first to total 5000 points.

Double Take is a charades game built for two, so to speak. Each round, time willing, two players will present clues for a half-dozen familiar phrases that all have something in common. A sample category, for example, is "Something's Wrong" with the words to be guessed being divided as:


    Play | Foul
    In the Closet | Skeleton
    Sheep | Black
    Gun | Smoking


Each clue giver acts out one side of the card, and since they're facing the guessers, the phrases will be acted out in left-to-right fashion ("Black Sheep", "Smoking Gun", etc.) If someone guesses one side of the card, that player scores – but the clue givers score only after both sides of a phrase have been guessed, so they need to work together – but separately – to make their clues clear.

boardgame: Un fin de semana en Madrid
description:
The game board shows a map of Madrid. Players roll a die and move through the city, trying to reach the famous sites (especially those two whose cards they drew at the beginning and which are marked). Other means of transportation can be used to get around more quickly, but they have to be paid for in travel chips. When a player reaches a site, s/he is asked a question. If answering correctly, the site card can be discarded and a new travel chip is drawn. When reaching an unmarked site, they can earn a new site card (if that belongs to someone else, it can be "stolen").
When a player can reach both sites they drew at the beginning and gain two more site cards and return to their starting location, s/he wins the game.

Questions come in two difficulty levels, as the game is designed for language learning. So the difficulty does not refer to the knowledge needed to answer the question, but to the Spanish speaking ability.

Hasbro describes it as:

"Go live! In this fun version of the classic MONOPOLY game, the electronic talking tower calls the plays! With motion sensing, sound effects and even surprise mini games, the tower guides the game from start to finish while you wheel, deal and call the shots! Slide the cab around the track to take a shortcut, play a quick game and then manage your cash at the ATM. With so many things to do, the “real estate market” is more thrilling than ever!

Includes electronic talking tower, gameboard, 4 game pieces, 4 bank cards, 4 card slots, cab and track, title deed cards, 32 houses, 12 hotels, 2 “green” pieces and startup guide."

Played with an IR tower containing a camera and reflective pieces and option spaces it seem to remove the need for book keeping, dice rolling, cards. And there's no money either. Kinda like a video game version just in real life.

Versability 'the poetry game' is a parlour game inspired by the Dictionary Game, where players complete a verse of poetry with missing line(s).  The players' attempts and the genuine version are read aloud and then the players vote on which is the genuine verse.

Rather than using cards, pieces and a board, Versability is published as a book.  Players need to provide paper and writing implements.  Randomness is achieved by closing one's eyes and pointing at a page of options.

boardgame: A Game of Thrones: The Card Game – Valar Dohaeris
description:
Game description from the publisher:

Valar Dohaeris is the second Chapter Pack in the "Beyond the Narrow Sea" cycle for A Game of Thrones: The Card Game. It introduces cards and strategies meant to welcome new players to the game directly after the Core Set, as well as challenge the traditional thinking of veterans immured in the game’s shifting balances of power.

Meanwhile, as the Great Houses continue to expand their influence into Essos, Valar Dohaeris brings more of the Eastern continent's flavor. Two unique locations encourage decks that unite characters from rival houses, players continue their journey down the Rhoyne with four flowing River plots, and a host of new personalities arrive, ready to serve. Knights, Fools, 
Allies, and Lords – all must show obedience and play their roles in the myriad battles and intrigues launched between rival houses as they seek to claim the Iron Throne.

description: A roll & move game for children in kindergarten, meant to educate them about the bad effects of bullying. Players roll a die and move their pawn. If they land on certain space they must draw a card. Players can choose whether to draw from the Friendship deck (moving them forward a few spaces) or the Bullying deck (moving them backwards a few spaces). The first player to reach space number 80 wins.

Shepherds is a 2 players game (with 4 players variant) in which players try to catch 3 (of 9) sheep. They try to do it with one... dog. The first who do it, is a winner.

Each player has a set of cards. Players (alternately) play a card and move the dog to try herding the sheep into their pens. Cards have values from 1 to 5, which represent exactly how many spaces the dog must move.

The used card is discarded. If a player runs out of cards, all of that player's discarded cards return to their hand. At the end of the dog's movement, the player places a pen in his color on the dog's final square.

Players take turns one after the other in this way until the game ends.

In Speak Up!, everyone plays in one of five languages – German, English, French, Spanish or Italian – and not knowing the language won't necessarily be a detriment.

To set up the game, lay out 49 of the fifty cards on the table with the picture side faceup; next to the picture is the name of the item in five languages. The back of each card features the name of some other item in five languages.

To start play, take the card set aside during set-up and read one of the words on the back, say "Markt" (the German word for "market"). Everyone then races to find the card depicting the market, which will also have the various words for "market" next to the picture. Whoever finds it keeps the card, then flips it over and reads the German word on the back. This process continues until all the cards have been claimed. Whoever has the most cards wins!

boardgame: Legacy: Gears of Time – Kickstarter Backer Promo Card
description:
The Kickstarter Technology Card is an exclusive promo card for Legacy: Gears of Time given to people who backed the project on Kickstarter.  This technology card is added to the deck and can be played like any other technology, provided the prerequisites to do so are met:


     The Internet 6
         Radio
             Electricity
        
         Logic
         Printing Press
             Writing
        
    
     Currency 4
         Writing
         Mining
             Basic Tools

The Mini-Expansion for Legacy: Gears of Time is a set of 5 unique Technology cards (10 Cards total):


     Art (3)
     Antibiotics (2)
     Banking (1)
     Satellites (2)
     Universities (2)


These Technologies build off the core set of technologies, but introduce a shift in balance to the existing technology tree Satellites and Antibiotics make the communication and medical branches even stronger contenders over the transportation branch, more choices and more challenges! Banking has a dependency on Law, giving you a fantastic reason to go out of your way to keep influencing the previously terminal technology. Universities depends on the new Fundamental Technology Art and has 0 discard cost. Be sure to have some Influence Cubes ready, the reward is worth the effort!

Note: There is a misprint on Antibiotics. The reminder of the reward for Hospital should be 5, not 6.

description:
Dice Dodge is a dice game related to those in the jeopardy family, which includes Pig and Farkle.  However, instead of the choices being "keep rolling" or "stop", one must choose whether to roll along a column, row, or across the entire board in order to maximize their chances of winning.

Gameplay involves tossing two dice and marking the space on the board corresponding to the row and column rolled.  The player then decides whether to roll one or both dice again in order to place more markers on the board.  The point value of a row or column is equal to the number of markers on it, squared.  If a player rolls a space that has already been marked, their turn ends and their score is tallied.  The winner of the game is the player with the most points after a set number of rounds.

To win Claim to Fame, you need to be the first team to move from start to finish by getting your team members to yell out five facts about celebrities and other famous people. Each round you have sixty seconds to deliver verbal hints, act out charades, draw clues – or all three – to aide teammates in guessing the facts listed on the back of your celebrity card.

Claim to Fame Travel Edition includes celebrity cards that can be used in the base game, while also being playable as a game on their own without the other components.
family: Versions & Editions: Travel Versions of Non-Travel Games

boardgame: Killer Bunnies and the Conquest of the Magic Carrot: Violet Booster Deck
description:
Game description from the publisher:

Killer Bunnies return to pillage and burn with the addition of Specialty Bunny cards such as the Priscillan trio that allows players to play extra cards during their turn! Killer Bunnies and the Conquest of the Magic Carrot: Violet Booster Deck adds 55 new cards to the existing Killer Bunnies and the Conquest of the Magic Carrot set, as well as a clear 20-sided die.

New cards include: "Reno 911" which causes a police raid on your opponent's cards, "Campaign Contributions" for "influencing" (wink, wink) the votes of Senators, "Betty Botter" for increasing your Radish and Milk supplies and the "Grand Slam" which, combined with lucky die rolls, awards a player with three Carrots! The new Defense Cards can eliminate threatening weapons such as "Blipverts" or the ever-vengeful "Heathers"!

Instead of forming the words with letters players forms sentences with the words randomly drawn from the bag.
There are also some joker-cards what can be used to be an any word.

Time is chosen by the participants and everybody starts to forming the sentences.

If time is over the scoring begins.
Each word (including the joker) earns you two points.
For each word taken from the bonus column: 1
If the player winning the bid is the current player: 3
If you have not used the joker: 6
If you used the 12 cards: 6

At the end of the game, whoever has the highest number of points wins.

From Their Website:
Master & Commander: Close Quarters is a set of rules designed for playing boarding actions between Napoleonic ships. Now you can play in detail such famous actions as ‘Nelson’s Patent Bridge for Boarding First-Rates’ in the battle of Saint Vincent, follow Captain Decatur in a boarding action against Barbary Corsairs, or board the Royal Navy ships with the San Juan Neponucemo at Trafalgar

Fast, innovative and simple playing system. Designed to play with our ship boards, and CAPITAN MINIATURES 18mm crews,all you need to play is downloable for free.

The Mighty Hood II is a two-player game that simulates the main World War II battles involving HMS Hood, pride of the Royal Navy. Players maneuver their ships on the tabletop (or floor) and try and defeat the enemy through combat. The standard game covers the Battle of Denmark Strait in May, 1941, pitting Hood and Prince of Wales against the German Bismarck and Prinz Eugen. Another scenario covers the Battle of Mers-el-Kebir in July 1940, when British forces led by Hood neutralized the Vichy French fleet along the coast of North Africa.

The game is a heavily-revised version of The Mighty Hood, which appeared in Panzerschreck magazine #14. The new game provides new ship ratings, introduces new rules and modifications congruent with the Battleship Captain standard game system, and adds ships allowing gamers to recreate the Mers-el-Kebir scenario.

Battleship Captain is a "miniatures" game system with scenarios played on the tabletop or floor, using the rules and ship counters provided. Scale is 1"=1000 yards. The game emphasizes playability, as players maneuver their ships and try and defeat the enemy fleet.

The game also requires a tape measure or ruler in inches, paper and pencil, and a six-sided die.

boardgame: Dragon Valley: Hunt for the Fugitive
description:
Hunt for the Fugitive is a BGG Store Promo card that expands Dragon Valley: Rise for Corundia.

Its effect is:

"Choose One: 

     Move one Friendly or Enemy Unit in your Barony one or two spaces.
     Place a purple cube in a built Dungeon in your Barony
     Remove all purple cubes in a built Dungeon in your Barony.  Score 1, 3, 6, 10 for removing 1,2,3,4 cubes respectively."


It can be added to the King's Aid deck during game setup.  It is divided and chosen in the same fashion as other King's Aid cards.

The goal in this card game is to climb up the career ladder to make as much money as possible. Each player is randomly assigned a profession at the start of the game; then a trick taking game is played in which each player holds six hidden cards plus some (depending on the number of players) cards which are drawn openly. The rank of the profession determines how players may talk to each others, mistakes are punished by penalty cards. After finishing a round, a player is demoted if s/he has more cards of the profession below him/her plus penalty cards. Promotions are then resolved as a result of demotions, and everybody gets their salary. The first player to own 100000 Euros is the winner.

These mini-games can be used as filler games or during a game of Dragon Valley: Rise of Corundia of 3 or 4 players while two players are waiting for the division of King's Aid. Purple cubes and/or Unit cubes from the Unit bag are used to keep track of game play aspects in Dragon Valley Tourneys.

Players start with Jousting. If finished before the other player(s) have completed the dividing in Dragon Valley: Rise of Corundia, they move on to Archery. If there is still time, they move to the Trebuchet Tourney and afterwards back again to Jousting. If the dividing is complete, finish any score checks but do not continue. If a scoring criteria was not met, no player gains score in Dragon Valley: Rise of Corundia from the current tourney. They do keep the score earned in previous tourneys during this division. Make sure to return any purple cubes to the board and units back into the cloth bag. The next division starts with the next mini-game.

If players are not comfortable playing traditional Rock Parchment Swords, an alternate is to pull Blue, Red and Yellow cubes from the bag and secretly selecting one (if enough are there, otherwise use cubes from the board, but remember where they came from.)

“Frigates!" cried Nelson,"Were I to die this moment, want of frigates, would be found engraved on my heart" (Nelson said these words when he was chasing round the Mediterranean in search of the French expedition to Egypt)

Attached to a fleet, the frigates were the eyes and ears, ranging in line abreast over a immense expanse of sea while battleships kept steadily on line ahead, repeating signals and carrying dispatches.

Detached either singly or in small squadrons the frigates might be sent anywhere in the world to attack enemy commerce and protect their own, to hunt down privateers or pirates, or to take part in the conquest of yet another colony.

In the British Admiralty the Frigates were rated 5th rate, and small frigates 6th rate, the other small vessels used to support the operations of the frigates were classified as unrated, and they include a variety of ships from the larger corvettes  and sloops of war to the small cutters and schooners. We include too, some 4th rates two deckers vessels, that, in some cases were used to execute and support frigate duties despite they were slow and cumbersome an many of them out-dated.

All this operations and engagements are covered by our rules Master & Commander Frigates. We have included  48 British, 48 Spanish, 48 French, 48 US, 40 Dutch & 24 Danish ships, including Two deckers, Frigates, Rasées, Sloops of War, Brigs of War, Schooners, & Cutters. The years covered with the ships are from the American War of Independence, French Revolutionary Wars, Napoleonic Wars, & 1812 War.

To play Frigates you need the Master & Commander Rules, Turning Templates, and Sailing Tables from Capitan Games.  Along with these you will need the optional rule for Beating.  All of these can be downloaded in pdf format (A4 paper size) from the Capitan Games website.

Collectible card game set in a future where skateboarding is illegal. The first two runs of the cards are called Vegalas and Socalia. The cards feature real art from Birdhouse skateboards and cartoon versions of two Birdhouse skateboarders. Each player creates a deck as in most collectable card games. You will play places to skate, money, equipment and people who will help you. There are also skateboard tricks that can be linked in sequence to earn "stoke" points. Earn 360 points and you win, if you drop to zero game over.

In the spring of 1893, a torrential season of constant downpour and flash floods exposed gold-bearing quartz in a large gully in the Goldfield Mining District in the small mining town of Goldfield, Arizona. $25,000 worth of gold began washing down the street that ran through Goldfield. The mad dash was on to collect the gold before it was gone!

In Goldfield Gully players press their luck to uncover as much gold as they possibly can before the ever-watchful eyes of the local Marshal catches them in the act. Players must choose which buildings in the town of Goldfield they will dig for gold in front of, then hide from the Marshal. Every round the Marshal visits one building, forcing the player in that building to give up his entire treasure. Players may also forgo their turn to bank their treasure by giving up half to the Marshal and "banking" the other half. The player with the most gold at the end of ten rounds wins!

The object of the game is to mark the most points by forming "hands" of poker.

Everyone draws 5 cards and in his turn, arises from one to five of his cards on the board, to complement the "hands" of poker, interconnected as crosswords.

At the end of the game the scoring begins.
Player scores 2 to 42 points for poker combinations:
2 points per pair to 42 points per Royal Flush.

The Jade Emperor

In the Jade Emperor players to take the Chinese zodiac signs to play.

The purpose of to travel the game board with zodiac signs in order to become the first to bring the final square your chips on the board.

The game board boxes contain divert to other players boxes and if you find another sign of the zodiac enemy in the same square, occupying the box the player must start again from scratch.

The first player to wear their zodiac three chips tabs at the end of the course wins the game.


El Emperador de Jade es un juego de estrategia sobre un tablero que refleja un año lunar chino y sus correspondientes estaciones anuales.
He diseñado un sistema de movimiento que no depende de ningún dado para ello, y si los jugadores lo desean tampoco serán necesarios los dados para los eventos, pudiendo utilizar ciertas cartas como sustitutos, pero si además quieren jugar una partida más rápida en tiempo, pueden usar un sistema mixto que acelera la mecánica del mismo reduciendo el tiempo a la mitad.
El tablero es algo más grande que el de un ajedrez 12×10 y aunque pueda parecer que en un principio es un juego de recorrido, este juego tiene un alto grado de estrategia y planificación, de forma que los jugadores deberán tener en cuenta como juegan con sus fichas y los posibles movimientos que más les interese realizar.
Los eventos son muy simples pero con un alto grado de implicación en el juego y como interactúen con ellos puede suponer una ventaja tanto como una desventaja a la hora de mover sus fichas, pero que nadie crea que se puede quedar esperando a que llegue una mejor oportunidad, por que otros signos del zodiaco podrán capturarles y dar al traste con los planes del más oportunista.
Para el control de azar los jugadores pueden disponer de dados o de cartas y los recursos que deben obtener los jugadores son limitados, pero el sistema también atiende a los casos que se pudieran producir vacíos de elementos y los puede volver a retornar al juego, aunque ciertas fichas de los jugadores pueden ser retiradas del tablero de juego no es un juego por eliminación y desde el primero hasta el ultimo dispondrán de las mismas oportunidades.
Otro tema que me parece interesante destacar es el turno en el que jugaran los diferentes participantes, en este aspecto no es un juego lineal y excepto en la primera ronda, los jugadores moverán sus fichas por el orden descendente a la posición en el tablero, de esta forma los jugadores se alternan pudiendo mover diferentes veces en cada ronda de juego según tengan posicionadas sus fichas, de esta forma si hay muchos jugadores nadie se aburrirá esperando a que le toque su turno.
Por ultimo otro tema que me parece se debe destacar es el de los componentes, todo el juego completo incluido el tablero son tres hojas de A4, y en realidad con el tablero y la Rueda zodiacal el juego tendría la base mínima de componentes, con unas fichas de colores del parchís, un dado de ocho, y uno de seis podrías jugar sin tener que imprimir nada más.
No obstante he incluido el juego al completo, el juego básico son tres hojas, y una hoja más para las cartas, para quien desee imprimirlas, las realice en formato pequeño para imprimir en papel adhesivo y pegarlas en cartas de pequeño formato,  baratas y fáciles de conseguir en “todo a 100″, todas las cartas entran en un A4.
Espero que disfrutéis jugando tanto como yo diseñándolo.
A imprimir y jugar un saludo.

You need to get photos of the world to accomplish a story by answering questions.
