When a player goes out, all other players have one final chance to play any cards from hand that they can before assessing the score of cards left in hand.

Players play their own melds to the table in front of them and can extend those in any combination as part of playing hands to the table (usually this is done as a final turn to shed cards in hand). E.g. in the round of 9 cards, if you'd earlier played a 3 of a kind and a run of 3, you could later on play another 3 of a kind to finish, OR you could for example put 1 card on the 3 of a kind to make it a 4 of a kind, and another 2 cards on the run to make it a 5 card run.

The rules for playing cards out of hand are as follows:

Round, Cards in hand to start, wild cards, allowed plays
1,3,A+3+(Joker), 3 of a kind (3K), or 3 card run flush (3R)
2,4,A+4+(Joker), 4K or 4R
3,5,A+5+(Joker), 5K or 5R
4,6,A+6+(Joker), 6K or 6R or mix of 3R, 3K
5,7,A+7+(Joker), 7K or 7R, or a mix of 3R,3K,4R,4K
6,8,A+8+(Joker), 8K or 8R, or a mix of 3,4,5 card run or set
7,9,A+9+(Joker), 9K or 9R, or a mix of 3,4,5,6 card run or set
8,10,A+10+(Joker), 10K or 10R, or mix of 3,4,5,6,7 card hands
9,11,A+J+(Joker), 11K or 11R, or mix of 3,4,5,6,7,8 card hands
10,12,A+Q+(Joker), 10K or 10R, or mix of 3,4,5,6,7,9 card hands
11,13,A+K+(Joker), 10K or 10R, or mix of 3,4,5,6,7,8,9,10 card hands

boardgame: Welt der Hansestadt Hamburg das Quiz
description:
Objective: score most winning points knowing correct answers about Hamburg.

With this quiz game the players will learn (with 220 questions) some detailed information about the city of Hamburg. From the box: 'With this game another lifestyle trend arrives now in the world of board games'. 
2 different playing versions:
Version 1: active player draws a card and reads the question & 4 possible answers (of which 1, 2 or 3 may be right); now each player may invest gradually chips to be placed betting on different answers; correct answer(s) is(are) printed on the back of the card;
Version 2: find out the proper sequence of three answers regarding posted question

Objective: Know correct answers on questions out of the former GDR
 - acting by turn - 
A small trivia game about GDR; with 100 questions and answers. The game itself is like a notebook (with spiral binding). Each page contains 2 questions and 4 answers, the back side contains the correct answers with additional information.
Questions like: What is a 'Gummifuffziger'? How many disticts did GDR possess?

Objective: know most of very generic questions

A small trivia game about knowlegde; with 100 questions and answers. The game itself is like a notebook (with spiral binding). Each page contains 2 questions and 4 nswers, the back side contains the correct answers with additional information.
Questions like:
Speed of light? When did the basic law of Germany  become valid?

This is a promotional edition of the classic trivia game Trivial Pursuit, published by an Austrian newspaper to celebrate its hundredth birthday.

The game contains 250 trivia cards with 6 questions each from the following categories:
Blue: Politics
Pink: Foreign Countries
Yellow: Local News
Brown: Local Scene
Green: Sports
Orange: Editorial Department (specific questions about the newspaper)

Every player (or team) gets a circular playing piece with six pie-shaped holes (standard Trivial Pursuit issue). There is no game board. A special six-sided die showing the six colors mentioned above is rolled by the player on turn. The color tells from which category the question comes.

If the player on turn can answer the question, he gets another turn and, if he does not has it already, the corresponding colored pie piece which he puts in his playing piece. If he answers wrong, it's the next player's turn.

The first player to collect all colors wins the game.

The set is fully compatible with every other Trivial Pursuit game.

Challenge your family to games based on the hit ITV gameshow The Cube . The show's nail-biting format has been cleverly downsized for this fun family game that retains all the best elements of the series. There are seven rounds of physical and electronic challenges to complete and players have just nine lives to use up before being booted out of the game. There are options to do a Trial Run or Simplify the challenges, just like in the show, and with 50 different games included, The Cube Game is sure to keep the whole family entertained. Designed for two to six players aged eight and up, the The Cube Game is great for fans of the TV show but just as fun for those who are new to the format. Features: Family game based on the ITV gameshow Complete seven rounds of games to win 50 different games to complete Use your nine lives, Trial Run and Simplify tokens For 2 to 6 players, aged 8 and up

A discussion-based, roll and move board game,with an optional role-playing element. It can be played competitively or with elements of co-operation. Each player starts at home, is born in a different position in life, has certain life-long qualities, and is faced with certain events and choices. The aim is to go home, and the game ends when one or more players have completed the journey and acquired the necessary number of tokens.The game is different each time it is played - depending on the players and the life events that crop up.

There are two journeys:


     Through age: by moving through the quadrants of the board representing childhood, youth, prime and vintage, each with a gateway into the next.
     Towards home: by accumulating tokens of particular colours, representing the different strands of home: shelter, family, community, environment and inner peace.


When one player goes home, s/he is able to help others do so with any extra tokens. At the end of the game, each player may tell the story of his/her character.

The game is based on the book of the same name (formerly The O of Home) by Jennifer Kavanagh. A significant percentage of the profits will be going to a national charity for homeless people.

All materials from sustainable sources, or recyclable.

An expansion for the Trivial Pursuit Family Edition game.

The box contains 200 yellow cards with easy questions and 200 blue cards with harder questions.

You're about to embark on the greatest adventure of your life.  You will have the chance to become one of six of the most unbelievable characters you will ever meet.  You become the owner of a highly technical fleet of trucks, with a dealer network worldwide.  Now you will find out if you have what it takes to be a real..."Gear Jammer".  A gear jammer is a trucker/owner that is prepared to take the chance....challenge and risk to become the most successful on the board game of...Gear Jammers!

Objective:  To see who can run the most successful trucking business...based on a combination of companies owned, trucks on the road and cash on hand.  To do this, you will want to buy all the trucks you can buy, invest in companies that are going places, and hope you won't get stuck with too many accidents, losses or heavy costs.  
What makes Gear Jammers really fun are all the chances you  take in hopes of collecting some profits.  You may run into a truck stop that means you miss a couple of turns!  We say "Go for the profits anyway!".  If you own a fleet of trucks (and the value cards in your favour) you could be the ultimate winner of the game.  But don't count on a thing until the timer goes off.  Just when you think you've got the game in the bag, your old trucking buddy may land on a "Price and Profit!" card - and turn the whole game around.

Start your engines....roll out your trucks and begin driving on the highway to success....if you dare!

boardgame: Battle of 4 Armies Expansion Kit: The Battle is Joined
description:
From the quick paced, deep decision rich, abstract strategy BattlessonTM game, Battle of 4 Armies, it's expansion kit has finally been brought to the fantastical battlefield. In the Battle of 4 Armies expansion kit: The Battle is Joined, a new Falcon Army spreads its wings and adds another element to the game. Each Hero (including the new army of flying units) now has a set of abilities that are both bold and cunning, giving you that extra chance to be victorious.

How will you sieze the Riverport of Chip? Find out with the Battle of 4 Armies expansion kit: The Battle is Joined!

Note, the Battle of 4 Armies Expansion Kit is not a ‘stand alone’ game. You must own a copy of Battle of 4 Armies to use this expansion kit.

Game Data:

Complexity: 1.5 on a 9 scale
Solitaire Suitability: 8.5 on a 9 scale
Scale: Each unit represents a single troop of fantasy warriors.

Game Components:

•    One 1-page, color instructions sheet
•    5 Hero cards
•    10 color, die-cut 1” mounted game pieces

A commercial game for 'Deutsche BP AG'. Produced by Otto Maier Verlag, Ravensburg.

Each player draws some cards which determine the tiles they have to reach on the modular labyrinth-like board. Once a player has reached all the symbols stated on his cards he has to hurry back to his starting tile in order to win the game. However, the layout of the board is going to change through the course of the game by exchanging or switching/turning tiles - which of these actions take place is determined by the player in turn using a wheel as random generator.

A desktop bowling set. Wooden base and pins, with a wooden ramp for launching the metal bowling ball which can be pivoted to aim shots. The base has a metal backstop. Scoring and pin setup must be done manually for each frame.

Objective: score most winning points knowing correct answers about Berlin.

With this quiz game the players will learn (with 220 questions) some detailed information about the city of Berlin. 
2 different playing versions:
Version 1: active player draws a card and reads the question & 4 possible answers (of which 1, 2 or 3 may be right); now each player may invest gradually chips to be placed betting on different answers; correct answer(s) is(are) printed on the back of the card;
Version 2: find out the proper sequence of three answers regarding posted question

The playing-field of Sqez is a rectilinear array of dots, of any convenient size — but arrays of less than 8 x 8 are too small, and exceptionally large fields may prolong the game needlessly, unless there are many players. The easiest way to make the arrays is to type up rows of full-stops on a typewriter; but it's just possible that somewhere or other you can buy dotted paper (wallpaper?), and it wouldn't be hard to get used to playing on small-squared graph paper, counting each inter¬section as a 'dot'. But I leave this to your ingenuity.
Each player in Sqez must complete a rectangle (oblong or square) by drawing a line through the dots. The line drawn must not anywhere duplicate an already placed line, though it may cross another line, or meet it at one end; and the rectangle drawn must enclose at least one 'free' dot — a free dot being defined as a dot that has no line passing through it or originating at it. The last player to complete a legal move is the winner.
Those are the entire rules, except to say that a player who places a line which he cannot (owing to miscalculation) use to make a legal move (by completing a rectangle as defined) forfeits the game.

Objective: Having the highest number of points after a certain number of question cards have been played (number to be decided by the players at the beginning of a game). Points are earned by correctly answering questions.

Trivia game with more than 200 questions about movies. Players choose for each question how many points they want to make and how risky they are willing to bet.

Gameplay: The starting player takes a card from the card stack, chooses a letter and reads the corresponding question and its answers. There are two types of questions:

- Questions with one to three correct answers: For each question, there are four answers. One up to three of them are correct, at least one is wrong. When all the answers have been read, the players place their chip(s) on the betting boards for the answer(s) that they think is/are correct. Each player (including the one who read the question) can place up to three chips. Players who put their chips on one or more wrong answers don't get any points this round. Players with exactly one correct answer get to advance their pawn one space, 2 correct answers means advancing 2 spaces, 3 correct answers 3 spaces.

Ausgerechnet Köln uses the same game play as the other Ausgerechnet! titles: Players deal three piles of 15 location cards from a deck of roughly 200 cards (naming locations related to Cologne for this edition), then place an interlude card between each set of piles to make a deck of 45 cards. One location card is placed on the direction board, which has arrows pointing north, south, east and west.

On a turn, a player draws a location card – keeping the latitude and longitude on the back of the card secret from all players – then places the card next to the direction board, indicating how she thinks this location relates to the starting one. Is 'Nippes' west or east of Bonn? Why sure, so place it by the west arrow. Location cards can be placed between cards already on the board.

After each location card is placed, any player can challenge the positioning of that card. That player reveals the back of the most recently placed card as well as one adjacent card. If the challenge was correct – and the card placed wrongly – the challenger wins a point chip from the placer and the card is removed; otherwise the challenger hands over a chip to that person.

After 15 cards are placed, the interlude card signals an interim scoring in which all players secretly guess how many cards have been placed correctly. Guess correctly, and you score two chips; if no one is spot on, the closest guesser wins a chip.

After three rounds and the final interlude scoring, the player with the most chips wins.

Aliens, UFOs – are they real? After years of research, Ufologists have answered this question, and the answer is...maybe. This is where you come in. Gather evidence, get your Doctorate, hack your opponent's computer, then – perhaps – you will solve one of the World's greatest mysteries: Are we alone in the universe?

Ufology is a card game based on the fringe scientific field of the same name. In Ufology, players use Research cards to collect Evidence cards from a 3x3 card grid (3x4 in 5-6 player games) called the Field of Research. Those Evidence cards, ranging in value from 0 to 6 points, are placed with Case cards that have corresponding icons. When a Case has the indicated pieces of evidence, it is considered Closed. The game ends as soon as a player closes three Cases, and the player with the most points wins.

Action cards allow players to, for instance, take cards from an opponents hands, move cards from Case to another, etc. There are also Credentials cards that increase the number of cards a player may use on there turn or their hand size, and Culture cards which change the game play for all players.

In Lands Unknown you control a group of bold adventurers who embark on a journey into an unexplored continent. They will have to plan their advance carefully and use the terrain to their advantage if they are to best rivaling groups for control of the continent.

Each turn, a player plays a land card onto one of the coastline roads already started, marks it (if necessary), then uses the power shown on that (drawing new cards, swapping hands with an opponent, moving tokens, destroying cards or tokens, and so on). At the end of the game, players score points for their explorer tokens on the roads, and the player with the most points wins.

"The New Game of Table Football".

Fairly basic game of finger-flicking dexterity. The box illustrates it rather well.

The board is anything you can find to act as a board, maybe an old blanket. The wire supports fasten the goals and the blanket to the table. Up to 4 can play. With your finger, you literally flick the ball, hopefully goalwards. There are no player figures so I expect the game got quite frenetic, especially with four players.

The most goals wins.

The board is wooden (made of exotic hardwood), into which there are 21 half-inch diameter holes. Basically the positional shape of the holes is a five times five grid with corners removed:


5 ooo
4ooooo
3ooooo
2ooooo
1 ooo
 ABCDE


The dark player has seven dark shafted game pieces to control. The light player has seven light shafted game pieces to control. Each game piece is formed as a stick which will fit nicely into any of the holes. Each piece has coloured caps at the end in six combinations:

1.) yellow / orange
2.) orange / red
3.) red / purple
4.) purple / blue
5.) blue / green
6.) green / yellow

Game rules give the hint that these form a circle of colours. This information might help and can be used to find logical game strategies here.

The seventh piece being special is

7.) white / black

Colour white is used being a 'wild' (joker). It can be used as a legal replacement for any colour mentioned before.
Black is basically a blocker not representing any colour.

Players take alternating turns. When a game piece is placed in a hole one of the coloured end caps is obviously not visible anymore. Placing a game piece the non visible colour must be announced loudly and clearly enough to the opponent while setting the piece.

On his first turn a player must place one of his pieces into any free hole. After the first turn a turn consists of one of the following actions:

1.) Play another own offboard piece into another free hole, or
2.) Turn any one of your already played pieces upside down in the same hole (announcing the non visible colour towards the opponent clearly and loudly), or
3.) Move any one of your played pieces to another hole (but not turning it upside down)

The object is to get three of the same colour in a row (horizontally, vertically, or diagonally) and the first player to do so wins. Since each player's set contains only two caps of each colour (as listed above), at least one of the three-in-a-row might has to be an opponent's piece.

The 'wild' game piece can only be played as soon as all other six pieces are already set before. This way you can build and win creating a three-in-a-row using own and/or opponent's white game piece as well. The black colour can not be used in a three-in-a-row constellation since it is used as a blocking piece only.

In general each player can only move game pieces with a shaft of own colour. But any game piece can be used to form the three-in-a-row to win.

Initially you draw by lot who goes first. In consecutive rounds the previous loser takes the first turn.

You might find similarities in game play like in Quarto (published in 1991, using opponent's pieces as well to form the winning row) and Memory (published in 1959, remembering the hidden colours is definitively helpful).

In contrast to Tau the newer Calypso does not include the aspect of a Memory game any longer. The colour which is not visible in Tau (and has to be announced each time) is still visible in Calypso due to the other construction of the game pieces.

This edition aims at elder players and offers questions from the following categories:

Blue: Sport & Leisure
Pink: Health & Diet
Yellow: Travel & Regeneration
Brown: Personalities & Celebrities
Green: History & Politics
Orange: Culture

The rules follow the standard Trivial Pursuit games:

Each player has a circular playing piece with six pie-shaped holes. The goal of the game is to collect a pie in each color. The colors correspond to different question categories.

The board consists of a circular track with spaces in seven different colors. Six of the colors correspond to question categories while the last color gives a new dice roll. Six spaces along the track are "pie spaces", and from these there are "spokes" of track leading to the middle of the board.

Players roll a die and move along the track in any direction they like. When a player stops on a color they get a question of the appropriate category. If the player answers a question correctly while on a pie space, they get a pie of that color (assuming they don't already have it). A correct answer on another square allows the player to roll again.

Once the player has one pie in each color, she can move along the spokes to the middle of the board to win the game.

High noon.

The dirt crunches underneath your boots.

Sweat drips slowly down your brow.

Your fingers twitch as you reach for your gun.

A split second later your opponent lies on the ground, lifeless.

You advance to the final round of the Dodgecity Gunfight contest.

Welcome to Wild Guns! The first gunslinger card game for two or more players.

Out smart, out shoot and out play your opponent in this fast-paced, high action card game.

Check out this game review from Father Geek!

Playing Wild Guns:
Each player acts as a Wild West Gunslinger, with a Gunslinger character card placed face up in front of him, each with unique skills and abilities.

The game play of Wild Guns is quite simple. Each player has a hand of Gun cards and Run cards. In a two player game each player starts with six Gun cards and six Run cards. During the game the players place one Gun card and one Run card facedown in front of him. The other player does the same. When both players have their cards facedown on the table the player with First Shot reveals his Gun card, while is opponent reveals his Run card. Then compare the cards to ensure a shot can be made. If player A reveals a Head Shot Gun Card and Player B Reveals a Crouch Run Card - the attack from player A cannot be made, the Run card avoids his shot. The play proceeds to player B as he reveals his Gun card to compare to player A's Run card. If player B reveals a Chest Shot Gun Card and player A reveals a Jump Run Card, he jumps into the shot! Player B gets to make a shot and rolls the dice, as indicated on the Gun card.

After rolling he compares the numbers rolled his Gunslinger card and dishes out the damage to player A!

Play continues until one or both Gunslingers are dead, with zero health left! The goal of Wild Guns is to be the last Gunslinger standing.

Looking for a party game? Wild Guns is a great party game and can play with up to ten players. Watch out, this is when the bullets fly and the Gunslingers drop dead!
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

boardgame: Decisive Battle of the Combined Fleet
description:
Decisive Battle of the Combined Fleet is a tactical level surface naval battle wargame in WW2 Pacific theater. Players control either the fleet of Imperial Japanese Navy or United States Navy, and fight each other. Players manage ships' movement, gun and torpedo attack.

The game includes three historical scenarios and one campaign scenario. All scenarios represent situation of autumn 1942 at Solomon sea.　Campaign scenario repeats four fictional battles in limited force. Winner determined by victory point based on damage given to opponent's ships.

This game was designed for wargame beginners.

Game scale: 0.5 Nautical miles per hex, 5 minutes　per turn, 1 ship per unit

Isle of Eldermoor Chess is a large board chess variant.

It is played on a larger 10X10 board to allow more pieces, and then borrowed the idea of the Citadel corner from Omega Chess, an idea quickly expanded to an area of seven additional squares on each corner, leaving the board with a more castle-like appearance.

The game also uses the 'river' idea in the centre from Xiangqi, as well as an inner King's sanctum area,

The first goal of Isle of Eldermoor was to toss out the traditional Western Chess game. There are great variants such as Omega Chess, Opulent Chess, and Grand Chess which take the traditional game and add to it with a few new pieces. It's been done.

So only the King was transfered to Eldermoor.

The rest of the army array reflects more of a fantasy Medieval army, so Paladins, Cavaliers, Yeomen, Soldiers, Archers etc are used.

As an ode to long nights of Dungeons & Dragons over the years a fantasy element with Wizards, Griffins and Dragons are also part of the game

With an all new piece array, I needed different movement patterns, well different from Western Chess at least. There are so many variant pieces almost every conceivable movement pattern has been done before in one variant or another.

The resulting movements draw on Western Chess, Xiangqi, Shogi and related variants.

The game has three win conditions;

The game is won by checkmating the opposition king.

or

If a player loses both Wizards the morale of their army is broken and they lose the game.

or

You win by occupying both opponent's wizard towers, and holding them for one full turn.

Space Infantry: New Worlds includes new missions, new enemies, and special creatures, all in one, neat zip locked package.

Once again gamers can take their squad of Space Infantry into the hives, or experience a pair of new surface missions. Additionally, you’ll battle the deadly Void Spiders or heartless Drones. But you’ll have some brand new equipment to battle them with, including the new Flamer. It’s all here and all ready for your preorder.

New Worlds Includes:

Two new Hive Missions (H007 and H008) 
Two new surface Missions: Mission S009 and Mission S010 
Two new Enemies: Beastmasters and Cthonians 
Two Special Enemies: Void Spiders and Drones 
One new Unit: Flamer 
One new Campaign Sheet 
Special rules for Flame and Poison Damage 
New counters: Flame Damage, Spider Eggs, Individual Enemy Counters, and more.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

boardgame: SkirmishElite: Screaming Eagles at Hamburger Hill
description:
SkirmishElite: Screaming Eagles at Hamburger Hill is a scenario book used to recreate skirmish-level battles and campaigns centered on the 101st Airborne's struggle to capture the NVA strongpoint on Dong Ap Bia (aka Hamburger Hill) from the 29th NVA Regiment in May 1969.  The book includes eight detailed scenarios that can be played alone or together in one of three campaigns.

To accomodate replayability, the book includes a variable order of battle for each scenario which results in unique scenario and campaign replays.  The book includes specific translation rules so that make it compatible with a variety of existing skirmish wargame rule sets.  It also includes a unique generic order of battle listing which will allow for the use of the scenarios in any skirmish rule system.

The scenarios are based on historical research and the book contains background information and a map of the actual campaign for Hamburger Hill.

The year is 2151, and the demand for pizza has never been greater.

As a hungry galaxy clamors for thin-crusted Italian delicacies, the little-understood, physics-defying Two Dimensional Drive has been employed to meet the need. By collapsing three-dimensional space down to a two-dimensional matrix, the 2D Drive makes it possible to cross vast distances in seconds. The catch? 2D ships can transport only flat cargo – which makes them perfect for pizza and not much else. (Possible caveat: Cosmic Pancakes.)

In this strange future, a daring pizza pioneer such as yourself can become rich beyond your wildest dreams – but only if you're willing to take on the risks of interstellar, two-dimensional flight. Perhaps the greatest challenge: Mastering the mind-bending reality that is two-dimensional space. Luckily for you, your ship has a tutorial mode.

Cosmic Pizza is a spatial reasoning game for 2-4 players in which players must master an ever-increasing array of dizzying maneuvers; it also boasts variable difficulty levels. If you're a hardcore puzzle fan, you can take on Space Crazy mode, while your kids zip along in the lighter Junior mode.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
"Clash in the South Pacific" is an operational level board wargame for Battle of the Santa Cruz Island. Players control either the aircraft carrier fleet of Imperial Japanese Navy or United States Navy. Players manage fleet movement, search for the enemy, and aircraft operation. The basic design is similar to Task Force: Carrier Battles in the Pacific.

Winner is determined by victory points based on damage given to only opponent aircraft carrier.

This game was designed for wargame beginners.

Game scale: 75 km per hex, 90 minutes　per turn, 1 ship, 2 Destroyers or 9 Planes per unit.
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable

description:
Each player draws 10 letters from the bag, then take turns trying to form the longest word.
Each letter has its own value showed on the chip. Once a new word is formed, the player adds the points for letters of that word. Words must be interrelated.
The round ends when a player has placed all of its 10 letters. The scores are counted and the first player to reach the score of 100 points (several rounds) wins the game.

"Auf Spurensuche am Limes" is a board game with an accompanying book with 48 pages of background information.

The game board shows a excavation site with zones of different depth. At the start of the game, findings and discoveries (tiles connected to one colour) are distributed on the site, face up. In a next step, all findings are covered with the dust of time - terrain tiles on top of them, thus creating multiple layers of tiles. Only after this, colours are assigned to the players - hopefully thy remember some of "their" findings.

Each player plays a team of archaeologists trying to excavate in particular those findings which match his own research assignment (colour). In trying to achieve that, however, fragments of roman documents might be discovered, earning the player additional points.

At a player's turn, he performs each of 4 actions:
- Move his expedition manager 1 - 3 spaces around the border of the site (to more promising sectors). His position determines the spaces of the site which can be reached by archeologists (neutral colour).
- Now the player has 5 movement points to move one or more neutral archeologists, either to the sector determined by his own expedition manager or to some bad position for the opponents.
- The 3rd action the site is explored. Two terrain tiles with archeologists on them are lifted and the things nderneath are now visible. They are (mostly) not directly available.
- Finally, the player can perform an excavation. To get the several findings visible, you need to place one or more archeologists around the space, depending on the depth of the zone.

Findings of your own colour are directly put into the museum, more worthy findings are replacing ones with lesser value.
Findings of an opponent`s colour are not that easily excavated. You need allocated points or some dice luck to get them.

Since mostly a finding is not discovered and excavated in the same turn, players have to be careful not to discover findings, which can be easily "grabbed" by their opponents.

The game ends when one player manages to excavate all of his assigned findings (winner of the game). If nobody manages to do so, the  game ends, when a player reaches the end of the "expedition track", where successful excavations and discoveries are registered, and the player with the most allocated points wins the game.

The advanced game offers fences to make it harder for opponents to excavates findings discovered by other players.
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable

description:
An Inheritance game for two to seven players

Your Great Aunt Hattie has passed away and named you in her will. All she left behind was a (sizeable) bank account and her collection of ART DECO prints from the 1920’s. Aunt Hattie was a bit eccentric - and created a game for her inheritors. If you want your share of the (sizeable) family bank, you’ll have to play! Inheritors start with a stake from the bank, and continually draw money from the pool to bid on Art Prints until all have been won. Each print is assigned points. The Inheritor who gains the most points takes home the entire collection - and the balance of the bank!

Each round, players roll dice [the bid]. Highest roll pays the difference between his die roll and the low die roll and gets the print [card]. If high roll is tied, everybody gets double the roll in chips and the card goes in the discard pile. Winning a Wild Card allows you to choose any card from the discard pile.

Cards range in points from 2 - 12. The 2 - 5 point cards may be 'DOUBLED DOWN', but the 6 - 12 point cards may not. During scoring, each DOUBLED DOWN card doubles the entire score of a color group. All chips accumulated score as well. Highest total wins.

ART DECO is primarily a party game, but is popular at Conventions and any activity with groups. Suitable for young and old.

COMPONENTS:

     A 75 card deck of ART DECO prints. 


For the two player game, only use the Color Cards that have between 2 and 5 points. Color cards are arranged into five color groups, or sets. There are an extra fifteen Wild Cards in the deck have no points on them - for the two player game, only use five.

     Rules are on an additional 12 cards.
     you'll need to provide your dice and poker chips.


AVAILABILITY:

     ART DECO is only available from The Game Crafters website.

"The Battle of the Bulge" is an operational level wargame for  Ardennes Offensive. There are other games of the same name, but this one is a unique game that is designed for the wargame beginner

Players will control either German Army or Allied Army. Players manage movement and combat by basic hex and counter mechanism.
The game winner is determined by control of the important cities and exit to the map.

Game scale: 6km per hex, 1 day per turn, a division per unit

First published with Tanks and Tenka fubu in one box titled "Simulation games for beginner #1".
Republished as Command Magazine Japan #120 in 2014.
family: History: World War II (Battle of the Bulge)

description:
This games takes a regular game of Captain's Mistress (this is sold under the brand name Connect Four) and turns it into a party game using a framework suitable for most other abstract strategy games.  In the game, a player's team selects where they go during that team's turn.  When it is not the team's turn, the player has an opportunity to leave their team.

This game was discussed as a variant in this thread on BGG, but has been spun off as its own entry due to additional development and changes on how the game is run.  The discussion can be found here.

Similar to Richesses de France.
Oise is a department in the north of France.

The goal of the game is to complete a circuit in the department  faster than your opponents by traveling from city to city. This is done either by foot, bicycle, or car. Money can be earned (to buy a bicycle or car) or lost during the game.

In his turn, the player moves one space or two space with bicycle or 4 spaces with his car following different routes. Its purpose is to visit every city in any order.
Every time you stop on a region that is not named, draw a card. That brings You some good or bad news.

Player who visits all regions on game board and returns to the space he started first is the winner.

Cinema Tactics is a game of sword fighting, bow shooting, spell casting, and gunslinging in a variety of settings ranging from traditional fantasy landscapes to the streets of Tombstone to the deep black of outer space.

Gameplay models every blow and desperate block with damage effects that lend encounters an especially cinematic feel.  Characters alternate spending Action Points to move and fight, calling suits or colors from a deck of playing cards to determine the the success of their attacks.  Players can engage in multi-part adventures or create bands of Heroes and pit them against each other.  Heroes acquire loot and new skills as they emerge victorious from their quests.

The Living Rulebook, maps, Heroes and Monsters, and adventures are all available for free at cinematactics.wordpress.com

TV related game based on one of Britain's favourite shows. Essentially, contestants took part in various games and skills (mainly for their comedy value) and the game captures that flavour. Accrue points and "points mean prizes" as Bruce Forsyth (pictured on the box) famously once said. Catchphrases were Bruce's speciality and he is the originator of the most famous ones.

Browsers might know the famous Beat the Clock television show on both sides of the Atlantic and this has a certain element of that show in its style.

boardgame: All Your Mechs Are Belong To Us
description:
AYM (as it is abbreviated) is a tactical boardgame where players take the role of a mech pilot.  Players custom design their mechs by use of a series of transparency overlays, giving them a final visual representation of the mech they designed.  Players' mechs then enter a map via dropship, where they will need to complete the objectives of that scenario.  These scenarios include search and destroy, retrieve resources, etc.  At the end of each scenario, players gain money and experience in which they can upgrade their mechs in preparation for future scenarios.

AYM is played as a tactical board game with some basic RPG elements.  Mechs are represented by game pieces on a physical grid map of the scenario.

To object of the game is to get 3 of Your 4 pawns to the end in the order that appears on one of your cards.

Each player will try to move the pawns in the order of the "trifecta color" of his choice.
In his turn player rolls three dice and advance three of four pieces, each being moved in the number of spaces shown by one of the three dice.

If the first pawn arrivs in Finish a die is removed. 
If the second pawn arrives next to it a second die is removed.
If the third pawn arrives next to them the game is over.
If The finishing order of the pawns gives the so called "trifecta color" showed on card the player wins the game.

Toufruit, published in 1990, is the precursor of Halli Galli.

The cards and the rules are identical but there is no bell in this game. Players try to shout "Toufruit" first instead of ringing the bell.

In his turn player places one card face up in front of him from his face-down deck. And if there are total of five of one kind of fruit on the table the player who shouts "Toufruit" first gets all played cards. When you run out of cards, you're out.
Game ends if there are 2 players left. Who has most cards is the winner.

Publisher Blurb:

Enter the Republic
 The year is 3085. In the wake of the Jihad, a new realm—the Republic of the Sphere—has been born. Drawing war-weary masses from all over the Inner Sphere, the Republic stands for unity and security, in the hopes that never again will its worlds suffer from centuries of relentless war. But even though the common threat has finally passed, few believe they have seen the last of war. As the combined realms and Clans of the Inner Sphere struggle to rebuild, a new balance of power has begun to emerge.
 Field Manual: 3085 updates the military and political state of the Inner Sphere in the years following the end of the Jihad and the formation of the Republic of the Sphere. This report includes a brief history and overview of recent developments in the BattleTech universe (including the formation and organization of the Republic of the Sphere), as well as current TO&Es for all of the major ’Mech and mercenary commands employed by the realms and Clans of the post-Jihad Inner Sphere. Special rules are also included, enabling players to create characters and forces for use in campaigns set in and after this critical point in BattleTech history.

Publisher Blurb:

Genesis welcomes you to the world of Dark Age, where different armies and factions battle for survival against each other and the planet itself. Dark Age: Genesis is a scaleable skirmish level game based on the dark images of BROM designed for 2 or more players with approximately 10-20 models a side.

The rulebook contains 150 pages of pure excitement! Imagine the harsh environments of Samaria, learn about the rise of the Forsaken and read about the rich history of the Dragyri.You will get the core rules, two comprehensive Forcelists, unit cards, templates and extensive background information... Everything you need to startplaying Dark Age.  Are you ready for something dangerous?

Publisher Blurb:

For those of you waiting to field your Saint Mary force. Or for those who have been waiting to unleash the madness of the Toxic Cult, now is your time.  Now is the time for Exodus!!  Exodus brings you 118 pages of intense background material, beautiful yet macabre artwork and allthe rules you need to field Saint Mary's forces or the Skarrd Cult of Toxin. Genesis is required for the core rules.

Publisher Blurb:

Evolution is your resource to field forces of either Forsaken: Saint John or The Brood in you Dark Age games.  This is Evolution.  This is not a book about biology or Charles Darwin.  What you will find hereis something else, something darker. We introduceyou now to the bitter reality of the Southern Crusade. Its proud general is John, a giant among theSaints. While in the Forsaken homeland, ruthless politicians manoeuvre for spiritual and worldly power. They struggle against each other with intrigue, deceipt, and any means available. In this twisted version of a classic battle of man against beast, Saint John leads his forces against the deviantcreations of The Mere, The Brood.  Their creators thought they could control them. They were wrong.

Publisher Blurb:

The definitive collection of the core Dark Age rules, scenarios and sample forces. In this book you will also find counters and templates that are used by all the forces of Samaria.

Publisher Blurb:

Welcome to Samaria... an abandoned planet, a ruined world, a place where few still remember! The world of Dark Age is a world of contradiction. Plasma rifles meet crossbows, laser torches meet horse-drawn ploughs, and future philosophy meets ancient religion. However, the inhabitants of Dark Age share one, simple trait: the instinct for survival at any cost!

Featuring art from the inimitable Brom and Dark Age's own in-house artists, the Dark Age: Apocalypse Core Rules contains everything you need to play Dark Age, a fully scaleable, skirmish-level wargame using 28-33mm scale miniatures. Within these pages you'll find rules for campaigns and scenarios, as well as information on terrain interaction and weather effects to liven up your game, plus 13 playable Forcelists, complete with fantastic artwork and intriguing storylines.

Re-implements:

    Dark Age: Core Rules
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description: This is an expansion for Zoff am Herd! and contains new cards. The expansion is sometimes called "The Brad Worst expansion" because one of the cards is named "Brad Worst". With this expansion you can play the game up to 7 players. The expansion was available in Essen in 2008.

Objective: Be the first ending the round trip in Friedrichshafen 
 - moving by turns -
roll the die, move your meeple & and follow instructions (glued on cover inside) ending on a marked (red) space

remarks: published beginning/mid 1910's
cute meeples (pewter figures) - see pictures

The "thief of stars" in English is a cooperative game where players play together against the mage.

The mage Fedor has stolen all the stars. Player must recover and hang these in the sky before he regains his powers.

In his turn player rolls the dice and advance the number of boxes. There are squares where the stars could be hanged or bad squares where player help the mage regain his power.

The game ends in two ways:
Either the players have hung all the stars in the sky and win.
or the mage Fedor has regains his power and the players lose the game.

Trivia game with the theme of the questions being on different areas of antique collecting; Porcelain, Silver, Paintings, and so on. The game is licenced by the BBC who have been broadcasting the Antiques Roadshow for well over 20 years.

The object is to answer enough questions correctly to 'build' a medallion. When assembled (10 correct answers in all), you must answer one more question from the subject of your choice and if you get it right, you win.

One particular aspect makes it a true family game. The questions takes three forms. At the start decide if you are an Expert, an Enthusiast, or an Amateur. If you are an Expert, then you get the question as is. If you are an Enthusiast, you get three possible answers and must choose one of the. If you are an Amateur, you get two possible answers and must choose one. Thus children can play with adults quite happily.

Card game using a regular deck of cards and a board.

"The Board represents a typical year;  a constant struggle to pay endless bills with a fixed income and a few opportunities of supplementing it.  But with good management and more good luck than bad, it is just possible to get through the year with a few pounds saved!  The winner of the game is the first person who proves he can do so."

You start the year with 100 pounds, and every player starts on January 1st.

On your turn you may play a card of your choice that matches the suit on the square on the board that you are currently on. If you do, you move that many spaces equivalent to the card that you played (Jack = 11, Queen = 12, King =13). The square you now land on will dictate the suit you should play next turn so that you can advance again. Squares you land on will have good or bad instructions (Gain 6 pounds, lose 9 pounds for example), and so on.

You win the game if you have at least 100 pounds and a card that will take you to New Year's Eve.

All Aboard! is a  pre-school game for 3 to 5 year olds. Match colours and shapes as you collect your passengers. Two games in one!
Game 1: Roll the colour spot dice to collect the coloured windows for your vehicles. 
Game 2: Turn your vehicle over and all aboard as you spin the spinner and collect passengers in shaped windows.
The winner of each game is to fill up your train respectively.

Contents:
4 double-sided playing boards
24 square coloured cards
24 shaped passenger cards
Spinner
Colour spot dice
Dice cup
Rules

A game for younger children.

On their turn, each player rolls a regular die. The number they roll indicates which part of their Beatrix Potter character card that they can 'assemble'. Unusually for these sort of games, like Beetle, is that you need nine parts to your character. Number 1 represents each corner piece so you will need to roll a 1 four times to assemble each corner of your picture.

The first to finish their Peter Rabbit character picture, wins.

There are 100 cards each with 5 questions and 3 answers in each card. The goal is to learn of financial transactions, stock exchange and other technical terms. Correct answers are marked according to budget in the index, an incorrect answer will forfeit the round budget. At the end of game player with the highest number of exchange points wins the game.

Tactical board game where you have to shop as quickly as possible while managing its budget. Each player receives 12 euros and its shopping list. 
In her turn player roll the dice and moves her pawn in the direction of her choice. Every time she lands on a colored box she can make a purchase in the store of the same color. She then roll the dice to determine Euros she may use. The amount sets her budget and she decides whether to buy. The winner is the first that comes out of the market with all purchases in her shopping list!

A German subsidiary card set for use with any Trivial Pursuit master game with specific questions about Bavaria. The game contains 500 cards with 6 questions each from the following 6 categories:

Blue: Geographie (Geography)
Pink: Unterhaltung (Entertainment)
Yellow: Historie (History)
Brown: Kultur (Culture)
Green: Weiß-Blau (White-Blue)
Orange: Sport & Freizeit (Sport & Leisure)

The questions in the green category (white-blue) center around slang terms and very specific bavarian topics, making it very hard for non-bavarians to answer.

This is a promotional edition of the classic trivia game Trivial Pursuit, published by an Austrian radio station to celebrate its thirtieth birthday.

The game contains 250 trivia cards with 6 questions each from the following categories:

Blue: Radio, TV und Kinohits (Radio, TV and movie hits)
Pink: Ö3erlei (specific questions about the radio station)
Yellow: Popgeschichte & Hitparade (Pop history & hit parade)
Brown: Prominente & Austro-Stars (Celebrities & Austrian stars)
Green: Sport (Sports)
Orange: Zeitgeschehen & Politik (Events of the day & politics)

Every player gets a circular playing piece with six pie-shaped holes (standard Trivial Pursuit issue). There is no game board. A special six-sided die showing the six colors mentioned above is rolled by the player on turn. The color tells from which category the question comes.

If the player on turn can answer the question, he gets another turn and, if he does not has it already, the corresponding colored pie piece which he puts in his playing piece. If he answers wrong, it's the next player's turn.

The first player to collect all colors wins the game.

Although it can be played stand-alone, the set is fully compatible with every other Trivial Pursuit game.

Relive the war the destroyed an empire and herald the rise of a new empire in the Spanish American War! Play the Cuban theater, or the Pacific theater! Or combine them!

Players choose which theater they want to take operations on per turn and take as many actions as they want. But the American has to worry about the Spanish player's mines that he is allowed to place concealed on the sea zone. With these advantages... Can the Spanish Player change history or will the superior American troops win again?

Football enthusiasts can test their football knowledge around the Austrian football events. It addresses questions about the entire Austrian Bundesliga. The aim of the game is to answer questions in 5 areas of knowledges from the beginning to the presence of the Austrian football properly. Who was the first  after completion of the two halves of play by reaching the goal-box wins the game.

A hardback book featuring three games based on Greek mythology. The three sets are:

Tribes of Legend: This is a miniatures game involving armies of humans, centaurs and satyrs. Combat is dice-driven, movememtn is simple, and the basic grouping for all activities is the unit (as opposed to individual figures). Rules are provided for terrain effects, and each faction has its own special rules. The overall game rules are very simple, shunning the usual complexity of miniatures rules.

Ancient Heroes: a miniatures skirmish game for smaller battles. This is a playing card driven battle game, where individual figures move around the tabletop and draw cards to perform tasks and fight battles. Different fighting abilities and levels of armour are represented by differing card values in the game. Ordinary playing cards are used to play with.

Trials of a Demigod: a solitaire quest driven by action cards.This is mostly an exercise in pulling cards and seeing the total they add up to - nicely presented but not very much of a game.

A quiz game, designed specifically as a travel game, for a  2 to 20 players, easy to carry and easy to play, you only need paper and pencil or the attached answer sheet. The two issues are separate sections for adults and children, the reader identifies the question number and reads the question and four answers. The players write the answer they think is right on the sheet and lay face down in the middle. If all answers are given, players with the correct answer get one point. Who has the most points at end of the game is the winner

The players have to draw a word that their teammates have to guess before time runs out. But that's not so easy, because 6 different categories representing more difficult. The painter must, depending on the category, draw the image upside down, paint standing with the "other" hand, humming, or standing on one leg etc.

Team who finish first the "winner's circle" wins the game!

Starmada is a miniatures board game of starship combat. At its most basic level, players take command of mighty fleets in a general attempt to reduce each other to just so much space junk. At its most advanced, the game can be customized to breathe life into any science fiction background that players might imagine.

The Nova Rulebook represents a new beginning for The Universal Game of Starship Combat. While in some ways this new edition is notably different from previous incarnations of the game, at its core Starmada remains the game players around the world have grown to know and love. As always, the guiding principle has been simple, but not simplistic. Within this book, players will find:

Streamlined inertial movement, with optional movement systems to encompass a wide range of settings;
An intuitive combat system, including three distinct and layered types of starship defenses; and
The most comprehensive set of starship construction rules available in any game currently available!

Dozens of stand-alone options and advanced rules are provided, from which you may pick and choose to fit your desired level of complexity and create the type of game YOU want to play.

Re-implements:

     Starmada X
     Starmada: The Admiralty Edition
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
Palomino is a casino-style table game with one dealer and up to seven players. The dealer's hand is always four cards or less, is never better than a pair, and is always completely face up. The player can buy up to five cards to try and beat the dealer, winning more money when the dealer's hand is better.

In addition to this Palomino board, which is available at the Cheapass website, you will need a deck of cards with one joker, and some poker chips.

Source: http://cheapass.com/freegames/palomino. Publisher's website.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
Boneyard is a silent-bid auction game in which players are bidding on buckets of money. The object is to be the first player to accumulate 40 dollars.  Each player starts the game with 26 dollars.

This game is part of the Poker Suite compilation from CheapAss Games.

A simple and quickly played trivia game about the Finnish language. The game comprises cards with 500 questions in five different categories, such as dialects, slangs and funny synonyms. The game also includes 49 special cards. The game box is designed to be small enough to carry with you when traveling.

Seven-Handed Poker is a clever little way to play poker!  This game requires a single deck of playing cards and seven poker chips.

In Seven-Handed Poker, players play any amount of cards from their hands.  The player with the better poker hand wins the round. After each round, players always draw three cards regardless of how many cards they just played.  Hand sizes will grow and shrink depending on how many cards a player plays each round.

Willow is a poker-like gambling game with a simpler hand structure.  Each player has two cards.  The best possible hand is a pair and other hands are ranked by the difference between the two cards: the greater the difference, the better the hand!

Willow uses a deck of 36 cards.  Use a standard playing deck but remove the jokers, kings, and every card of a single suit.

Willow is part of the Poker Suite compilation by Cheapass Games.

A fun and entertaining game well suited for social evenings. The game is about answering 10 questions in a fast pace without hesitation. Also, you may not answer yes or no, you may not cite the question, repeat an earlier answer or answer with a question. Be quick and guard your tongue! If you fail to abide by the aforementioned rules you fail and lose your turn. However, if you manage to answer all questions in a correct fashion you get to keep the question card. The players may agree upon how many cards are needed to win (and thus affect the playing time).

boardgame: MASK: Die Jagd nach den Sonnensteinen
description:
An old roll-and-move boardgame loosely based on the popular M.A.S.K. cartoon series and toy franchise of the 1980s.

Every one of the (up to 4) players takes control of one MASK character and must try to move him on a mini-course (16 spaces) back to the starting point. Movement is determined by rolling a D3, and a special Sunstone-Die determines how many of such stones a player might receive. Once a player gets more than 3 Sunstones at any point, he has to return them all to the box.

Note: the Sunstone-Die also determines how many spaces the 'Miles Mayhem' character (a villain) moves. Once the villain lands on the same space as a character, the player has to move his character back to the last space with a vehicle.

If, at any time, a player rolls the same number as his Sunstones, he cannot advance, but has to go back instead.

Contents:

    deck of cards

    4 miniature cars (red, green, yellow, and blue)
    game board

War at Japan Sea is a strategic wargame for Russo-Japanese war. This game issued with Japanese self-published magazine SLGamer Volume 5.

Players control either Japanese Navy or Russian Navy. Players manage fleet movement and battle, to control of seas. The basic system of this game is reference from War at Sea.
The fall of Port Arthur fortress is a big event in the game. Russian Pacific fleet will be wiped out by fall of the fortress. Russian player can be delay the fall of the fortress by the control of the sea areas.

Winner will determine by victory points. Victory points can be earned from control of seas, and also earned from surviving warships at the end of the game.

A monopoly (from Greek monos μόνος (alone or single) + polein πωλεῖν (to sell)) exists when a specific person or enterprise is the only supplier of a particular commodity.

Monopoly is a roll-and-move game where players move around the game board buying or trading properties, developing their properties with houses and hotels. When opponents land on players owned property, the owning player collects rent from the landing player. Owning sets of properties increases the rent paid to the player who owns them, by the player who lands on the square. The ultimate goal is to have the most money and drive opponents into bankruptcy.

A version of Monopoly licensed with the Limited Too retail store clothing chain.

The ancient prophecies announce the arrival of a child with amazing powers. The Haliwell sisters will need to eliminate the demons and prevent corruption of the chiled calledWyatt.

In her turn player rolls the dice and makes action accoridng to the squares she landed. There are different types of actions: Power, Demons, Allies, Places and Prophecies.

The game ends:
If demons reach the end of the Corruption track - the players lose the game.
The letters drawn by landing the Propecy squares form the word GIDEON - the players lose the game.
All demons are eliminated - the players win the game.

This is a Trivial Pursuit edition with questions exclusively from the year 1998.

The game contains 250 trivia cards with 6 questions each from the following categories:

Blue: Schlagzeilen (Headlines)
Pink: Showbusiness
Yellow: Weltgeschehen (World Affairs)
Brown: Klatsch & Tratsch (Gossip & Chit-Chat)
Green: Sport (Sports)
Orange: Rundblick (Panorama)

Every player gets a circular playing piece with six pie-shaped holes (standard Trivial Pursuit issue). There is no game board. A special six-sided die showing the six colors mentioned above is rolled by the player on turn. The color tells from which category the question comes.

If the player on turn can answer the question, he gets another turn and, if he does not has it already, the corresponding colored pie piece which he puts in his playing piece. If he answers wrong, it's the next player's turn.

The first player to collect all colors wins the game.

The set is fully compatible with every other Trivial Pursuit game.

The marketplace ("tori" in Finnish, "torg" in Swedish) of Vaasa ("Vasa" in Swedish) was a vivid trade center in the 1930's, to which people from the surrounding countryside came to sell their goods. Townsfolk frequented the marketplace and various goods traded owners.

The war had its impact on the nation in the 1940's and there was shortage in food as well as other products, which lead to that the range of goods was reduced in stores as well as in the marketplace. Trade in the black market became usual especially when the ration cards were taken into use. The nation recovered in the 1950's and the belief in the future got the trade started once again.

In the game the players try to accomplish various tasks and bring goods from the market stands to the stores located around the marketplace at the same time as they are looking for the missing whistle of the marketplace guard.

During the game the players receive rewards for accomplished tasks, such as new skills, which allow them to move on to more difficult tasks. However, the life at the markeplace is not that easy. Good and bad crop years, the rising population, the war, economical success, the belief in the future and different events in the society have their impact on the prices and the players' possibilities to carry out tasks.

This is an exciting and educating game for 2-4 players, in which local history and the memories of the elderly townsfolk of Vaasa are interwoven into an innovative boardgame.

The board has two sides, one easier and one harder. The game is suitable for both young and more mature players.

description:
What is the smallest mammal in the world? How much did the first mobile phones weigh?

A fun and quickly played trivia game for the whole family. 500 questions in five different categories, including: sports, entertainment, travel, technology, animals and nature. The game also comes with 49 special cards.

Etsi ja löydä ("Seek and Find") is a game for children. There are 36 picture cards and 50 question cards in the game. The picture cards are placed on the table face up. Then a random question card is drawn (by a player who can read, otherwise the players do not necessarily have to know how to read yet) and a player tries to find quickly as possible one or more cards matching the question card. Usually the question relates to some detail in the picture cards (for example: find as many animals as possible with a tail!). The player marks the found cards with a piece and scores points as shown on a timer (a special timer included in the game). The player with the most points in the end wins.

Shrek and Donkey crosses the country to free Princess Fiona captured by the Dragon.

The game takes place in two phases. 
In first phase players walk around the board to recover the weapons needed to face the dragon and to free the princess. 
In second phase they recover 6 objects (flowers, poems, balloons, etc..) to protect Fiona before reaching the top of the tower by Lord Farquaad.

In his turn, the active player may:
1) move, rolling a die with six faces.

2) get a random event. Some of these events apply immediately, as the "angry farmers" or "guards", others may be kept by the player and be used later as "My turn" which allows you to replay a turn or "The onion is good" which wastes a turn the player of your choice.

3) roll a six sided figure, and advance in the number of spaces indicated. The player can move his figure in any direction and by taking any path. If it encounters an event-driven model (brigand, custody, etc..) It solves the encounter, usually on a combat die roll.

4) if the figure reaches a square, the player can perform a search to find items still available. 
Depending on the phase in which the player is, he will seek weapons to face the dragon or gifts to seduce the beautiful.

When a player wishes, he can go on the square with dragon to confront it. It will then launch a die which he will add his bonus weapons. If he gets at least 7, the princess is saved. Then begins the second phase. In each round, Lord Farquaad will rise a box in his lap (there are 12 laps). 
The first player who gets the 6 gift before the evil Lord marries the nice Princess is the winner.

How to become an expert at recognising the shlps. missiles and aircraft of the Royal Navy.
 You can use these cards in many ways that will help you memorise the 'hardware’ of today’s Royal Navy.

A Recognition Quiz is one game you can play. Each card is numbered so you earn that number of points for every ship you Identify,

Here are two card games you can play:
Navy Snap—Deal seven or more cards to each player. Place remainder of pack in centre of table. Play in rotation, each player trying to match from his own hand the top card of the table pack (which is discarded on to a second pile).
Players match cards by producing any ship of the same type — i.e. any class of frigate with any other class of frigate; a nuclear sub with a nuclear sub but any non-nuclear class of sub with any other non-nuclear.
Players may be challenged on their matching and. If they have mixed one type with another, they have to take their card back and forfeit their throw next time round.
First player out wins.

Navy Rummy — Deal seven cards to each player. Play like ordinary rummy, collecting sets of three (i.e. three subs of any type. three aircraft. three ships of any type) and runs of four (in ascending or descending order of displacement tonnage — i.e. carriers, auxiliaries, cruisers. Polaris subs, missile destroyers, frigates and so on).
First player out wins.

Win a presentation colour picture for your own idea. If you think up a game of your own — which must be simple as well as fun — let's know about it at the address on the other side of this card. If we like It we’ll send a presentation colour picture of a modern Royal Navy ship or aircraft.

Game is played by pulling chips from a bag. Each player has a Search bag and a Dogfight bag. Players start by using their Search bag to find opponents aircraft. Player to find the other players aircraft gets to draw from their Dogfight bag and will either lose sight of the enemy or shoot one of opponents planes down.
Very easy.
Comes with 44 chips and 4 bags. 12 of the chips are tokens to keep track of your aircraft. These tokens are being produced now and will be shipped free of charge if you order before they are complete. These are not required for game play but does add a little to the game.

The game consists of 66 cards (54 numbered cards 1-54 and 12 betting cards, 2 for each players "more" or "less")
Number cards are shuffled and 6 cards are dealt for each player.

The object of the game is to get rid of his number cards first.

In his turn player plays a card on the table face-down. The other players bet on this card with their betting cards what they think is it greater or lower card as the last card.
Player who loses the bet, picks an additional card.
Some wildcards allow You to not pick up a card if You lose a bet.

On June 26, 1876, Lt. Col. George A. Custer drove his seven companies of the U.S. 7th Cavalry into the valley of the Little Bighorn River in search of that last big victory to cement his place in history. There he met a vast gathering of Native American nations not willing to back down from the normal tactics of the U.S. Army. Custer planted his name in history, but for the wrong reasons as he and 255 of his men were slaughtered at the Little Bighorn.

Little Bighorn Rummy brings back both the famous and infamous cast list of this incident to create an unusual style of rummy, specifically a set collection game in which players collect both Native American and U.S. Cavalry melds to secure points while attempting to empty their hand. Special cards and support cards alter play, allowing players to play single cards (chiefs) to their table, look through the discard pile, and help Lt. Col. Custer (worth -20 points) escape the scene, among other actions. The first player to achieve 150 points wins.

A Texan-made American football game based on the exciting new tactic at the time, the T-Formation. This is not a statistical game and is played head-to-head against your opponent. British card game, described as a game and a puzzle.

Any number of players from two upwards can play. The cards are dealt round in the usual manner, faces (pictures) downwards. The players must not look at the face side of their cards. The player left of dealer leads and turns his or her top card face up in the centre of the table. Each player plays a card in turn Each successive card should be quickly played so that a "body" is fitted to the "Head" on the last card played. The title line "Grandfather's Whiskers" on the back of each card indicates the half containing a "body."

Lots of combinations of head and body will be created to the amusement of all the players, but when Grandfather's Head appears on Grandfather's Feet, players must call "Out" or "In". This is because his whiskers might be under the eiderdown on the card or poking out from under the eiderdown. The first player to call correctly takes all the cards in the centre. If a wrong call is made, that player must give every other player a card from his hand.

The objective is to be the last player with all the cards.

THE SIX JIG-TYPE PUZZLES
The backs of the cards comprise six different jig-type puzzles. The cards numbered from 1 to 8 constitute a quite simple puzzle, for the picture is formed merely by placing the cards in a certain order with their edges touching. In the second puzzle group (numbers 9 to 16) the cards overlap.
The remaining groups, numbers 17-24, 25-32, 33-40 and 41-48, become successively more difficult. The last group (41-48) in which Grandfather and his Whiskers are depicted, is the most advanced puzzle of the whole series.

Following on the heels of Thunderstone Advance: Towers of Ruin, the game goes underground in Thunderstone Advance: Caverns of Bane. Monsters now carry treasure and use it against you – but when you slay them, you can claim the treasure and use it a new way when playing it from your hand. Thunderstone Advance: Caverns of Bane also includes seven new heroes and 14 new village cards.

description:
The hardest part of design isn’t doing the design. It’s working with everyone else without wanting to run screaming for the hills.

Collaborating with smart people on challenging problems is, inevitably, fraught with conflict. Conflict can be good for creative work, moving a project forward as designers wrestle with the challenge. But not all conflict is healthy, and frankly most designers aren’t, shall we say, diplomats.

Surviving Design Projects is, among other things, a little game to help designers hone their people skills. It’s pretty easy. Each player takes turns being the Creative Director. He or she turns over a Situation card, and then tells a little story about the situation shown. She can make it up or draw from her own experience.

Let's say I turn over the situation "Lack of Inputs." Maybe I make up this story:

Though the client has provided stacks of user research, they can’t offer us any clear business priorities. They’re just asking us to make their site better without giving us a sense of what they want to accomplish. I need to go talk to the client about this, and try to get them to give us some inputs.

Each player around the table chooses a Pattern card from their hand and places it face-up next to the situation card. The Creative Director chooses one player to go first and describe how they would apply the pattern to the situation. That is, to describe the technique would they use to make the conflict productive. Let’s say one player uses “Make Educated Guesses” and describes it this way:

The Make Educated Guesses pattern suggests we fill in the holes ourselves with some assumptions. I guess the idea is that we’re smart people, so we should know what the starting point is. We could brainstorm a list of assumptions about the project’s business priorities, then share that with the client to validate it.

The Creative Director then chooses which technique she would use to deal with the situation and leads a group discussion about the choice. The person who suggested the pattern receives the situation card. The player to the Creative Director’s left becomes the next creative director, flipping the top Situation card, and starting again. At the end of the game, the person who has the most Situation cards is the winner.

So that’s it. It’s a pretty simple game, but the idea is to get people talking about difficult situations in a no-strings environment.

boardgame: Le Livre de la Jungle: à la recherche de Mowgli
description:
Character from the children novel Mowgli.

Mowgli, Shanti and Ranjan is threatened by Shere Khan (the man-eating tiger) and hide themselves in the ruins deep in the jungle.

Players are Bagheera, Baloo and baby elephant and they search for them rolling the dice and following the footprints of Mowgli. Who first discovered the hiding place wins the game.

At start each player in turn places 7 game pieces of his color on the game board. Then, the game continues by sliding each turn a gamepiece in a free adjacent position. 
The first who close a square with four of his game pieces wins.

In Six-Word Memoirs, teams use SMITH Magazine's famous storytelling form to guess the names of famous people, places, and pop culture icons. Teams use imagination and quick word-writing wit to create and identify six-word descriptions on more than 1000 different topics.

Can you describe George Washington, Lindsay Lohan or the City of San Francisco in exactly six words? You will need to if you want to win Six-Word Memoirs by University Games. The game invites teams of players to battle for excellence in defining and deciphering famous topics of people and places in exactly six words. For example, do you know who, “Julie’s mom, wife of Tricky Dicky” would be? If you guessed Pat Nixon, then you’ve got it.

Six-Word Memoirs is the creation of Larry Smith’s online storytelling community, SMITH Magazine. Smith first introduced the concept of describing a person in just six words on his web community and in one of the first Twitter feeds in 2006. He called the form the “Six-Word Memoir” and this simple way to define a life went on to become New York Times bestselling book, Not Quite What I Was Planning in 2008. Now the wildly popular concept jumps to life in a competitive team-based game.

description:
After dealing out all cards to each player equally, Players will play one card each round, starting with the first player, and rotating to the right.  Cards are played by alternating face-up and face-down: the first card each round is played face down, the next card is played face up, and the third card is played face down, and so on, until each player has played one card.  Starting again with the first player, everyone will take one card of their choice, choosing either a face-up card, or pressing their luck by choosing a face-down card.

The cards represent different fruits, with varying number values.  Players are trying to get the highest values into their stack.  There are also three special cards:
   Super-fruit: this card will double the value of a single fruit card,
   Caterpillar: the hungry caterpillar will reduce the value of one or more cards by half,
   Pistachio: the delicious nut will lure a caterpillar away, canceling out one caterpillar card.

After all cards are exhausted, scoring begins.  Highest score wins.

boardgame: Arm Chair Quarterback: A Game of Skill
description:
This game was manufactured by Novelty Manufacturing Co. out of Richmond, CA in 1955. Arm chair quarterback is a game that consists of a game board, first down/yardage marker, 11 red defensive pawns, 1 tan offensive pawn, and a huddleboard with two metal stands.

The object of the gameplay: The huddleboard is placed in the middle of the board allowing the offensive and defensive teams to call their play.  The offense has 9 spots/plays to place their pawn.  The defense has 11 pawns with 16 spots to try and stop the play of the offense.  When the huddleboard is removed each team can see the result of the play. At each spot on the board a result of the play is posted as whether its a gain, loss, turnover, TD, or incomplete. After each play, you record the information on the yardage/down marker. Like football, the offensive player must make 10 yards in 4 downs to continue or the the board is turned around to switch positions.  The game can be played for 30 or 60 minutes depending on your preference. Note: All plays are based on national averages. There is no advantage to either team. It all depends on how good you are at playing QUARTERBACK!

Expansion for Popular Front: Escalation expands the base game by adding 54 cards and a new  power for each faction, encouraging players to develop unique strategies for each of the six factions. It also introduces new retreat and naval combat rules that increase the strategic depth of the combat system. The stakes of each turn's political resolution have also been increased, as the next turn's turn order is based on this resolution.

"Fall of the Titans" is a 2-player asymmetrical card game, based on the video game "Shadow of the Colossus." In "Fall," one player takes on the role of Ronin, a human assigned with the task of defeating the great Titan...which is played by the other player.

Each player has a different process and actions that they perform. Essentially, the Titan player sets up a defense, the Ronin player determines a course of action against those defenses, results are determined, and the player then moves. But careful consideration must be taken into account with regards to planning, bluffing, and resources available in hand.

The game uses a standard deck of cards, a pawn, and some coins or some other markers, in addition to the Titan board.
