description:
This game was invented by D. J. Springgay who is a well-known chess player, so the game is worthy of attention. Zug is an abstract game for two players with simple rules and fairly obvious affinities to several well-known games and has been well tested in practice. A provisional patent application for the game was filed in October, 1971.

The game is played on a square board composed of 17 squares by 17 alternately coloured light and dark, the corner squares being dark. Two lines marked on the board are called advance lines (between the 4th and 5th rank and the 13th and 14th rank depending on the end you are starting).
These are of two contrasting colours but otherwise identical. Each player has twelve. The player of the dark pieces places nine men on rank 1 and the remaining three on any squares on rank two. The player of the light pieces does the same on ranks 17 and 16 respectively.

The players are referred to as Black (the player of the dark pieces) and White, regardless of the colours of the men.

The object of the game is to be the first  to move all one’s pieces to the opposite end of the board such that the 1st (17th) rank is occupied by nine pieces with the remaining three pieces on the 2nd (16th) rank.

A piece may jump over one or more other pieces of either colour provided that each jump is over an adjacent piece to a vacant square and in a forward direction only.

Neither player may play a piece to the end rank unless all his pieces are forward of his advance line. When a player has all his pieces forward of his opponent’s advance line, he is permitted to move pieces backwards if he wishes.

Art Attack is a tile collecting, market speculation game. Players work to gather tiles from specific artists in a collection and then exhibit their collections for profit when the artist is at their most popular. 
Art Attack uses a unique slide device, known as the "MUSEUM" to indicate the current value of a collection. Two paintings by the same artist in the "MUSEUM" mean big profits while a third painting of the same artist floods the market and collections go bust! This forces players to wait consecutive turns for their hard earned collections to hopefully hold value once more.

KOLS is a 2-player perfect information card game that simulates a conflict between two opposing fronts.

The game is split into 2 phases. In the first phase you will use cards from your hand to obtain cards from the middle, attempting to increase your military strength. In the second phase you will play cards to 5 different battlefields attempting to achieve key victories and win points.

KOLS uses a standard 52-card poker deck.

boardgame: Travel Pack: Road Signs and Spylo
description:
A game to play whilst travelling on a long car journey. It includes cards for two games but they are merely the same game with an emphasis on two aspects of the journey.

The first is called Road Signs and the cards feature illustrations of road signs at the time. The other is called Spylo and the cards feature landmarks or non-sign related subjects to look out for.

The winner is the one who spots the most.

Review by John Humphries in Games and Puzzles #30:

Crown Chess is an unfortunate choice of name for this interesting and original game, as it bears no resemblance to chess. Perhaps Crown Prince may be a more apt name.

The game is played on a large, circular, wooden board on which are printed five concentric rings which are radially divided into eight sections, giving forty spaces or panels in total. The outermost ring or band is gold in colour, the remainder being black or white, in a pattern such that each adjacent pair of sections are of a similar colour and each panel is surrounded on three sides by panels of like colour.

Four sets of playing pieces are supplied. Each set consists of a Prince, two Barons (elephants) and two Viscounts (horses). The pieces are well moulded from resin, each set being in a distinguishing pastel colour. In addition there is a Crown, resembling an up-turned vase. The rules are simple and easy to follow.

The Crown is worn by one of the Princes, who is thus elevated to King. The object of the game is to promote your Prince and then manouevre him to the gold (outer) band. All pieces may be moved in or out one panel, the Prince can be moved an unlimited number of panels in a clockwise or anti-clockwise direction, the Baron only clockwise and the Viscount only anti-clockwise. No diagonal moves are permitted and the only piece allowed to move onto a panel already occupied is the King, and this only to capture. The King is the only piece able to capture other pieces. The Crown is gained by moving a piece onto a panel of the same colour and adjacent to the King, who is then demoted and the new Prince is crowned.

Although not a difficult game to play, Crown Chess does require thought and planning. However, it is rather difficult to formulate strategy because the situation changes so rapidly with four players. The Hints on Play provided advice that the main objective should not be to take the Crown, which should only be done to prevent another player from winning, to prevent possible capture of your pieces or to win the game. As pieces cannot jump over one another, there is a certain amount of aimless movement. and blocks can occur. Princes may not be captured, thus there is no way of forcing them to move. One of the best winning strategies seems to be to move one's Prince onto a panel adjacent to a Gold, keep it protected and wait for other players to blunder, then take the Crown at the opportune moment and make the winning move.

The game does have many unique concepts and apart from the box is extremely well presented. It is also most decorative though probably beyond the means of most games players (this game cost 18 pounds at the time of release).

Funny domino-game with parts of different characters on the tiles. Players must get rid of their tiles first by placing them on the table that the parts - hairstyle, head, body, legs, feet - fits together correctly.

Player who gets rid of his tiles first is the winner.

The object of the game is to get rid of his cards first by placing these on the table and helping Leonardo to mix colors correctly.

Each player draws 4 tiles with brushes and 8 cards.
In his turn player may play a color-card if he can paint with that color. eg. Player has card with blue and yellow. He can play this card if he can place on the table tile with blue and yellow or tile with green (which is mixture of this 2)

Player who plays his last card is the winner.

The appearance in the sky of the Great Comet (Halley's) foretells much bloodshed; battles will ravage the land when three kings lay claim to the throne of England!

1066: The Year of the Comet is a block wargame covering the crucial campaigning months of 1066, the year Britain was invaded twice, first by the Vikings and later by the Normans!

The map covers all of England proper. The Viking player invades  the eastern shores, while the Normans invade from the south. Favorable winds for one invader means the other must stay in port. Once ashore, invaders are faced with the prospect of fighting pitched battles or a guerrilla style campaign, depending on the English player's response.

This is a strategic level treatment. All of the 1066 campaigning season is represented. As such, the battles of Stamford Bridge and Hastings will not necessarily occur. It is up to the players to determine when, where, and how they fight.

This is a low complexity, three player wargame playable in an hour or two. Contains rules, map, battle board, and 56 blocks in three colors. Rules include invasions, mustering Fyrdmen, pitched battles, skirmishes, routs, etc.

Salient features include: buckets o' dice combat resolution, a battle board, a relatively small number of blocks... and no cards.

This game is provided "as is." Although the designer has enjoyed many plays of 1066, the game has not been through a blind playtest. In a sense, this is the blind playtest.

Any problems with the game should be noted in the General forum, but the designer makes no guarantee that they will be addressed. Players should feel free to explore patches, and if they find something that works, please mention the fix in the appropriate thread.

boardgame: World War II: Barbarossa 1941 – Battle for the Danube Expansion
description:
World War II Wargame Expansion Set for Operation Barbarossa 1941, a tactical-level wargame with a unique simultaneous-order mechanic. This expansion includes scenarios for early-war action along the southern Russian front, and includes rules for Romanian and Soviet border guard units.  Includes 11 kits (units) from the Art of Tactic range, bases, board game, cards, dice, new scenarios, and rule book. Models in the game are typically 1/72 scale (infantry), 1/100 scale (most vehicles, except the trains and boats in this set), or 1/144 scale (aircraft).

Additional available units (not in this kit) now expand operations with the use of paratroopers and transport aircraft as well as new anti-aircraft weaponry and new light and medium tanks.  HQ operations now offer new objectives and more control over units.

Contents:
2 Romanian Infantry
1 German Headquarters
1 Soviet Headquarters
2 Soviet Border Guards
1 Soviet Armoured Train
2 Trains
2 Soviet Armoured Boats
3 double sided game boards
15 double sided terrain hexes
6 elevation hexes
11 unit cards
2 store cards
1 turn card 
10 dice
8 smoke markers
2 fire markers
2 water based markers
Game Rules
Scenario booklet that includes 1 tutorial and 5 additional scenarios (6 total)

Japanese collectible card game.
The card game images are captured from Japanese animation (anime).

Deck construction of the game has exactly 40 cards in a deck and can contain only 3 duplicated copies.

The game has 3 card types: Character cards, Event cards and Scene cards. Character cards are the main card type for every activities during the game. The Event cards are once use then discard and Scene cards are used as permanent. Event cards and Scene cards are support cards and only use for trickery during the game.

Each player starts with 25 life point. The player's life point whose become 0 is the loser. The player can reduce the opponent life point by unblocked attacking character card in play. The damage is counted by the character's rank. Almost of them are Rank1 that can deal 1 damage to opponent when it is unblocked.

The game flow is simple. Each player has draw a card at the start of his/her turn and then has 2 main phases and 1 attack phase (battle phase between 2 main phases).

boardgame: Battles of Westeros: House Baratheon Army Expansion
description:
Game description from the publisher:

A new primary House faction joins Battles of Westeros! For the first time, the seasoned forces of the crowned stag take to the field of battle to defend their claim to the Iron Throne. In the House Baratheon Army Expansion for Battles of Westeros, you can unleash the Baratheons' fury with eight mighty commanders and over 100 detailed troop miniatures. An epic battle awaits you on the stunning Blackwater map, and innovative rules for Alliance battles allow up to eight players to clash in the deadliest of conflicts.

Force the entirety of Westeros to bow to their true King and defend what is rightfully yours: the Iron Throne.

description:
User summary
A Japanese small press card game. In its base, a bluffing trick-taking game where certain suits have some special effects.
All cards (except the first in each trick) are played face down, and instead of playing a card, the current player can try to call the bluff of the previous player, forcing him to reveal the played card; if it was of the wrong suit, that player gets penalty.

Cards and effects are based on the Japanese animated TV series "When they Cry…"

Fantastic and addictive game with your family and friends. 2-4 players. Team mode for 4 players.
90 cards including 60 normal artifacts and 30 power artifacts. 10+ highly playable expanding rules.

Get most magic points to become the winner!

Brief rule:

There are 3 phases in a player’s turn: the beginning phase, the operation phase and the ending phase.
1) The beginning phase: The active player can draw 1 card from top of the library...
2) The operation phase: The active player can place 1 normal artifact. He can also play at most 3 power artifacts...
3) The ending phase: If the active player doesn’t play any card, he can draw 2 cards. If he has more than 6 cards in hand, he should choose to discard extra cards.

https://www.thegamecrafter.com/games/magic-artifact

Super fast statistical tennis game, so quick that an entire Wimbledon can be completed in one evening. Each player has certain skills (serving, etc) and these are accessed or avoided depending on dice rolls.

My set is dated 1992 and has 1200 player ratings from 1973 to 1992.

A statistical sports game, focusing on the indoor soccer leagues of the MISL. Designed to play quickly so that a whole season can be played in one evening.

Each team is rated in various areas of skills. The statistics are in the form of paper sheets rather than cards. Dice are used to determine results of actions.

My set dates from 1990 and includes season stats for 1988 to 1990.

An unusual subject for a statistical sports game. Designed to be very fast so that an entire Olympic gymnastic competition could be played in one evening.

Over 250 gymnasts are rated for their abilities. These are based on performances at the real Olympic Games. Essentially, roll dice for each perticipant and that roll is checked against their rating to give them their score.

Statistics are provided in paper lists rather than individual cards.

Statistical sports game based Ice Hockey. Includes the 1990/1991 NHL Teams and the 1991/1992 Playoff teams.

Each team is rated in certain areas and dice rolls are checked for each team to see the results of a match. Designed to be fast so that a whole season can be played in an evening.

Statistics are printed in lists rather than on cards.

Unusual to find this sport in a statistical game. Designed around results for over 100 Men's and Women's Olympic teams.

Teams are rather basically rated but it only needs a dice roll or two to decide a result, meaning that a complete Olmpic Volleyball event can be replayed in an evening.

Statistical replay sports game based on the sport of 10 Pin Bowling. Over 100 professional bowlers are rated in various areas and matches are decide by a couple of dice rolls. This speed means that you can play an event featuring all the rated players and finish it in one evening.

Statistics are listed on paper rather than on cards.

Sorcery the Congregation is a deck building game with elements of direct conflict, player interaction, and chaotic 2-4 player action.  Each player will start with a 12 card deck that contains 9 Essence, 3 Lackeys and 20 life force.  The goal of the game is to acquire stronger cards from the center and conjure minions or cast spells to slow and injure your opponent.  It is a game of with a parody feel to classic games such as Magic the Gathering and other deck building games.

boardgame: Taipan: Merchants of the Far East
description:
The year is 1845 and the Far East is a new land of possibilities.  Foreign businessmen called Taipan, have settled here to capitalize on the growing trade routes through the oceans of the Far East.  From the southern tip of Nippon down to Makassar, the Taipan are making large profits.  But with any lucrative endeavor, criminals, smugglers, and pirates seek to take advantage of weaker prey, making these waters very dangerous.

You have just arrived in Hong Kong and taken out a loan to get your new Trading Company started.  You have a ship and a crew.  All that is left is to command your ship through the trade routes of the Far East and be the first to pay off your debt to win the game.

Playing the Game:
At the start of the game, each player chooses a special Captain Card with a special ability, they also receive a small amount of starting funds, and their Debt that must be paid off to win the game.

Players take turns moving their ship throughout the Eastern Seas trading goods between different ports, Hong Kong, Singapore, Makassar, Nagasaki, and more.  At the start of the game, players will only have a small amount of funds with witch to buy cargo.  Balancing they're purchasing of cargo, choosing the best place to sell the cargo, upgrading your ship, and using their Captain's special ability will give the players the best chance to win the game.

In addition to making the best choices, players will have many events that can change each players strategy.  Cities may have events that alter the value of goods at that port.  A fire in a city may make wood very valuable there for a short period of time.  A gold mine may create an over abundance that dives the price down making that city a prime place to get gold at a good price.  Also, Pirate Tokens placed on the game board hold a great risk of loss of cargo, a damaged ship, and upgrades that will have to be repaired before they can be used again.  But winning a battle against pirates can also grant the rewards or plundered cargo.

Who will be the first Merchant to successfully navigate these dangerous waters, pay off his debt, and win the game?

description:
Requirements: Paper and pencil. Match- sticks.

The board is a large hexagon,, divided as shown into 24 triangles. The size is such that matchsticks can be placed along the sides of the triangles. The large hexagon contains seven smaller 6-triangle hexagons, the centres of which are marked with large black dots.

One player is the 'mouse'. This is represented by a small coin which the player may place in any of the 24 triangles at the start of each game.

The other player is the 'cat' who attempts to trap the mouse in one triangle, by placing his matchsticks on the lines and gradually restricting the mouse's move-ments. His first move is to place a match anywhere on the board. The players then move alternately.

The mouse must move when it his turn. He may move to any triangle in the same small hexagon. That is to say he may move anywhere round the one dot. He may not cross over a matchstick.

Each player has two consecutive turns as the cat. He has 19 matchsticks which he may use on his two attempts to catch the mouse. He scores one point for each success. If he catches the mouse with 8 matchsticks in his first turn, he has 11 matchsticks for his second turn.

The first player to score 9 points is the winner.

The cat can always win with his first turn, but to get a lead over his opponent he must win and still leave himself enough matchsticks to win the second game as well. The best technique for the cat is usually to place his first two matches in opposite directions from the centre of the board as if to divide the board in half. He then ensures that the mouse is confined to one side of this division and in the smallest possible area.

The mouse, on the other hand, tries to keep to the largest possible open space, and to triangles where he has a choice of several dots round which he may move.

Post Captain is a solitaire PNP game of sailing, sea chases, and espionage during the Napoleonic Wars. You take on the role of a young captain who has just been given command of his first ship. The game's missions revolve around the the tangled web of political intrigue surrounding Napoleon's plan to invade England.

The game play focuses on navigation, 1 on 1 sea battles, and assaults by landing parties. The dramatic chases of naval literature are reproduced using an abstract system that represents the relative speed and position of each ship. Each turn the ship that has the highest maneuverability is considered to have the "wind gauge" and gets to move (closer or farther away) and/or attack. The captains navigational and crew assignments choices define his ships maneuverability each turn.

The primary game play mechanic revolves around the orders you give to your crew. You assign crew to different positions on the ship in anticipation of where you think the wind or the enemy will be. The game is divided into two segments. A "Mission" segment where you navigate on a large map attempting to reach objectives on time and a "Chase" segment where ships engage each other.

The goal of the system is to simplify naval combat to focus on dramatic storytelling rather than focusing on charts, modifiers,  counters, and complex rules. All conflict resolution is handled by drawing colored cubes from a cup.  This will tell if a test was successful and in combat it also tells what part of the ship is damaged. As a solitaire game the goal is to achieve enough mission objectives to earn renown in order to increase your rank and upgrade your crew.

Once again the checker board provides the basis for a game, only this time the size grid required is 10x10.

In 'Freeze' the movement of each playing piece is dependent upon its position on the board in relation to the other pieces. The object of the game is to prevent one's opponent from moving any of his pieces.

Each of the players has ten cube-shaped pieces, the two sets being of contrasting colours. During the game, a piece can be in one of three states: free, polarised or frozen. Thus the faces of the cubes are marked, so that the one facing upper most indicates its current state. These marks are:
Plain face — free
A line (either diagonally or orthogonally) — polarised
A large central dot — frozen.

A cube moves according to the state that it is in, as follows:
Free — any distance in any direction (as the Queen in chess)
Polarised — any distance along the line of polarisation
Frozen — no movement

To start the game, the pieces are set up in opposite corners, with the three corner pieces being in a free state and the remainder diagonally polarised towards the opponent's corner. Players choose who is to start and then move one of their pieces alternately. No piece may move over or onto any occupied square.

The state of pieces change as follows:
If two free, opposing pieces come into line, diagonally or orthogonally, without any other piece between them, then both become polarised and are turned to show a polarised face with the line in the correct direction.
If a piece becomes polarised along two different lines, it becomes frozen and is unable either to move or to polarise or freeze opposing pieces.

The action of freezing a piece may well release one or more pieces that were under its influence before it was frozen. When a polarised or frozen piece becomes separated from a piece which was polarising it, by the intervention of another piece, it is no longer under the influence of that piece and may move up to the next advantageous state; thus a frozen cube becomes polarised and a polarised one is freed. Any piece may change its state by the movement of any other piece on the board, whether friendly or enemy. Each such move can easily set off a chain reaction. It is the responsibility of each player to point out those opposing pieces which have become polarised or frozen as the result of a move. He should also note when his own pieces are freed.

The winner is the player who first freezes all of his opponents pieces.

A Spielbox expansion for Hawaii.
It adds a new God Kamapua'a (2 tiles) and 2 new islands as well as new fish/price chips and a 2 VP spear marker.

Kamapua'a: Frontside: One fruit per turn; At game end, 2 VP per permanent fish tile. Backside: Two fruit per turn; At game end, 4 VP per permanent fish tile.
New island: One fruit, foot, shell and VP each
New permanent fish tiles: Replace the 5 and 6 tiles in the bag. If you fish one of the permanent tiles you keep it and have 2 fish every round. Replace it with a 5 or 6 tile in the bag.
2 VP tile: Place in first pass place in last round.

Game from Games and Puzzles magazine, issue 38.

The game is for 2-6 players and is played on a board laid out as shown in the diagram. Each track or run is in two parts, the Base along the edge of the board, and the Approach. Each pair of Base and Approach runs would be of a different colour, but are lettered in the diagram for the purpose of identification. Each player would have a cube, of the same colour as his track. This cube is marked from 1-6 in a similar fashion to a dice, but with ants replacing the usual spots. A collection of ants' eggs with a container and a normal die complete the equipment. The eggs are quite simple to make from papier mache, plaster or a similar substance and painted white.

At the start of the game, the eggs are placed in the container, which in turn is placed in the Queen Ant's Nest in the middle of the board. The cubes are situated in their respective bases with the side showing the six ants uppermost. The die is thrown in turn, and the cubes moved the appropriate number of squares in the direction indicated, along the Base run and then up the Approach run.

The first player to reach his X on the Ant Nest surround, stops on that square and becomes the Defender of the Queen Ant's Nest. All other ants to reach their X's during that turn become Attackers, and attack the Defender. These attacks are resolved as follows: both players throw the die and the highest score wins, a draw counting as a win for the Defender. When the Defender wins, he remains where he is, and the Attacker returns to that square on his Approach run, having the same number as that thrown by the Defender. If the Attacker wins, he becomes the Defender, while the ousted Defender goes back to the square on his Base run having the same number as that thrown by the Attacker. Should a Defender be beaten by a throw of 6, he is returned to his Base and loses one ant from his group, and so recommences with the new ant group showing face up on the cube. A player with only one ant is knocked out of the game when beaten by an Attacker's throw of 6. On his turn, a Defender must attempt to circumnavigate the Nest, in accordance with throws of the die, and defend himself each time he lands on an opponent's X square containing a cube. When he returns to his own X, he stops (regardless of the throw) and is entitled to collect eggs as follows:

Size of    
Ant Group
6-5 = 3 eggs
4-3 = 2 eggs
2-1 = 1 egg

Play continues until all the eggs have been removed from the nest. The initial number of eggs is optional, but an ideal number is 30. The winner is the player having most eggs at the end of the game.

Although it may sound complicated, 'Antheap' is in fact very simple to play. It is well worth going to the trouble of making the board and the cubes, for it will certainly provide the younger members of the family with hours of fun.

Grab the bigger boats, sail on and pick the biggest treasures. It will do more to bring your loot safely and avoiding other buccaneers who will not let you do so easily.

Caribbean Sea is a dice game. On each turn, you roll as many dice as pirate your fine team have available. Then according to their value, you place your pirates on the board for hire new pirate, borrow boats, get cards with special effects, or place yours buccaneers on the best place on the trail to treasure. You'll also be able fomater mutinies to take the place of pirates enemy, attacking enemy ships and send them to the bottom and take them treasures, but also try to place your pirate in their crews to take advantage of their wealth.

In the end, the player who collected the most treasures will be proclaimed the master of the oceans ...

Players are divided into two camps playing with their pawns.
They put words on the board horizontally on the line closest to his line of defense one word in turn.

In his turn player may form a word on his line of defense by placing the letters on the board and by connecting the excisting words.
If he reaches the defense line of the opponent he earns the TAKENO (bonus token)
He can capture one or more words of the other side of the board by overlapping letters of the word.

When a player can not draw enough chips for the tokens required, the game is over. The winner is the player who has the most chips on the game board

You who were shut in in the Haunted house which a ghost wanders must escape from this place.
I sometimes cooperate and sometimes escape than anyone else earlier from this house.
Finally I wait for the confrontation with the god of death who is the guard of this house.

This game moves the piece with a card. The confrontation with the ghost depends on each room in the way. The player confronts it using an item tip on hand and must beat a ghost.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description: Both the Offensive coach and the Defensive Coach have 8 cards in their hands. The cards show the offensive plays; Fullback thru line, Halfback Off Tackle, Halfback Around end, Long Pass, Jump pass, Flat Pass etc. The Offensive player selects his play call while the defensive player puts the cards in order depending on which play he thinks the Offensive player will call. The order that the cards are placed in determines how successful the offensive play is. If the defense matches the offense then the defense has guessed the right play and the gain will be minimal. If the offensive play was Halfback around end and the defensive player had that card 4th in order the gain might be 7 yards. If the defense had the card 3rd in order the gain might be reduced to 4 yards. So the closer to the actual play call the defense is, the lower the gain potential. There is also a clock that the players move. Run plays take 30 secs while pass plays take 15 secs.

This is an educational card game.  The stated goal from the publisher is that it is designed to "increase general knowledge and to aid students in becoming 'culturally literate'.

The theme is American Folklore.  The game consists of a question tablet and 50 over-sized clue cards, each card representing a different American Folklore character (e.g. Daniel Boone, Paul Bunyan, Wyatt Earp and Rip Van Winkle).  Note that some characters are also historical.

On each card is listed 20 questions about that character.  Players take turns picking a number, which is tracked via the question tablet, and guessing based on the corresponding clue, until the character is guessed.  One point is scored per character guessed correctly.

The rules include variant team scoring rules and ruels that confer bonuses based on how many unused clues remain.  The game is also ideal for family game nights, road trips (even for adults!), etc.

In Legacy you will strive to become the new leader of the people of Ilyenashae – a continent upon which the people have risen up and overthrown their previous governments in fear for their safe future. You will campaign to become the leader of the continent’s territories and then create a new infrastructure by building developments in the territories, like citadels and universities.

In your turn you have two actions which can be any combination of: campaign for a territory by playing campaign cards or your hard won influence so the people side with you rather than any other player; progress, where you develop your territories so they produce more or help to defend them from other players; support, where you call on your followers and patrons to provide you with more influence and cards to play; and enlighten where you can play ideology cards like liberalism, utilitarianism and pioneering or even nihilism.

The combination of the ideologies you play shows what form your new government style takes they also give you advantages which will help you to win over the people. Territories and ideologies both give you points which represent the support of the peoples of Ilyenashae. The winner is the one who reaches the tipping point of territories held and ideologies played that convinces the whole continent to follow them.

Legacy gameplay is a mix of strategy, resource management and civilisation building driven by area control and card play.

- - -

"This was already our second chance. We do not dare hope for a third but we must try, or else accept the end of everything. In our desperation we have rid ourselves of all self-serving princes, casting them down the mountain. We now look to those men and women who rise from the flame and ash still flying banners of hope, still urging us on despite the pain and devastation. We now turn to these heroes for new leadership and new ideas on how we should live and rule ourselves for the betterment of all. One of them will forge a new society that will unite all the peoples of Ilyenashae to stand and face our trials together. Our future will be their... Legacy."

The combination of card and dice game includes 288 questions in six levels of difficulty around the German national football team.

Roll "one" and answer the simple question, where correct answer gives You one chip.
Roll "six" and answer difficult question, where correct
answer will be rewarded with six chips.

If the deck is exhausted the player with the most chips is the winner.

Contents:

     48 cards
     1 dice
     150 chips

Roll "six" and answer difficult question, where correct
answer will be rewarded with six chips.

Have you ever noticed how quickly you recognize songs while flipping through radio stations? Out of the thousands and thousands of songs you’ve heard in your life, you can single one out within a second or two from hearing just a few words - it’s pretty amazing! Music Mania is based on that simple principle and then taken to a whole new level of awesomeness: If I sing two words from a song, can you guess what the song is? I bet you can!

Music Mania is a party style board game with 4 different card categories and 12 different card styles, each of which offer up different challenges. While some of the cards require you to recognize a song in just a word or two, others have you trying to figure out a song when your teammate sings one line in reverse. The gameplay is quick and the laughter a-plenty.

This mini-expansion for Carcassonne comprises 10 tiles marked with a 10th anniversary icon. When placed, the tiles allow a player to place a follower as normal, or return a follower currently on the board back to their supply.

This expansion was originally packaged with Carcassonne: 10 Year Special Edition, but was also sold separately by some retailers.

Part of the Carcassonne series of games.

The Festival II has the same tiles as the 10th anniversary edition, but are marked with a 15th anniversary icon. The tiles have the new artwork.

boardgame: Ancient Egypt Go Fish for Art
description:
Go Fish for Great Art!

Discover Ancient Egypt as you play "Go Fish" and "Concentration" card games!  Learn about their gods, goddesses, the great pharaohs, how mummies were made, and more. There are dramatic images and facts on every playing card, and a fact-packed companion book included with the game.

A deck of 36 museum-quality playing cards and a guide to the artists. Ages 7 to adult.

From the publisher:
Play a new version of "Go Fish", honing your skills of strategy and memory, and discover Ancient Egypt as you play. Learn about their gods, goddesses, the great pharaohs, how mummies were made, and more. There are dramatic images and facts on every playing card, and a fact-packed companion book included with the game. The cards can also be used to play Concentration and various memory games.

Learning skills:
• Strategic thinking
• Concentration
• Classification
• Art appreciation
• Symbol translation

boardgame: Modern Artists Go Fish for Art
description:
Go Fish for Great Art!

A game that introduces children to modern art by playing "Go Fish" and "Concentration" card games.! Get to know 8 great artists and read about the genius and fun behind each work of art in the companion fact book.

A deck of 36 museum-quality playing cards and a guide to the artists. Ages 7 to adult.

Promo card for Ghost Stories. The Crazy Shaman is a 2-black tao ghost blocking the special power of all taoist when in play. It moves around to the next space available to his left at the begining of every turn. It gives one qi or one yang when defeated.

This card is sometimes distributed with the Village People expansion.

Card number 89.

Naval Bombardment should be described as a sophisticated version of the old favourite, Battleships.

The objective of each player is to destroy the opponent's fleet.

Each player provides himself with two grids of 20 x 20 squares, lettered A to T down the side and numbered 1 to 20 across the top. One of these is used for disposing the player's own navy (the 'home' chart), the other for recording salvos on his opponent's chart (the 'away' chart). In addition to these, at the foot of each chart there should be a diagram similar to that shown in Fig. 1 for the purpose of recording hits. This fulfills the dual aim of showing at a glance the combinations of salvo numbers required for locating ships and the amount of fire-power left to each player at any time. Both players should locate themselves in such a way that their charts cannot be seen by the opponent.

Each fleet is composed of thirteen ships. The following table shows the names, sizes and fire-power of each vessel
1 Flagship (FS) . . .8 squares. . .3 guns (3-3-2)
1 Battleship (BS) . . .7 squares . . .3 guns (3-2-2)
2 Heavy Cruisers (HC) . . .6 squares . . .2 guns each (3-3)
2 Light Cruisers (LC) . . .5 squares . . .2 guns each (3-2)
3 Destroyers (Dy) .. .4 squares . . .2 guns each (2-2)
4 Corvettes (Cv) . . .3 squares . . .1 gun each
The figures in parentheses above indicate the way in which guns are destroyed by successful bombardment. For instance, the flagship's first gun is knocked out of action after any three of its squares have been hit, the second gun after any six squares have been hit, and the third when it has been totally sunk. It will be seen that initially each player has a salvo of 24 shots.

In addition to his fleet, each player is provided with twelve mines. Four of these are high-explosive type, the rest ordinary, and their functions are explained later.

Ships may run vertically, horizontally or diagonally, but must of course be in a straight line. No ship or mine can be contiguous either laterally or diagonally with any other ship or mine — there must always be at least one separating square in each case.

When both players have placed all their ships and mines, a coin is tossed to decide who starts. The winner of the toss then proceeds to enumerate the 24 shots of his first salvo. As he calls out the squares both he and his opponent mark the appropriate charts — i.e. his 'away' and the opponent's 'home' respectively - putting a figure ‘1’ in each square as it is called. It is advisable, in order to avoid mistakes, for the opponent to repeat the squares after each one is called or at the end of the salvo.

The salvo having been completed and marked, the opponent then indicates what damage, if any, has been sustained. The information that he gives is restricted to the type of ships hit and the number of squares in each case — not the location. For instance, he might say: Two hits on the flagship, one on a destroyer and one on a corvette'. The only time that any further identification is given is when two or more ships of the same type have already sustained damage, as described later. It then becomes the turn of the second player to fire off his salvo, and the same procedure is observed, both players marking the squares called with the figure '1' again, but this time in the other charts. Naturally, before the second player starts his turn both will have checked to see whether he is still entitled to the full original twenty-four shots or whether any of his fire-power has been reduced. Play continues in this way, the next set of salvos from each player being record¬ed with the figure '2', and so on.

When a hit or hits are registered on one of two or more ships of the same type which have already suffered damage, the ship must be differentiated by salvo numbers. For example, player A has hit both of player B's Light Cruisers, one with salvo 2 and the other with salvos 3 and 4; if on salvo 5 he again hits one of these two ships, player B must say whether it is the '2' ship of the '3-4' ship. One way of arranging this differentiation neatly is to assign names or letters to those ships which have more than one in their class, e.g. Destroyers A, B and C, Heavy Cruisers A and B, etc.

The only square to be identified immediately is one containing a mine. When a player's shot lands on one of these his salvo comes to an abrupt end and he loses the rest of his shots in that salvo. In the case of a high-explosive mine he not only loses the remainder of the current salvo but the whole of the next one as well.

Fig. 1 shows a typical layout at the start of a game, while Fig. 2 shows the same chart but with all the "used" squares filled in — i.e. the squares actually occupied by vessels and mines plus all the surrounding squares which are unusable through the application of the ship placement rules. This is done to illustrate the importance of correct location of opponents' destroyed ships; note that although the occupied squares number only 73, there is a total of 323 'used' squares plus a further 8 in which no ship could possibly be, (designated in the diagram with an 'x').

It is noticeable when playing with beginners that they tend to pepper an identified target area instead of limiting the follow-up shots to a few well-placed attempts. This blitzkrieg approach may well knock out a gun somewhat more quickly than the more patient player, but it has two distinct drawbacks: (a) it will waste most of the salvo doing so instead of exploring other areas and (b) more seriously, the player will probably end up not knowing where the destroyed ship is and consequently not knowing which of his opponent's squares are unusable, which, especially towards the end of the game, can be a strong disadvantage when salvos are small and every wasted shot a severe liability.

MD is a promotional game by the German paper factory of the same name. It shows the production steps of high quality paper.
The game essentially uses a roll and move mechanism. Additionally, there are certain fields on which the players can pick up "forward" and "backward" cards. These can be used to either cancel bad event field effects, to double a die roll, or to set back opponents. The goal of the game is to get to the final storage fields for the completed product.

Uncle Scam has its roots in Monopoly but adds to it the effects of Politics and Bribing.  The game is won when the first player goes broke and then the money is counted and the winner is declared the player with the most money.
There are Capitol Investments, Rents, Prison, and Bribery but no mortgaging, borrowing, or selling between players allowed.

The game is designed and published by William T. Harvey of Carson City, Nevada, USA.

description:
Goal of this game is to teach children counting.

The game comes with 60 domino-like tiles showing numbers, numbers written in words and objects (to be counted) on both sides.

There are several game modes:
- play domino
- sort tiles from 1 to 20
- build telephone numbers  
- calculate differences
- build a tower with all tiles showing the same numbers or number of objects
- run a domino race

boardgame: Modern Naval Battles: Global Warfare – Campaign Expansion
description:
Build a Modern War Fleet!
Carry-Out Vital Missions!
Lead your Nation to Victory on the High Seas!

The Campaign Expansion puts you in command of a nation fleet in a series of linked battles. Each battle has its own parameters and victory conditions. Between each battle, you’ll have the chance to reinforce, repair, and rearm your fleet for the next battle. Master your tactical and strategic options and lead your fleet to victory!

Each mission lasts 3 to 6 game turns and a complete campaign consists of 2 to 8 missions.

This expansion adds a total of 112 Ship, Mission, Strategy, and Target cards to your Modern Naval Battles - Global Warfare game!

More Ship Cards!
More ships for: USA, USSR, UK, France, China, Argentina, Taiwan, Norway, Japan, Germany, Italy, and Canada! Plus, the new navy for India!

Mission Cards!
Each Mission card modifies the upcoming battle for each side and sets the battle’s victory conditions.

Strategy Cards!
You can purchase these cards to expand your strategic and tactical options.

Target Cards!
Some Mission cards utilize Target cards as part of their victory objective. You will be responsible for either destroying or defending these key objectives.

Counters!
Damage counters to record the hits inflicted on ships. The game also includes rules and counters to track the expenditure of missiles when ships perform huge missile volleys.

You must have Modern Naval Battles - Global Warfare to use this expansion.

Components
112 Full Color Cards
1 Full Color Counter Sheet

From the box: Two games in one!  Our specially designed double-sided board offers two favorite games - Steeplechase on one side and Ladders & Slides on the other.

Steeplechase is a game where the players roll two dice and move their markers (horses) the number of spaces shown on the dice.  There are special squares that either help you or hinder your progress.  The first "horse" to reach the finish line wins the game.

Ladders & Slides is basically the same as Chutes & Ladders, Snakes & Ladders, Knights & Dragons, etc.  Roll one die and move forward the number shown.  If you land on a square with the botton of a ladder, you automatically move to the square at the top of the ladder.  If you land on a square with the top of a slide, your mover is sent to the square at the bottom of the slide.  The first player to reach square 100 by an exact number wins the game.

This amusing little two-player game can be enjoyed by young and old alike. All that is required is a 7 x 7 squared grid, four playing pieces per player, and a die.

The pieces start in the positions as shown in the diagram (squares 0 and X) and the object is to manoeuvre the pieces so as to occupy two opposite centre squares (A-A or B-B in diagram).

Players move in turn according to the roll of the die as follows:
1 or 4 = 1 square,
2 or 5 = 2 squares
3 or 6 = 3 squares

Movement is only allowed in a vertical or horizontal direction. It is not permissible to occupy the centre square, nor is one able to jump over another piece. Also only one piece can occupy any one square and it is obligatory to move the full number of squares if possible. The highlight of the game occurs when a piece lands upon a square occupied by an enemy piece. The owner of the moving piece must shout in a clear, loud voice the word "Clobber". Having complied with that rule, he may remove the enemy piece and replace it anywhere on the board that he wishes, preferably as far from its target as possible.

This quick, simple game is well worth a try, it can become quite compulsive.

System is a Player Versus Player card game on which Player's take on the role of hackers and try to take control over their Opponent's System Cards via damaging them by battling on the plains of cyberspace with the use of Virus cards while defending from their opponent's attacks with their own anti-Virus cards.

A Player starts with a Memory value of 0. During the start of a Player's turn they may play (upload) Anti-Virus Cards that increase a Player's Memory value while on the field and or Virus cards that decrease a Player's Memory value while active on the field. If anytime during the game, a player's Memory value is below 0, then they must send their active Virus cards from their field to the discard pile until their Memory value is restored to at least 0, if not higher.

In addition to Anti-Virus and Virus Cards, Players may also use "Input" costs which have a Memory Cost and can be activated at anytime except the Upload Phase by returning Anti-Virus cards that have an equal or higher memory value than the costs of the input card back to the owner's hand.

To reduce your opponent's System Points, you declare which Virus Cards you choose to attack with and then the defending player may block with an Anti-Virus card (An Anti-Virus card may block any number of Virus Cards but if it's Memory Value is equal to or less than the total sum of the Virus Card's Memory Value, it is destroyed)

If a Virus card attacks succesfully, you inflict 1 Critical Damage to your Opponent's System (ie you reduce their System Points by 1.) When an Opponent's System is reduced to 0, you win the game.

Start-Up
Both Players draw 5 cards. Player who goes first may not attack.

Phases

    Draw – Turn Player draws 1 card
    Upload – Turn Player uploads Anti-Virus or Virus Cards. No Input Cards may be activated.
    System – Turn Player selects which Virus cards to attack with.
    End – Some effects may happen here.


Zones

    Deck - Directory
    Discard – Recycle Bin
    Field - Desktop
    Hand - Hand
    Removed From Play – Archive


Other Terminology

    Card Collective (on table) - Program
    Card Collective (off table) - File
    Counter - Cancel
    Destroy - Terminate
    Discard - Delete
    Effect - Process
    Player - Host
    Return - Buffer

User Description:

Star Confrontations utilizes paper miniatures in a science fiction wargame between armies of deadly warriors. Create an army by selecting which squads and vehicles your warriors will use. Every miniature possesses its own abilities and attributes so your army will always be unique.

This is the core rulebook for Star Confrontations.  The rulebook includes two armies: Federation Marines and Orc Raiders.

From the Introduction:

The Matrix Legion army book requires the Star Confrontation rules book in order to play. The Matrix Legion is a robot army that uses self repair to keep their army on the move no matter what damage their forces receive. Matrix robots use advanced stealth and teleportation technologies to dominate the battlefield.

From the Introduction:

The Coalition Forces army book requires the Star Confrontation rules bookin order to play. This army book introduces the the Coalition Forces, which is a mass tropp army supported by APCs and heavy tanks.

From the Introduction:

The Star Elf Guardian army book requires the Star Confrontation rules book in order to play. Star Elves are master snipers that use crossfire to tear enemy forces apart. Star Elf worlds have limited population so drones make up most of their military forces. These fearless robot warriors are tough as nails and each provides a special ability to their squad, making Drone Squads one of the most versatile and durable squads in the game.

boardgame: Epic Spell Wars of the Battle Wizards: Duel at Mt. Skullzfyre – Haggatha the Heffer's Crushazorian Godstorm Promo Cards
description:
Add some primal action to your copy of Epic Spell Wars of the Battle Wizards: Duel at Mt. Skullzfyre with Haggatha the Heffer's Crushazorian Godstorm. This promo carries the same great flavor and dark comedy that the original game features, and it adds plenty of new spell combinations for you to unleash on all of your foes!

6-card promo pack that includes 2 copies of these promo cards are available with purchase of the game at events

Formerly available at the Cryptozoic Entertainment e-store. Quote from a representative follows:

"Thank you for contacting us. We no longer have any stock of the Haggatha promo cards, so they do not come with the game anymore. Your best bet is to look for a reputable online re seller. If you have any further questions, please let us know.

Thank you,

-Cryptozoic Team"
family: Game: Epic Spell Wars of the Battle Wizards

description:
You won't ever find these Food Fight promos in a kid's meal! The sumptuous Cheeky Royale, Aunt Butterface, and Humping Dumpty can be added to your regular Food Fight game, allowing you to spice up gameplay with these tantalizing instants! Oh, and as an added bonus: they are a hilarious departure from the cleaner comedy you may find in your regular copy of the game.

The 3-card Food Fight promo pack can be acquired by purchasing the game from Cryptozoic Entertainment at events or through the Cryptozoic Entertainment e-store.

As your senior year is about to begin you promised yourself: It’s going to be legendary!
From Skipping School to Hack Grades to Road trips, you are ready to do whatever it takes to have a blast. Even if it involves subtle manipulation, a bit of betrayal and destroying a few hearts along the way.
And if things don’t turn out your way, gather your gang and Start a Teenage Riot! 
This is your last shot to go down on history.

The goal of the game is to score the highest number of victory points. Players score victory points by:
- Successfully performing events: 
- Accomplishing the Plan
- Beating the Snitch Brigade
If the Snitch Brigade defeats Bullies, players responsible for bulling loose victory points. So watch out!

Special Victory Condition
If a player manages to start a teenage riot he wins the game even without having most victory points.

Inspired by cult teen comedies “Endless Days" is a board game for 2 to 4 players, aged 12 and up that can be played in 25 minutes.

This is a Subbuteo replica using the HW miniatures of the 70s.

The box contains everything you need to play the good old Subbuteo, two teams with "moulded-type" miniatures (blue team and red team), two goals, two white balls and a cotton made soccer pitch.

As the Subbuteo, the rules here are alla about the flicking with your fingers of miniatures depicting actual football players. Any miniature can touch the ball no more than 3 times, after that you have to use another miniature. If another your miniature touches the ball, the previous miniature regains the maximum touches avalaibility. If you miss the ball, or this touches any of the other side's players, the ball possession passes to the adversary. Other rules include details to address situations like penalties, off-sides, free-kicks and such. This is after all a total Subbuteo-clone even in the popular ruleset that, from the 70s, has seen little or no change at all.

Anyone not happy with the new Subbuteo 2012 edition can still buy this one then!

The Egyptians were famous for hiding intricate mathematical expressions in their buildings, such as π, for example. In this game, each player is an explorer in Egypt, and must reveal the secrets of a mathematical pyramid.

Goal of the game:
Get to the top of the pyramid, revealing its secrets, and obtaining the most precious stones (treasures).

Playing the game:
In your turn, you need flip a card number ( pyramid's stone) and do mathematical operations that have this number as a result. You should do this for each stone in 2 minutes, and will be the turn of another player. Who gets reaches the top of the pyramid first, earn gems.
Three rounds are played, and the player who have more gems wins.

Tactical level wargame based on the Japanese animated series "Armored Trooper Votoms". The game was developed over five different games published in Dual Magazine issues no. 6 to no. 10. Each game featured a different setting but used same basic rules with different map and units. Game no. 5, "Armored Trooper," serves as an omnibus edition and collects most of the rules from the previous four games.

Players each control one or more piloted Armored Trooper mecha units (AT for short).

Each turn, players choose a behavior, called an "Action Pattern" for each unit. An action pattern (such as Run, Walk, Fire (Weapon), Dive (Underwater), and Roller Dash (use roller skate-like wheels)) determines how a unit may move and how well it performs in combat for that turn. Players also plot their movement and how many shots they will fire from a ranged weapon, if any.

All movement takes place simultaneously and the players move their units on a hex map.

After movement, ranged and close combat (called "Battling") are resolved. All action is simultaneous, so results of damage do not take effect until the end of the turn.

In the basic game, each player use one Armored Trooper and battle to destroy the opponent. A skilled player can handle more Armored Troopers for large-scale battles. A few scenarios are included, but players are expected to design their own combat situations.

Game no. 3 "Outer Space Battle" is very different from the other games. It uses vector-based movement in outer space and only a small number of AT units are able to be used outside of the atmosphere.

New Deal is a solitaire card game in which you are trying to help the US recover from the great depression. You play as Franklin D. Roosevelt and will implement economic policies and deal with various crises that arise.

There are 8 objectives in the game, each representing an area of the US economy, such as Banking, Agriculture, and Public Works. The game is played over 15 turns. Each turn consists of 5 phases in which you 1) draw 9 cards 2) Play one crisis card (if you have one) 3) Play a legislative card 4) Play a set of cards to enhance one of the objectives 5) Discard between 1 and 5 cards. At the end of the game, you determine your final score by multiplying your lowest objective score with your highest objective score.

French word game's Octoverso Junior version. 8 double-sided letters are placed on a rotative rail. Each player must form a word using the letters in front of him. The word has to begin with at least the last 2 letters or end with at least the first 2 letters of the "word" currently on the rail. Since the rail rotates, that effectively gives 4 possibilities.

The first player to get rid of all his letters win.

The letters look more appropriate for young audiences and are marked with colors.

At start each player receives seven cards.
There are 2 letters on the cards, one black and another purple.

The goal is to find a word during 3 minutes with 3 or 5 black letters but using a purple letter in the middle of your word
to socre 2 points.

At the end of the lap, put your cards face down on the table except the card with used purple letter what You put on top of the deck face-up.

The cards are given to neighbors who have 90 seconds to find in the word of his opponent's word of 3 or 5 letters.
Each player announces the words they found.
If you can not find a word or Your word is smaller than word of Your opponent, his score is doubled.

With several rounds the player with the highest score wins.

The game consists of 30 theme cards for the Tomot Original.

Each player receives seven cards of the original game and a theme card is drawn face-up on the table.
There are several themes like: Geography, Sports, Games etc.

In his turn player must give a word beginning with a letter from his card and related to the face-up theme-card. An answer can be given only once throughout that round.

The round ends when one player has given answer for each card from his hand. Then he scores a point.

A player must take an additional card, if He does not have words or he is too slow.

After several rounds the player with the most points wins.

boardgame: Ghost Stories: White Moon – The Cursed Children
description:
"Les Enfants Maudits" or "the cursed children"
First scenario for the Ghost Stories expansion White Moon.
It proposes a specific setting and new rules to play the White Moon expansion.
It includes the set of rules in French/English and a new cemetery board.

The board and rules can be found on the Repos website. (see weblinks)

boardgame: Ghost Stories: White Moon – Green Roots
description:
Green Roots is a downloadable scenario for the White Moon expansion to Ghost Stories, giving new powers to green ghosts.
It includes a set of rules in French/English and a new cemetery board .

The board and rules can be found on the Repos website. (see weblinks)

boardgame: Sherlock Holmes Détective Conseil: La rançon du diable

description:
Copied from a review by John le Neve Johnson in Games and Puzzles #12:

The equipment for the game comprises a plastic bowl in the lid of the box and another one fixed in the bottom half; a gross of small cards in six colours with letters on both front and back; and a six-sided top with a differently-coloured spot on each side.

The cards are shuffled and dropped in the bowl in the lid of the box. The top is spun in the other bowl and the idea is to grab the cards one at a time and build up words while the top is still spinning. Letters that are unused must be returned before another one is taken. The grabbing must stop immediately the top falls over.

One point is scored for every card used in a word which is the same colour as the spot on the top side of the dice when it falls. If the word starts with a white card the score is doubled.

Each round consists of two spins of the top; then the words are broken up and the cards returned to the bowl for the start of a new round.

The winner is either the first player to score a set number of points or the high¬est number of points in a set time.

Adds 30 Picture-cards for the Tomot Original
Each card has a picture and word describing that picture with 5 letters but only the middle letter is shown.

The game is played only with black letters from the original game.
At the beginning of the round, each player receives 5 ​​picture cards and the top card from the original deck is drawn face-up.

Players must be the quickest to find the word from his pictures
that contains this letter and to score point. The picture card is discarded.
If a player makes a mistake he draws an additional card.
The round ends when a player has no more cards in hand.
Player with the most points is the winner.

Adds 30 blank cards to Tomot Original.

Players receives 7 original cards with letters and 3 blank cards.
First player forms a word to start telling a story.
Eg. plays cards "R","A" and "T" and says: Once upon a time (a) RAT..."
In next turns player forms a word and continue a stroy.
Player must always has 7 orignal Tomot cards in hand and 3 blank cards what replaces any letter.

Winner of the round is the player who is the last in game.
Player is eliminated if he:

     He has no word to continue the story,
     He makes a mistake in grammar, spelling etc.
     He doesn't remember the story to continue

DOUBLE DEALER: Auction Americana With A Sly Twist!

Object of the Game:  The object of “Double Dealer” is to buy antiques listed on your client cards at auction and resell at a profit.  It is important that players try to buy the combination of antiques listed on their client cards, since they are worth more together than when sold singly.  The winner is the player who has the most money after cashing in his antiques at the end of the game.

One of the "big box" Hasbro family fun games from the mid 70’s, which also included The Great Estate, Power Broker, and High Stakes.  On the cover of each of these huge games is a similar picture with similar people, and a game quote from Jerry Lewis.  For Double Dealer the quote is:  “You’re hot…buy low, sell high…wheel and deal, then double deal.  That’s the name of the game!” – Jerry Lewis

This is a published version of the familiar party game. One player reads an adverb off a card and then is told by a second player to perform an action in the manner of that adverb. The second player then tries to guess the adverb. Play alternates and continues until, the publisher suggests, ten words have been read.

Following on from his father's great wargaming classic THE WAR GAME, Charles Grant Jr. has collated years of rule development, alteration and additions to make this, the updated and complete set of rules. 56 pages, A4 size, colour photographs throughout, card covers, including a separate laminated set of play sheets. The rules are designed for the XVIII century, at the battalion level. 
Infantry is based in line (2,3 or 4 ranks deep), column of attack (6 mini wide) and in column of march (3 figures wide)
Cavalry goes in 2 mini wide excdept for column of march (with 3)
Each turn represent round 15 minutes and it has the following sequence:
1. Write new orders
2. Declare charges
3. Normal moves
4. Artillery fire
5. Musketry fire
6. Melee
7. Morale Checks
8. Compulsory Moves
Units move from 4" to 18" depending of type and terrain and the range of weapons goes from 0" to 18" for infantry and 0" to 60" to artillery (with special ranges for canister and howitzers)
Combats are resolved with D6, either for melee or fire.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups
family: History: War of the Austrian Succession

description: This is a party game in which a player and his opponent debate an issue that is determined by a card draw. The debate is judged by the other players according to a criterion that a spinner decides, eg, funniest, most unique, worst, etc. The judges award chips to the players. Play continues through each bracket, with the two players who have collected the most chips squaring off in the finals.

This two-person game is played on a six-by-six checkerboard with squares of six different colors. Each player has a marker and three blockers. Player one moves his marker and blockers, attempting to corner player two's marker. A die determines the distance a piece may move and the color of the square to which it may move. If player two's marker is not cornered after six turns, the roles are switched, and player two tries to corner player one's marker.

The combination of card and dice game includes 288 questions in six levels of difficulty around the German national football team.

Roll "one" and answer the simple question, where correct answer gives You one chip.
Roll "six" and answer difficult question, where correct
answer will be rewarded with six chips.

If the deck is exhausted the player with the most chips is the winner.

Roll "one" and answer the simple question, where correct answer gives You one chip.
Roll "six" and answer difficult question, where correct
answer will be rewarded with six chips.

If the deck is exhausted the player with the most chips is the winner.

Fibonacci 21 is a deck of 89 cards numbered 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, or 21. Several rummy-style games and a solitaire game can be played with the deck. The idea in each game is to form sets of cards based on the Fibonacci sequence and then to meld those cards onto the table. The winner is the first player to go out after acquiring the correct number of sets.

A Strategic Game of Aerial Dragon Combat
Blood poured from a ragged wound in his neck and his wings were frozen and sluggish from Syrinaxx’s icy breath, but Ulmasaur drove himself onward.  The frost dragon’s wingbeats thrummed behind him, relentlessly matching each maneuver as they banked around a mountain. But Syrinaxx had grown overconfident and Ulmasaur climbed, twisted and flipped in an unexpected wing over to arrive directly behind the white dragon and a vicious bite delivers a final mortal wound.

A vile smirk splits his maw as the white dragon plummets into the mist below, but victory is short lived, as the full bulk of the gigantic Lavendros collides mid-air with the black scaled Ulmasaur as they lock claws and tumble earthward - wings, gore and fangs in a deadly embrace.  The wind screams as they bite and rend one another, but as the ground rushes at them there is no hope for Ulmasaur, who takes the full brunt of the crash beneath the bulk of Lavendros.  The big dragon sniffs the corpse below him once before bellowing a victorious roar and taking wing, he is battered and broken but the skies are his and his alone.

Overview of Play
In SkyWar Dragon, Each player controls a mighty dragon embroiled in a battle for supremacy in the skies. Players will play Flight Cards to maneuver on the board and gain position over their opponents so that they may then play damaging Attack Cards.  Flight Cards modify your Evasion and Combat, so careful section of the right card at the right time is essential to victory.

SingStar: Das Brettspiel is an adaptation of the SingStar video game series, in which players sing existing songs and are scored based on how well they do.

Experience real SingStar fun, without electronics! Players simply sing the songs that come to mind. The only requirement: the song must contain predetermined words. No problem with "love", but what about "green" or "train"? Whether duet or duel, whether pop or punk: the path from starlet to SingStar is guaranteed to be a lot of fun!

Contents: 76 song cards, 24 jury cards, 4 overview cards, 1 variable game board, 4 play figures with stands, 1 play microphones, 1 hourglass

Adds 52 cards with poker chips to the Tomot Original.

Each player gers 250 credit and 4 Original cards with letters and one letter is drawn face-up on the table, which will remain in this position throughout the race.

The goal is the same as traditional poker: having the best combination: pair, 3/4/5 of a kind, word with 3,4 or 5 letters,
3 or 4 letters in alphapetical order or TOMOT/Super TOMOT
word with 3/5 letters with black letter in the middle.
Players bid with their credit.

At the end of the game the player with the most money wins.

Taken from the game's official web site:

"The object of the game is to be the first to complete three laps and win the ultimate prize on the podium and the glory of winning –“Michael J. Richardson’s Ultimate Driver 300!©”. The trick doing  this is to play “Race Point” cards in front of yourself face up to speed yourself along and get those laps locked away as fast as possible and to play “Event” cards and “Damage” cards on other players to slow them down and hinder their race so you can be the first to complete three laps, (300 points), and win the race."

A traditional target game. The target is a wall with five arches. The arches are worth points if you can roll one of your marbles into the opening. The scores 'on the doors' seems arbitrary; they are, from left to right, 7, 8, 5, 10, 3 .
Above each arch is an animal;
An elephant, a lion, a sea lion, a tiger, and an ape. The illustrations, that give the game some character, are by English illustrator, Kenneth Townsend (1931 - 1999).

The first player to reach a pre-agreed total, wins.

"Arabistan Fleet is a contemporary game of naval warfare taking place in Middle East, focusing on Persian Gulf and Iran.
Time Scale : 
- 8 hours per turn
- 80 km per hex.

The players maneuver war ships, submarines, aircrafts, amphibious war ships and cargos on a map covering Asia from Iran to Indonesia.

The game reuse rules from Victory Games Fleet series, but includes new weapons as missiles, ground lauched ballistic missiles, SAM, cruise missiles
There are also new rules regarding submarines, Aegis ships, anti-missile defence systems, long range air-to-air missile,

The game can be linked with Arab-Israel Fleet.

More Information:

The game includes 6 scénario, from very simple to more complex ones:

1/ Fateful Closing
2/ Gulf Incident
3/ Nuclear Strike
4/ Escort Fleet
5/ Storm over Karachi
6/ Revolt in Middle East"
